context.content:
September 14, 2022. Past 11 PM.

Just stepped out of the study. Wang Xiaomei is already asleep. The light in Xinyu’s room is still on; she’s probably reading.

During this afternoon’s meeting, Xiao Wang said something that’s been looping in my head: "Mr. Shen, you seem to have changed lately. You used to be so eager to prove yourself, but now you seem... wiser."

I didn't say much at the time, just gave a nod. But honestly, my heart skipped a beat.

He’s right. I *am* changing. Or rather, I’m trying to. Lately, I’ve been making a conscious effort to listen more and talk less in meetings, to ask questions instead of rushing to correct people, and to give my subordinates more space to express their ideas. It’s all intentional.

But here’s the problem—I’m aware that I’m doing it. I’m watching myself change. Doesn't that just make it a new form of performance?

When I heard the word "wiser," I admit that for a split second, my ego was satisfied. That feeling of being recognized still feels so damn good. Then I immediately got defensive—fuck, am I falling for it again? Am I actually changing the way I work, or have I just switched to a more "sophisticated" way of showing off?

This is so messed up.

I’ve always used different things to prove my worth. It used to be passion, networking, and performance. Now it’s "reflection," "change," and being "wiser." At its core, it’s still a performance. I’m still waiting for people to watch, to validate me, to say, "Mr. Shen, you’re really different now."

Is this really what I want?

Or is what I actually want... just to do meaningful work? Regardless of whether anyone sees it. Regardless of whether I get credit. To just do things in a way that I can live with. But now, I’m even monitoring *that* thought—am I falling into a new performance trap?

Dammit.

The most annoying part is that I’m aware of this now, but I still can’t change it. I’m still "striving" to change. This "striving" itself is a performance. I need to learn how to let go, but I don’t even know how to start.

Maybe real change is... stopping the observation of your own change?

I don’t know.

I have to go home for dinner tomorrow. Xinyu’s mock exam scores dropped again. Wang Xiaomei has been even quieter lately. Mom’s rehabilitation fees for the month are due again.

I’m tired.

But I have to keep going.

# WeChat Chat with Golf Buddy Lao Wang

**Lao Wang** 15:42
Hey man, up for a round this weekend? The weather looks great, I’ve got a few people together.

**Shen Linchuan** 16:08
Hmm... I’ve been feeling a bit drained lately. Might have to pass this weekend.

**Lao Wang** 16:09
Tired again? What’s going on with you lately? That’s the third time you’ve turned me down.

**Shen Linchuan** 16:15
Haha, it’s nothing. Just some annoying stuff at work. You know how it is with the company...

**Lao Wang** 16:16
What’s up? Problems at the office?

**Shen Linchuan** 16:22
No real problems, just... forget it, it’s hard to explain. I just feel like there’s no point to it anymore. I used to be driven, but now it feels like I’m just on repeat.

**Lao Wang** 16:23
That sounds pretty heavy. Is the higher-up putting pressure on you again?

**Shen Linchuan** 16:28
It’s not about pressure. It’s just... I’m thinking, maybe this is it for me. Look at me, I’m 42 this year and I’m still doing the same old things. Sometimes when I think about it in the middle of the night, I just feel exhausted.

**Lao Wang** 16:29
Don't overthink it, man. With your connections and ability, you could get a promotion and a raise anywhere else in a heartbeat.

**Shen Linchuan** 16:35
Heh, connections and ability... I don’t really believe in that stuff anymore. I’m just a smooth talker, that’s all. No real skills.

**Lao Wang** 16:36
Have you been drinking?

**Shen Linchuan** 16:40
No. I just haven't been sleeping well lately. Thinking too much.

**Lao Wang** 16:41
All the more reason to get out and move around. Let’s play this weekend—dinner’s on me, how about it?

**Shen Linchuan** 16:50
Thanks, Lao Wang. Let me think about it. I’m really not in the mood lately. You guys go ahead, don’t wait for me.

**Lao Wang** 16:51
Alright then, hit me up whenever you feel like playing. By the way, how’s Xinyu doing? How’s the Gaokao prep coming along?

**Shen Linchuan** 17:05
Her... her grades are still the same, just average. I don’t know how to help her. Sometimes I wonder if I’ve failed as a father, too.

**Lao Wang** 17:06
Don’t think like that. You can’t rush things with kids.

**Shen Linchuan** 17:12
Yeah. Anyway... you can’t rush anything, and you can’t change anything. I guess I just have to accept it all.

**Lao Wang** 17:13
You really seem off. How about we grab a drink sometime? Have a real talk?

**Shen Linchuan** 17:20
Forget it, there’s nothing to talk about. It’s all in the past. Go do your thing, don’t worry about me. I’m just a bit tired; I’ll be fine in a while.

**Lao Wang** 17:21
Alright. But man, don’t overthink things. It’s not good for your health.

**Shen Linchuan** 17:30
Yeah, I know. Thanks, Lao Wang.

# chat_private

**To: Wang Xiaomei**
**Time: 2022-09-14 21:47**

---

Xiaomei, are you asleep?

There’s something I want to talk to you about. Xiao Wang came to my office this afternoon and said something that really stuck in my throat. He said, "Mr. Shen, you seem to have changed lately. You used to be so eager to prove yourself."

I didn't argue; I just admitted it. But after I did, I spent the entire afternoon wondering—am I actually changing, or have I just found a new way to perform?

You know, he followed that up by saying I "seem wiser now." For a split second, hearing that felt deeply satisfying, just like it used to feel when people said "Mr. Shen is so well-connected" or "Mr. Shen knows how to get things done." But I immediately realized I was doing the exact same thing again—only this time, the "persona" I’m chasing is one of "depth" and "reflection."

I don't know if I’m truly changing, or if I’ve just moved my performance to a different stage.

I’ve been thinking about this for days. I thought about it while driving to the outskirts with Xinyu. I thought about it on the golf course. Even at dinner parties, when people were raising a glass to me, I was asking myself—is any of this real?

Can you tell me? From your perspective, have I really changed? Or have I just become better at self-reflection, while remaining, at my core, that same person who just wants to be seen and validated?

I need the truth from you. Not words of comfort.

---

I’m a little tired.

September 21, 2022, 7:15 AM

I didn’t sleep well last night, but for once, it wasn’t anxiety. It was excitement, mixed with a feeling I can’t quite put into words.

Xinyu showed me the photos she’s been taking lately. They aren’t "masterpieces" or anything—just shots of bookshelves in the library, light streaming through a window, and that stray cat that always hangs around downstairs. My first instinct was to post them on WeChat Moments with a caption like, "Look how talented my daughter is." But I stopped myself.

I just sat there, looking through them one by one.

I’d never noticed the details on the bookshelves she photographed—the play of light and shadow on the spines, the worn edges of pages that had been turned a thousand times. There was such a sense of stillness. When I asked her why she took that photo, she just said, "I just felt like that moment was really quiet."

Suddenly, something clicked.

The world she sees is truly different from the one I see. I’ve always wanted her to be more lively, more extroverted—more like me. But the truth is, I’ve never actually looked at the world through her eyes. I’ve been measuring her by my own standards instead of seeing what she is actually looking at.

Lying in bed last night, a thought came to me, and I pulled out my phone to jot it down: "My value isn't in what I can say, but in what I can hear, what I can accompany, and what I can see."

After I wrote that, I felt a strange sense of fulfillment. Not the "I achieved something" kind of satisfaction, but more like... I had finally let something go.

In the past, I was always trying to prove something. Proving that I had the connections, the ability, the accomplishments. Cracking jokes at dinners, socializing on the golf course, showing off on social media. It felt like stopping meant failing.

But looking at Xinyu’s photos last night, none of that crossed my mind. I was just looking, listening to her talk, and feeling what she was trying to express. In that moment, I wasn't "performing," and instead, I felt a genuine sense of peace.

It’s a feeling I haven’t had in a long time.

I don’t know how long this will last. Tomorrow there will still be social obligations and work matters. But at least now I know there’s another way to live. Not necessarily better or worse, just... different.

Maybe I should pick up a camera too, and really look for once. Not to take a "good" photo, but just to see.

# Phone Memo

**2022-12-18 8:30 AM**

---

Why wasn't I disappointed this time?

When I got the email yesterday, my first reaction was actually a bit of a daze. It wasn't a promotion; just a new project—integrating marketing strategies across several regions. By my old logic, this should have disappointed me. But I spent half an hour sitting in my office, reading the project description over and over.

Then I found myself thinking: how can I do things differently this time?

Not thinking about how to finish it quickly to climb the ladder. But really thinking—how can I help the people under me learn something? How can I make this project more solid?

When did this shift happen?

Maybe it started with that trip back to my hometown. Watching Mom slowly recover in the rehab center, watching Lin take care of her every day, I suddenly understood—not all achievements need to be on a resume. Some things are just more important.

Driving to the outskirts with Xinyu last week, she was looking out the window from the passenger seat, and we didn't say much. But those two hours felt more fulfilling than attending ten business dinners. Her grades have slipped a bit lately; I would have been anxious before, but now I think—being able to be there for her through this phase is a success in itself, isn't it?

Quality vs. Speed. It’s an old question, but now there’s a new answer.

I used to always be in a rush, always thinking about the next goal. Now, I want to slow down. To do this project well, and let the team actually learn something in the process. Not for a promotion, but just... because the work itself is worth it.

Xiaomei asked me what I thought last night. I told her I wanted to try a new way. She looked at me for a while and then said, "You seem different."

I don't know if it's for the better or what. But it feels different.

The exhaustion is still there, but it’s a different kind of exhaustion.

---

*To be continued? Or maybe I'll just leave it at that.*

Xiaomei, are you still awake?

I wanted to share some thoughts with you. This might seem a bit sudden, but I feel like I can’t wait any longer.

I went back to my hometown today to see Mom and Linlin. While we were in Mom's rehabilitation room, Linlin said something to me. She said that my involvement made her feel a different kind of pressure—it was no longer the pressure of carrying everything alone, but the pressure of us being in this together. My eyes welled up when I heard that. I realized that for all these years, I thought I was busy building networks, maintaining relationships, and trying to be a "successful" person, but I haven't truly been there for the people who matter most.

When we hugged, I thought of you.

You know, for years I’ve been looking for a sense of belonging at business dinners, on golf courses, and at all those social events. I thought that was where my value lied. But today I finally understood that true companionship and responsibility are what actually make me feel whole. This isn't to say I’m going to quit my job or change everything overnight, but I finally understand what is truly important.

I still owe you and Xinyu so much. All those dinners where I was physically there but mentally miles away, all those moments I replaced quality time with my phone, and the growing silence between us. I see it now, and I feel it too.

I want to change this. Not everything all at once, but starting now, with a real conversation.

Would you be open to that? It’s the weekend tomorrow—could we have a real talk? Not about our daughter’s grades or household chores, but just the two of us, talking about what’s on our minds, talking about *us*.

I know this sounds sudden, but I mean it.

Waiting for your reply.

Shen Lin, I’ve been thinking, and I really wanted to say thank you.

To be honest, I felt a bit of regret for breaking down like that in front of Mom today. I came back intending to just spend some quality time with her, but instead, I ended up dragging you into my mess. But you were just sitting there—you didn't turn away, and you didn't pretend not to notice. I felt that.

All these years, you’ve been there taking care of Mom while I’ve been running around busy over here; we haven't really had a chance to talk properly. I’ve always felt like I owe you, but I never knew how to put it into words. In that moment today, having you there... I don't know how to describe it, it’s just... I didn't feel so alone.

I know it hasn't been easy for you either. Everything with Mom and the house is resting on your shoulders. I’ll figure something out, and I’ll send extra to cover more of this month’s medical expenses.

I just wanted to say, thank you for being there today.

Just now in the meeting room, a colleague asked me what I thought about the recent departmental restructuring. I said something that even surprised myself.

"To be able to stay honest at work, to be there for your family at home, and to remain true to yourself—that is success."

I used to think this was just something people said to comfort themselves. Not anymore.

Lately, I’ve been wondering why I spent so many years trying to prove something. Promotions, performance, networking... it never seemed to be enough. Until one day, you realize that driving your daughter to the countryside and watching her read quietly in the car feels more grounding than any successful business dinner ever could.

Last month, when I went back to my hometown, I sat in my mother’s room while she slept peacefully. I just sat there, doing nothing, yet I felt an immense sense of fulfillment.

I’m not sure if it’s just part of getting to this age, but you slowly start to understand the meaning of "enough."

I no longer need the applause on social media. I just need to know that what I’m doing is real.

Xiaomei, I’ve stopped at a service area for a while.

Xinyu said some things to me in the car just now. I... I’m still trying to process it all.

She told me that I’ve been unhappy lately, and that she can feel me judging her grades. She said she’s exhausted—not because of her studies, but because of my anxiety.

I wanted to defend myself, but I didn't. Because she’s right.

I always thought I was hiding it well, but the truth is, I’ve been projecting all my pressure onto her. I’ve been using her grades to prove my own worth. It’s so selfish.

When she put her hand on my shoulder, I... I broke down.

I’m sitting in the lounge at the service area now, still feeling a bit dazed. I don’t know when I became this kind of father. When did I start defining myself by my daughter’s grades?

Do you feel the same way? Have I been too hard on her?

We need to have a real talk. Not about her grades, but about... how we support her. About how I can stop hurting her.

I’m lost. I don't know what to do anymore.

# MEMO

**Time**: 2:30 PM
**Subject**: Must-do list for tomorrow

---

The situation with Xiao Wang—I have to fix this.

I really shouldn't have spoken to him like that this afternoon. His report formatting was definitely off, but my tone... with so many people watching in the office, he went pale. That’s not like me. I’ve never been that kind of boss.

**To-do tomorrow:**
- Catch Xiao Wang for a private chat before work starts and give him a proper apology. Not a half-hearted "sorry about my attitude yesterday," but something sincere. He’s a good kid, and his recent performance has been decent.
- Look out for him during Friday’s team-building event. Let him know I’m not angry and I’m not holding a grudge. If possible, find an opportunity to praise his other recent work.
- Consider giving him a small bonus or some form of compensation—not as a bribe, but just... to make up for that afternoon.

---

Honestly, I don’t know what’s wrong with me.

The pressure has been getting to me lately. The situation with my mother, Xinyu’s grades, and... never mind. Regardless, I can’t take these emotions out on my subordinates. It’s not their fault.

I need to remember who I am. I’ve always been an easygoing person. I can't let things turn me into someone else.

---

**Misc:**
- Remember to pour Xiao Wang a few drinks during Friday’s dinner and keep the conversation light.
- See if there’s an opening to get him onto next month’s key project.

I can’t let this incident dampen his passion for the job.

[Image: Mom sitting by the window, sunlight streaming across her face. She’s looking at the newly framed photos on the wall with a faint smile on her lips.]

[Image: A photo wall featuring several black-and-white shots—mom doing physical therapy at the rehab center, sitting in the courtyard, and moments of her chatting with Nurse Li.]

Took some photos of Mom this afternoon. The weather was lovely today, and she was in high spirits. I got them framed and hung them up in her room.

Watching her look at those photos, it suddenly hit me—companionship itself is the best form of recovery. It’s not about the profound words I can’t seem to find; it’s just about being by her side and using a camera to capture these ordinary days.

Mom asked, "When will you be back?" I said, "Next month." She nodded and went back to looking at the photos.

Sometimes we don't need to do anything special. We just need to be there.

---

A rare moment of relaxation this weekend. 🏌️ The weather is perfect, and with the whole crew here, the laughter and conversation haven't stopped. This is what life is all about—good friends, bright sunshine, and something to look forward to.

As I hit middle age, I’ve come to truly understand the meaning of "cherish." Cherish the people around you, and cherish every moment you get to unwind. No matter how busy work gets, remember to stop and take in the view.

The family is waiting for me—my wife and daughter are both home. We’ll have dinner together tonight; simple company is the best kind. Sometimes we overthink things, but really, all we need to do is work hard, show up for our families, and live well.

Don’t worry too much about the distant future. Just making the most of today is enough. 💪

A bit tired, but it feels like I'm doing the right thing.

February 18, 2022, past 9:00 PM

Sitting in the car, parked in some corner of the old neighborhood. My phone is dead, but I can’t be bothered to go home and charge it.

At the court today, Old Wang asked me, "You seem off today." I heard myself say, "Yeah, nothing about me is 'on' right now." The moment the words left my mouth, I startled myself. I’ve never spoken like that in front of my teammates before.

They all laughed, thinking I was joking. I laughed along, but the sound died halfway in my throat.

After the game, they suggested going to a bar to watch the match. I told them I was tired. That was the truth. But it wasn’t my body that was tired. I didn't want to go to that place; I didn't want to keep playing the role of Shen Linchuan—the passionate, talkative, well-connected guy. I was afraid that if I stayed any longer, I’d completely fall apart at some point.

So I just drove out. I’ve been circling the old neighborhood for nearly two hours, taking a bunch of photos. Using photography as an excuse, but really, I’m just hiding. Hiding from what? Hiding from going home and seeing Xiaomei, from pretending everything is fine in front of her. Hiding from Xinyu, afraid she’ll see how panicked I actually am, afraid my anxiety will be contagious.

Sitting here, I’m wondering—who exactly am I?

Is the person who said "nothing about me is 'on' right now" the real me? Or is the person I’ve been playing all these years the real me?

I don’t know.

All I know is that on the day of the department merger in 2021, I stayed silent at home for days. On the surface, nothing changed. I still went to the office, went to social dinners, went to play golf. But since that day, I started doubting myself. Authority slashed, office reclaimed, budget cut—what does that mean? It means I’m actually not that important. Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along.

Besides drinking and chatting, remembering people’s names and their family members, what else can I do? Do I have any real professional skills? I say "no problem, I’ve got this" at the company, but what am I "getting done"? The things I handle, others can handle too—maybe even better.

My daughter’s college entrance exam is approaching, and her grades are middle-of-the-pack. I want to encourage her, but I’m afraid of putting pressure on her. But honestly, I don’t know what to do myself. What advice can I give her? A father who’s already hitting his professional ceiling?

Then there’s my mother. I go back once a month, and seeing her struggle to move around makes me feel incredibly powerless. Medical bills are piling up. My sister takes care of her back home while I earn money here. But when does this end?

Xiaomei and I have less and less to say to each other. We each look at our own phones, occasionally arguing over Xinyu’s education, but neither of us wants to truly talk about anything. I don’t know if she sees me breaking down. Maybe she saw it long ago and just doesn't know what to do either.

I’m 42. Not young anymore.

In a few years, what will I have left to rely on? Connections? Enthusiasm? Those things were assets in my twenties. Now? Now they’re like a mask that’s getting more and more battered. Every day I’m taping it back onto my face, terrified it might fall off.

But today, a piece of it fell. On the court. In front of Old Wang.

I said it out loud. "Nothing about me is 'on' right now."

And then I ran.

Now I’m sitting here, looking at the old neighborhood outside the window, the lights blurred. I don’t know what to do next. Go home? Keep pretending everything is fine? Or...

Whatever. Let’s charge the phone first. It’s dead.

**Saturday, August 13**

Conversation with Xinyu in the Car

---

At 2:30 PM, she suddenly asked if I could go with her. I said yes almost without hesitation.

We drove around the old neighborhood with no particular destination in mind. She sat in the passenger seat, silent at first. Later, she started talking about her paintings and her thoughts on college. I listened, asking a question here and there, but mostly just listening.

She said she didn't want to let me down.

My eyes welled up a little.

I told her, "You are who you are; you don't need to become who I want you to be." I meant it.

She gave my arm a quick squeeze.

This afternoon, we didn't talk about the Gaokao or her grades. We were just in the car, in some corner of the city, the two of us sitting together.

It occurred to me that I hadn't spent time with her like this in a long while.

Perhaps some things I thought were lost are still there.

---

*Mark this day. Don't forget.*

🌅

Sometimes I feel like time just flies by. A couple of days ago, I came across some photos from my college years—back then, it felt like anything was possible. Now, I turn around and I'm already 42.

Youth really is a beautiful thing.

—— 11:47

October 12, 2022. Evening. On the high-speed train.

My phone has been off for twenty minutes now.

The moment the screen went black, I saw a message from my tennis partner, Old Wang: "Weekend match is canceled? What happened?" followed by a confused emoji. I didn't reply. I didn't even offer an explanation. I just shut it down.

Outside the window, the fields are rushing past, and the sky has grown dark. The carriage is quiet; across from me, a young girl is reading a novel. I don't know where to look, so I just keep staring at my luggage.

While packing this morning, I suddenly realized something strange—I was folding my clothes meticulously, placing them in order, even making a checklist for fear of forgetting something. I paused for a long time before realizing how foreign this "sense of planning" felt to me. When did I start becoming so careless? 2015? Or even earlier?

In the past, when I traveled for business, I’d just grab a handful of things and go. Wang Xiaomei would always remind me as I headed out the door: "Don't forget..." This time is different. This time, I know how long I’m going for, what I need to do, and what I have to take on. This certainty, ironically, brings a sense of indescribable fear.

I’m still not entirely sure about Mom’s situation. Sister Li said her appetite hasn't been great lately, and she’s still dragging her right leg a bit when she walks. Listening to these details, I realized how pitifully little I actually understand them. I go back every month, but it’s as if I’ve never truly *looked*. I just sit there, watch TV with her, chat about trivial things, and then leave.

And Linlin has been over there by herself all these years; I’ve never truly asked her if she’s tired.

The train pulled into a small station. Watching the people hurrying on and off the platform, a thought suddenly hit me—can I really keep this up? Twice a week, back and forth. Giving up the dinners, the tennis matches, the things I’ve grown accustomed to that make me feel like I still have value.

I’m not running away from the decision. I’ve already talked to Wang Xiaomei; the plan is in place. But sitting here now, I’m finally feeling it—this is about changing something.

It’s not just about changing a schedule.

It’s about changing how I understand myself.

I used to think I was successful. I could remember the names of my clients' children, I could be the life of the party at any dinner, I could maintain a vast network of connections. These things made me feel valuable, needed. But now? Now I’m giving all that up to do something I’ve never truly done—to be a real son, a real brother.

I’m afraid I won’t be good at it.

What’s even scarier is realizing that my fear of "not being good at it" is far greater than my regret over losing those social opportunities. What does that say about me? That I’ve been running away all this time? Or that I never actually cared about those things in the first place?

The phone screen is still black.

I realize it’s been a long time since I’ve done this—doing nothing, saying nothing, just sitting here and letting myself feel exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion. It’s something else. A weariness of the soul.

I don't know if I can keep going like this.

But I know I have to.

At a company meeting today, a Gen Z colleague asked me, "Mr. Shen, what do you think success is?"

I was taken aback for a moment. In the past, I would have talked about networking, performance, or titles. But over the last two years—especially recently—I feel like I’ve finally figured something out.

Success isn't at the finish line. It’s about who you walk with, who you are responsible for, and whether you are truly there when people need you.

Over the years, I’ve closed countless deals over dinners and built plenty of connections on the golf course. But the year my mother suffered a stroke, I realized that those things are truly weightless when your family needs you.

I’ve recently shifted my focus at work so I can go back to my hometown every month to be with her. Some friends call this a "waste of time." But now, I see it as the most worthwhile investment of my life.

My daughter asked me recently why I’m going to fewer business dinners. I told her that her dad is learning to do something more important—learning how to be a son and a father.

It’s not that work isn’t important. It’s just that I finally understand what real success looks like.

Something for us all to reflect on.

February 12, 2022 – Past 11:00 PM

I’ve put my phone down, but my mind is still racing.

At dinner, I saw Xinyu’s report card. She’s right in the middle of her class. Without thinking, the words just slipped out: "You need to work harder. Don't end up like your father."

The moment I said it, I regretted it. But it was too late.

She kept eating with her head down, but I saw her eyes welling up. Wang Xiaomei asked me, "What exactly do you mean by that?" I just brushed her off, saying something like, "Nothing, I just want her to do better." Then I started scrolling through my phone, pretending not to hear whatever she said next.

Now, sitting alone in the study, it’s finally hitting me what I’ve done.

I was projecting. When I looked at her grades, I was seeing myself—mediocre, stagnant, still stuck in the same place at my age. So I dumped my own fears onto her. "Don't be like your father" wasn't encouragement; it was a self-indictment, and I used my daughter as a punching bag for it.

She’s only sixteen. What’s wrong with being in the middle of the class? She loves drawing, she loves reading; she has her own rhythm. Why did I have to crush her with my own anxiety?

My authority has been stripped at work, and now I’m starting to fail as a father at home, too.

I can’t find a way out in my career, and I haven’t given my daughter anything decent either. Just a sentence full of self-loathing, followed by that look of hurt in her eyes.

What can I actually do?

Besides drinking at business dinners, pretending to be relaxed on the golf course, and coming home to hide behind my phone—what else am I capable of?

I have to apologize to her tomorrow. But I don’t know how to start. I’m afraid she’ll ask me why I said it. Because I don’t even have the answer for myself.

Lately, when we drive out to the countryside together, she’ll share things with me. Not a lot, but it’s genuine. I’m starting to realize that those moments are more meaningful than any number of business dinners I attend.

# Monday, July 18, 2022 – Rain

I don’t know how to write anymore.

During this morning’s meeting, I said something that is still looping in my head. My subordinates proposed a new marketing plan—it was quite creative. I looked it over and then just said, "I’m no longer the kind of person who can innovate."

The entire conference room went silent after I said it. I could feel them looking at me, but I didn’t look up. Later, I just said, "You guys decide," and shirked the decision that was supposed to be mine.

A Marketing Director, in his own meeting, saying something like that.

What was I thinking? I’m truly, completely finished.

I got a call from my sister yesterday; Mom fell again. Although it wasn't serious, my sister sounded exhausted. I told her I’d go back to see her this weekend, but I know my going won't change anything—I can’t change Mom’s health, and I can’t change the pain she feels every day. I can only sit there for a few hours and pretend that everything will be okay.

Xinyu has been silent again lately. Wang Xiaomei mentioned that her mock exam scores dropped again. I was eating at the time, and I just put my chopsticks down. I wanted to comfort her, but I’m afraid that anything I say will just be more pressure. I’m afraid my concern will become a burden. So we just sat there at the dining table, each staring at our own phones.

Back during the department merger in 2021, I thought I could handle it. I thought that with all the connections and passion I’d built over the years, I could stand my ground. But now I understand—those things were all illusions. When real hardship hits, when everything crashes down at once, my "passion" becomes a joke.

I used to be the person who made everyone feel comfortable. I remembered the names of my clients' family members; I could liven up any dinner party; my subordinates all said I was easy to get along with. But what was that, really? It was me escaping. I escaped real managerial responsibility, I escaped deep thinking, and I escaped holding myself to any real standard.

Now I’m in my forties. No deep professional expertise, no real growth, and no long-term preparation for anything. I’m like a person flattened by life, failing in every single dimension.

I’ve failed professionally. I’m powerless at home. My mother is suffering back in our hometown, my daughter is lost at school, and there is an increasingly thick wall between my wife and me. I want to change something, but I can’t change anything.

I sit in my office, watching the rain outside. Sometimes I think that maybe it would be better if I didn't exist at all. No pressure, no feeling of powerlessness—nothing.

But I don’t even have the courage to run away.

I can only continue to sit here, continue to go to meetings tomorrow, continue to pretend I’m still a Marketing Director, continue to laugh at dinner parties, and continue to go home to a silent wife and daughter.

I have failed completely.

I really don’t know what to do anymore.

# Diary

Saturday, August 20, 2022, 11:30 AM

We’ve been stopped at the service area for nearly an hour now. Xinyu is in the convenience store buying water. I’m sitting in the car with my phone on my lap, haven't moved in a long time.

What happened on the drive back yesterday... I still can’t quite put it into words.

When she said, "Dad, I know you’ve been really unhappy lately," my grip tightened on the steering wheel. I thought I had hidden it so well. Every morning, I still smile and ask her what she wants for breakfast; at the dinner table, I still tell those same old jokes; on weekends, I’m still the one suggesting we go for a drive in the countryside. I thought that was enough.

But my daughter saw right through me. She said I’m "always trying to prove something."

...

I don’t know when it started, but I began defining myself by her grades. Defining myself by whether she was outgoing enough, whether she was "successful." It was as if every choice she made was a reflection of whether I was failing as a father. I took all my work anxiety and my fears about aging and projected them onto her.

I cried in the restroom at the service area. It wasn't a breakdown, it was... the kind of cry that comes when you’ve been seen through. She didn't blame me; instead, she put her hand on my shoulder and said, "I’m okay, Dad." In that moment, I realized I had been looking for answers in all the wrong places.

I used socializing, enthusiasm, and networking to prove my worth. At dinner parties, I’m the one telling the jokes; on the golf course, I’m the one with the most friends. But none of that is real. What is real?

I don't know.

Maybe being real is just... sitting here and admitting that I’m scared. Admitting that I don’t actually know what I’m doing. Admitting that I’ve held my daughter’s life hostage with my own fears.

When she asked me, "What do you love doing most?" I couldn't give her an answer right away. I paused for a long time. Because I truly don’t know. Everything I "like" is for the sake of socializing, relaxing, or escaping. I haven't truly loved anything in a long time.

Or rather... I’ve forgotten how.

She’s back from the convenience store now, holding two bottles of water, sitting in the passenger seat. She didn't ask why I’ve been sitting here for so long. She’s just sitting there, being with me.

Maybe that’s enough.

Maybe I don't have to prove anything anymore.

March 15, 2022, 11:47 PM

I’ve been sitting in the car all night.

At 5:30 PM, Mr. Wang sent a WeChat message saying there was a dinner gathering tonight and asking if I wanted to go. I glanced at it and didn't reply. The phone buzzed twice more—it was my golf buddies, asking to play this weekend. I didn't reply to them either.

I’m just sitting here in the parking lot. The car is parked, engine off. Upstairs is home. Wang Xiaomei is probably cooking, and Xinyu is likely in her room drawing or reading. I know this. But I just haven't gone up.

This morning at the office, Xiao Zhang proposed a marketing plan. It was quite creative. I took a look and said, "Fine, just flesh out the details." That was it. In the past, I would have discussed it with him for half an hour, asked him about his thought process, and given him some encouragement. Now, I don't even have the energy for that. I heard my own voice—cold, like a machine.

I know he was a bit disappointed. I could tell. But I really can't force out any enthusiasm anymore.

Lately, I keep thinking: I’m 42. At this age, it feels too late for everything. Change jobs? No one wants a middle-aged marketing director. Change careers? With what? In the fifteen years I’ve spent accumulating "experience," what do I actually have besides knowing how to drink, how to talk, and how to remember clients' names? No hard skills, no real expertise, nothing substantial.

That department merger back in 2021 was when I truly understood. I thought I was a "Marketing Director," but in reality, I’m nothing. As soon as my authority was scaled back, I was exposed.

My mother is still back in our hometown; her legs still don't move well. When I went back last month, she sat in her wheelchair looking at me, something in her eyes. I said a lot of comforting things, but I knew they were all lies. I can't change a thing. My sister, Lin, is there taking care of her alone. I go back once a month, then I come back here. That’s it.

Xinyu’s grades are still average. Xiaomei brought it up at the dinner table again, asking if we should sign her up for tutoring. We didn't pursue the discussion; we just went back to our phones. I don't know what to do. I want to encourage her, but I’m afraid of putting pressure on her. I want to help her, but I know I can't.

So I just sit here in the parking lot.

No one needs me at that dinner. Mr. Wang will find someone else. My golf buddies will keep playing. Company matters will proceed according to the established plan. *I* will proceed according to the established plan. I said that sentence today, and it felt like I was talking about someone else.

I don't know how much longer I can keep up the act. I used to think I could wear this mask forever. Now I realize it breaks. It cracks, bit by bit.

Maybe this is just who I am now. No way back, no way out. This is it.

I should go upstairs.

But I’m still sitting here.

# Camera Roll - September 8, 2022

Xinyu at her desk, her profile caught in the light. She’s pointing at a detail in her drawing, a slight smile playing on her lips. Sunlight streams through the window, casting soft shadows across her sketchbook. She’s wearing that old grey sweatshirt, her hair pulled back into a messy ponytail.

A close-up of her hand and the drawing. You can see the crisp pencil marks on the paper and the faint smudge of graphite on her fingers. It’s a bird, drawn with incredibly fine lines.

Looking at these photos, I realized it’s been a long time since I’ve just watched her quietly like this.

Not to post on social media, or to brag to anyone about "how talented my daughter is." Just... wanting to capture the moment. The way she looks when she’s focused. The light in her eyes when she talks about her art.

Before, I might have thought about how to share these, how to let others see. Now, I just want to see her. Having them on my phone is enough.

She doesn't actually need me to put her on display. She just needs me to see her.

I think I’m finally starting to understand that.

# WeChat Message to Wang Xiaomei

Xiaomei, are you still awake?

It took me a long time to decide to send this. It might seem a bit out of the blue.

I’ve been thinking about something lately. I’ve always felt like I’m constantly charging forward for something—promotions, making money, looking successful in front of friends. But it feels like the faster I run, the further away I get from you guys.

When I got back today, I sat in the car for a long time. Xinyu was reading, and you were tidying up the house. It suddenly hit me that maybe I’ve been caring too much about work stuff. The department merger, the titles, the clients… none of that seems so important anymore.

I am a father, a husband, and a son. Maybe those identities are enough on their own. I don’t need to prove myself through anything else.

I know we haven't been talking much lately. We both just look at our phones, argue about Xinyu’s grades, and then that’s it. I’m responsible for that too. I’ve been running away, using social events and the hollow buzz of my WeChat Moments to fill a void.

I don’t know how to change all of this. Maybe it can’t be changed. But I wanted to tell you that I’ve been thinking about these things. I want to start over and really be there for you and Xinyu.

This probably sounds a bit stiff. Sorry.

Go to sleep, no need to reply. I’ll be home early tomorrow.

# Monday, August 15, 2022 | Sunny

Old Wang called yesterday afternoon. He said he’d rounded up a few of the guys for a round of golf this weekend and asked if I was in. My gut reaction was to say yes, but the words caught in my throat. In the end, I told him, "I’ll pass this time; I want to spend some time with the family."

There was a two-second silence on the other end. Then he laughed and said, "Alright then. Since when did you become such a family man, Old Shen?" I laughed too, but there was a feeling in my chest I couldn't quite put into words.

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time, trying to figure out why I said that. Usually, a weekend golf game is my top priority—it’s my time to unwind and keep up my professional network. But this time, I just didn't want to go.

It wasn't physical exhaustion. I just... didn't want to.

Lately, whenever I’m at a business dinner, I find myself wondering what my daughter is doing at home. Last weekend, she was in her room painting while I was in the living room watching the game. We were just there, each in our own space, with nothing to say to each other at dinner. Wang Xiaomei was in the kitchen. The three of us live under the same roof, yet it feels like we live in three different worlds.

The day before yesterday, the rehabilitation center called. Sister Li said Mom’s been in low spirits lately, maybe because of the heat. As I listened, it suddenly hit me: how long has it been since I actually sat down and spent time with her? Every time I go back to my hometown, it’s a rush—ask how she’s feeling, give her some money, and then hurry back. My sister is over there running herself ragged, and it never even crossed my mind to bring them both here for a visit.

So last night, I told Xiaomei that when we go back to my hometown next month, we should all go together and bring Xinyu along. Xiaomei gave me a look; she didn't say anything, but I could tell she was surprised.

I was surprised too.

I don’t know what this is. Maybe it won’t change anything big—work is still work, life is still life, the pressure is still there. But maybe... maybe I can change the way I deal with it all?

I wouldn't go so far as to call this an epiphany or a transformation. I’m just tired—tired to the point where even maintaining the image of the "enthusiastic Mr. Shen" feels like a chore. Maybe instead of keeping up the act, I should just sit down and see what’s left around me that I haven't already ignored.

My daughter is downstairs doing her homework right now. I can hear the sound of her pen on the paper. It’s very soft, very quiet.

Maybe that’s enough.

August 25, 2022 – Just past 11:00 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s already very quiet outside. Xiaomei went to bed a long time ago, and the light in Xinyu’s room is off. I don’t know why I’m still awake. Maybe it’s because of that meeting this afternoon.

The meeting itself was nothing special. Just a routine team meeting to discuss next month’s marketing plan. But I found myself doing something very unfamiliar—I didn’t immediately jump in and say, "There’s a problem with this plan; it should be changed like this." I just... listened. I listened to Xiao Wang explain his ideas, and to Manager Li raise his concerns. Then I asked a single question: "Where do you think the risks are with this direction?"

That was it. Not a particularly remarkable question.

But the way my subordinates looked at me changed. I could feel it. They were thinking—really thinking—instead of waiting for me to give them the answer. Later, Xiao Wang pointed out an issue I hadn't even considered. I felt... like I wanted to cry.

After the meeting, one of them asked how I was doing, if something was up. I didn’t deflect or tell a joke like I usually do. I said, "I’m rethinking some things. Maybe I’ve been looking for answers in the wrong places."

He froze for a second. I did, too. I’ve never said anything like that to a subordinate before.

Driving home, I thought about that conversation I had with Xinyu last week. She said I was "always trying to prove something." I was angry then; I felt she didn't understand me. But now... now I’m starting to understand what she meant.

I’ve been defining myself through achievements, titles, connections, those business dinners, and rounds of golf. It’s as if as long as I’m successful enough, popular enough, "enough" of whatever, then I have value. But what was the cost? I passed that anxiety on to Xinyu. I was looking for validation through her.

The moment she put her hand on my shoulder, I finally understood—she doesn't need me to prove anything. She just needs me to be real.

I’m sitting here now, 42 years old, and I suddenly don’t know who I am. Not "Marketing Director Shen Linchuan," not the "well-connected Mr. Shen," not even "my daughter’s father." Just... me. This person.

This person is exhausted. This person is uncertain. This person is a little bit afraid.

But maybe... maybe this is the beginning.

I don’t know what to do next yet. But at least today, I didn't pretend.

I’ll keep going tomorrow.

September 26, 2022. Late night, 23:47.

I can’t sleep. Again.

But this time is different.

I picked up my phone just now, intending to scroll through the news, but ended up flipping through those photos Xinyu sent me a while back. She’s been working on a project lately, photographing people in the old neighborhoods—the aunties selling vegetables at the wet market, the old man repairing shoes, the kids sitting in doorways dazed in thought.

I’d looked at them before, but I hadn’t truly *seen* them.

I only understood the difference tonight.

In that photo of the old lady selling wontons, the background is a blur; only her hands, gripping the wooden ladle, are in sharp focus. I never realized a person’s hands could tell so many stories. The wrinkles, the calluses, that sense of practiced ease—it’s all there. Xinyu turned her lens toward the things we overlook every single day.

It suddenly hit me: what have I been busy with all these years? Closing deals on golf courses, making toasts at business dinners, posting my smiling face on social media. What have I been looking at? Nothing but the surface.

And my daughter—the one I used to wish was a bit more outgoing—she’s using her lens to look at the silent ones. The people with no social media presence, no "status." She is looking at their truth.

I think maybe I could use photography to see those silent people, too.

Not to post on my feed. Not to prove I have "taste." Just... to see. To truly see.

This thought has changed the nature of my insomnia. Before, I’d lie in bed with my head full of company matters, my mother’s health, Xinyu’s college entrance exams, money... those things sat on my chest like heavy stones.

Now... I still can't sleep, but my mind is imagining. Imagining the things I’ve never truly looked at. Imagining what I would see if I picked up a camera myself.

Maybe I should ask Xinyu if she can teach me. Not as some "Dad wants to learn photography too" performance, but because I genuinely want to hear her talk about how she sees the world.

I’ve ignored her for so many years. She’s been speaking through her lens while I’ve been busy networking.

I’m only realizing now that maybe this is what I’ve been looking for all along. Not a promotion, not more connections, not proving I’m still young. Just... to see. To be seen. And to see others.

I need to have a real talk with her when the sun comes up.

Not as a father trying to guide her, but as someone who wants to learn how to see, going to her to learn.

I looked at my phone, then glanced out the window. The afternoon sun was a bit blinding.

I thought about it for a moment, but decided not to post.

In the past, I probably would have taken a photo, come up with a caption, and waited for the likes and comments to roll in. But sitting here now, it all suddenly feels less important.

I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately. Not work-tired, but a different kind of tired. I can’t quite put it into words.

Anyway, I think I’ll just get some rest.

# MEMO

**Date:** September 5, 2022, 8:00 AM

---

What Mom said keeps playing over and over in my head.

"Maybe sometimes, you could just slow down."

It couldn't be simpler. But it’s like I never truly listened.

Yesterday at the service area, I cried. It wasn't because of anything specific Xinyu said, but because of a sudden realization—what have I been doing? And for what?

What exactly am I trying to prove?

It’s been 15 years. From salesman to Director. The golf games, the dinners, the networking, the targets... it feels like I’ve always been running. Running fast, but never stopping to ask myself: is this what I actually want?

Xinyu saw right through me. She’s right. I *am* always trying to prove something. And I’ve been pushing all that anxiety onto her. Thinking about it now just hurts.

And yet, she still put her hand on my shoulder.

Maybe that’s what Mom meant. Not giving up, but—

Not having to get everything right. Not having to win every time.

Maybe I can just be a real person. Someone who gets scared, gets tired, and feels uncertain.

It sounds so foreign to me. But maybe it’s exactly what I need.

---

# September 24, 2022, Saturday, Sunny

What happened at the office yesterday has been looping in my head.

The proposal Zhang Hao brought over was definitely flawed; I saw the issues immediately. In the past, I would have just taken it over and said, "Go focus on something else, I'll fix this," then stayed up late to finish it myself, only to pat him on the shoulder the next day and say, "See? Like this." Back then, I would have felt a great sense of accomplishment, as if I were some kind of savior.

But yesterday, I didn't do that.

I asked him, "Where do you think the problem might be?" He started to stammer. I just stayed there with him, went through it together, pointed out a few spots, and let him try to fix it himself. He was a bit nervous while making the changes, asking me, "Is this right?" I said, "Keep going, think about it some more."

In the end, he figured it out on his own. It wasn't perfect, but he did it.

I told him, "Helping you learn how to do these things is more meaningful to me."

The moment I said that, even I was a bit stunned. Where did that come from? It didn't sound like something I would usually say. But as the words came out, I felt a strange sense of satisfaction—not the vanity of "I helped someone again," but seeing that spark of being trusted in his eyes.

It reminded me of driving to the outskirts with Xinyu last weekend. She pointed at the light and shadow on a tree and asked, "Dad, look at this." I took a photo with my phone. She didn't comment on my photo, and I didn't explain why I took it; it was just that moment where we both saw the same thing. That feeling was like... not needing to prove anything.

I used to be so eager to show what I could do. Telling stories at dinners, showing off my connections on the court, being the "guy with the answers" in the office. It was as if I didn't exist if I wasn't doing those things.

But over the last couple of days, I’ve realized that maybe value doesn't come from that.

When I truly just spend time with Xinyu, instead of trying to "guide" her to be more outgoing, our time together is actually more comfortable. When I help Zhang Hao learn instead of doing it for him, the satisfaction I feel is deeper than that old "I fixed it" feeling.

Last time I went back to my hometown, Sister Li said Mom was in a particularly good mood that day because we sat for a long time just chatting, without that restless "I'm here to help you" urgency.

I’m wondering if maybe this is why my insomnia has been easing up lately. It’s not because the problems are solved—the problems are still there, the career bottleneck is still there, Mom’s health is the same, and Xinyu’s college entrance exam is still looming. But I don't seem to need external things to validate myself as much anymore.

The person in the mirror looks the same, but something feels different.

Maybe this is just how it is. Take it slow.

# October 3, 2022, 9:30 PM

I’ve been lying in bed for half an hour now, still wide awake. Xiaomei fell asleep ages ago, and the light in Xinyu’s room is out. It’s quiet downstairs.

I’m still thinking about that notebook.

She suddenly pulled it out to show me this afternoon. It’s a thick volume, starting from 2019, filled with dense notes—the names of Mom’s doctors, dates of every follow-up visit, medications she’s taken, which days her legs wouldn't cooperate, notes from Sister Li saying Mom was in a bad mood and needed more company... As I flipped through the pages, my hands started to shake.

"You kept all of this by yourself?" I asked. My voice was so low I could barely hear it myself.

She didn't say anything, she just looked at me. I’ve seen that look many times before—it wasn't reproachful, which made it cut even deeper. It was as if she were saying, "You’re finally seeing this."

I always thought I was going back to my hometown every month. I remember being there; I remember the drives. But looking at this notebook... I suddenly realized that when I went, I was just *there*. I wasn't truly present. I didn't remember a thing. How many times did Sister Li tell me about Mom’s progress? I forgot it all. Every time I went back, I’d ask the same questions all over again.

Xiaomei was here, keeping track. Alone. For over three years.

As I held that notebook, I felt something deep taking root in my chest. It wasn't just guilt—that word is too light. It was... I don't know how to describe it. It was like suddenly seeing exactly how selfish I’ve been.

I wanted to say I was sorry. But I knew the words were useless. I whispered "Thank you" instead. She probably didn't even hear me.

Lying here now, I’m wondering—what exactly have I been so busy with these past 15 years? All those business dinners, the golf, the nights watching games at the bar... what do they look like now? I can’t remember a single joke I told at some dinner last month, but I can vividly picture my wife sitting under the lamp, writing those notes.

I am not a good son. That is the most honest thing I can say right now.

It’s not because I’m busy with work. It’s because I’ve been running away. I used "busy" as an excuse, but the truth is... I just didn't want to face it. I didn't want to face the fact that Mom is truly old, that she truly needs me, and that she might never be the same again. So I’d go once a month, take a quick look, and then flee back here. That way, I could tell myself, "I did my best."

But I didn't.

Xinyu has her college entrance exams next year. I’m always distracted by her grades during dinner, but have I ever truly been there for her? Do I even know what Xiaomei does around the house?

Something needs to change. I know that. But right now, I’m just lying here, feeling exhausted, powerless, and... full of regret.

Xinyu said she doesn't want me to give up.

September 18, 2022, past 8:00 PM

Just got back from the bar. I’m sitting alone in my study; it’s pitch black outside the window. Wang Xiaomei is downstairs watching TV, and Xinyu is probably in her room drawing. I haven’t turned on the lights. I’m just sitting here.

I met up with my golf buddies today. It was supposed to be a relaxing get-together, but not long after we sat down, I started saying some strange things. I told them, "I’ve realized I’ve been running away from something. I use my enthusiasm and my network to prove my worth." The moment I said it, even I was a bit stunned. It didn’t sound like something I would say.

Xiao Li paused for a moment after hearing that, then said, "This version of you is more trustworthy than the one who’s always full of energy."

I didn’t respond at the time; I just nodded. But those words have been spinning in my head ever since.

*More trustworthy.*

I had never thought about that. I always believed my value lay in that enthusiasm—remembering every client’s name, being the life of the party, making sure everyone was happy. That was my weapon, my proof of existence. But now he’s saying that the real me is more trustworthy than the performance.

I don’t know if that’s a good thing or a bad thing.

Sitting here, I wonder: how long have I been living through this "performance"? Since college? Or even earlier? What am I trying to escape? The fact that I don’t actually know what I want? The possibility that I’m not that successful? My fear of failure?

Or am I escaping the fact that I have no idea who I am?

I’ve made a decision. Starting next month, I’m cutting my weekly social dinners from three down to one. I know it sounds crazy. These dinners have always been the center of my life. But I need to save that time for other things. For Xinyu, for Wang Xiaomei. And maybe for photography—that thing I’ve always said I’d learn properly but never took seriously.

But to be honest, I don’t know what I’ll become once I cut back on those dinners. I’m afraid that without these activities, I’ll seem boring, mediocre. I’m afraid of losing that feeling of being needed at the table.

I’m still performing. Even now, as I realize this, I’m still performing—playing the role of a "man who is changing." It’s ironic.

But maybe that’s just the process. Maybe I can’t become my true self all at once, because I’ve already forgotten what that person looks like.

I can hear Wang Xiaomei laughing downstairs. She’s watching some show. It’s been a long time since I sat down to watch TV with her.

I should go down. But I want to sit here a little longer.

Sitting alone in the dark, it seems easier to see myself clearly.

March 16, 2022, 8:30 PM

Xinyu asked me today, "Dad, are you unhappy?"

I froze for a second. She was sitting on the sofa with a book in her hand, her eyes fixed on me. In that moment, it hit me just how long it had been since I’d really looked into her eyes.

I said, "Yes, Dad hasn't been very happy lately." I was a bit surprised by my own words. For so many days, I thought I’d been putting on a good front—smiling, asking about her homework, asking what she wanted to eat—but I had never actually told her that I wasn't okay.

Then she said, "I'm scared you guys are going to get a divorce."

My God.

I went completely numb. Her voice was small, but every word felt like a physical blow to my heart. I suddenly realized that she had seen everything: my silence these past few days, the coldness between Wang Xiaomei and me, the way we both just stare at our phones, the way we argue about her grades because we’re unwilling to actually communicate... I thought I was protecting her, but I was actually hurting her.

I walked over and sat down beside her. I didn't know what to say. I wanted to say "We won't," but I realized I couldn't say it with any certainty. I don't even know what state Xiaomei and I are in right now. We’re still together, yet it feels like we aren't.

I cried. Right there in front of her. Me—a 42-year-old man, a Marketing Director, a "success story" in my social circle—just broke down in front of my daughter.

Xinyu didn't say anything; she just leaned against me. I could feel her trembling.

I told her, "Dad has been feeling very lost lately, but it’s not your fault. I need to do something about it." I don't even know what I can do yet, but I know I can't go on like this. Not for anyone else’s sake, but for hers.

She’s asleep now. I’m sitting in the study, and Wang Xiaomei is in the next room. There is a wall between us, and so much more.

I don't know where to start. But I know I can't give up anymore.

I just can't.

# Diary

January 15, 2023 | Afternoon

Just got back from the office, and things still feel a bit surreal.

I had a meeting with my supervisor this morning about flexible working arrangements. I had prepared a whole list of reasons—my mother’s recovery requiring me to head back to my hometown regularly, my daughter’s upcoming college entrance exams and the need for support at home, things like that. But when I actually sat down across from him, I found myself deviating from the script.

I just said it straight: "The way I work might look different from before, but I believe I can do even better within this new framework."

Just that one sentence. No explanations, no justifications, none of that "making sure everyone feels okay" rhetoric I used to be so good at.

He said, "We’ll consider it." A very flat response.

But what I feel is a different kind of calm.

It’s a strange sort of peace. Not because I gained anything—he didn't give me a direct "yes." It’s because I finally spoke the truth. No hiding, no trying to act like that omnipotent Marketing Director. Just being honest: I need this.

In the past, I would have felt tense in a moment like this. I would have overanalyzed his expression and actively sought out his feedback. Now, I simply accepted his "consideration" and let it go. It’s not that I don’t care about the outcome; it’s that I know—regardless of the result—I have done what needed to be done.

It reminds me of a night not long ago back in my hometown. I was sitting in my mother’s room while she was asleep. I just watched her face, watching her slowly recover bit by bit. In that moment, I suddenly realized that I don't need to prove anything anymore.

I don't need to prove that I’m a "success." I just need to be a responsible son, father, and husband.

The work still needs to be done, but it’s no longer my everything. It’s not even the most important part of me anymore.

Tonight, I’m driving out to the suburbs with Xinyu. She’s been feeling a bit stifled lately, and I thought maybe a change of scenery would help her relax. We don’t need to say much. Just driving together, looking at the winter landscape.

That’s enough.

Really, that’s enough.

August 3, 2022, 8:30 PM

…I don’t know how to write this. Or rather, I don’t want to write at all. But if I don’t put something down, I feel like I’m going to completely fall apart right here on this sofa.

Xinyu asked me, "Dad, are you disappointed in me?"

I said, "I’ve stopped expecting things to change."

As I said it, I heard my own voice, and it sounded terrifyingly foreign. That wasn't me. At least, it wasn't the Shen Linchuan who tells jokes at dinner parties and remembers the names of his clients' children.

She lowered her head and kept eating. I lowered mine too. We sat there in silence, like two strangers.

I wonder, at what point does a father say something like "I’ve stopped expecting things to change" to his own daughter? When does he give up? I don’t know. All I know is that today, I gave up.

Another call from Mom’s side. Lin said Mom’s leg is swollen again and the recovery isn't going well. Listening to Nurse Li’s voice in the background—so professional and warm—only made me feel more useless. Even a nurse can do a better job than I can as a son. I go back once a month, and every time, I’m visiting a mother who feels more and more like a stranger. I am powerless.

And the company? Forget it. I don’t even want to talk about it. My authority is gone, and so is my sense of presence. I’m like a piece of old furniture in the office—still functional, but no one actually needs it.

Wang Xiaomei is in the bedroom on her phone. It’s been a long time since we’ve truly spoken. It’s not the silence of an argument; it’s something more terrifying—a total estrangement. I don’t even know how to start a conversation with her. What would I say? That I’ve failed? That I can’t protect this family? She already knows.

I used to be so passionate. I loved making friends. I believed I could handle anything. And now? Now I’m just sitting here, 42 years old, with nothing. No, not nothing—I have plenty of failures.

A part of me thinks that maybe it would be better if I didn't exist. At least then Xinyu wouldn't have a failure for a father. Mom wouldn't have to wait for a monthly visit from an incompetent son. Wang Xiaomei wouldn't have to live in the same house as a man who is completely broken.

But I don’t even have the courage to run away.

So I just sit here on the sofa. My phone is ringing; friends from the social circle are inviting me out. I didn't reply. I no longer have the energy to pretend to be that enthusiastic Shen Linchuan anymore.

That man is dead. Or maybe, he never truly lived to begin with.

…

I don’t know what tomorrow will bring. I’ve stopped expecting anything.

I’m sitting in my office, the computer screen glowing, but I’m not looking at it. My phone is on the desk; I’ve checked WeChat several times now, but I still haven’t opened it.

I got back from my hometown last night. The whole way back, my mother’s words kept echoing in my head: "You sound different. Is something going on?"

I stopped at a service area and cried for a long time.

It’s 8:00 AM now. Wang Xiaomei should be at school by now. I want to message her, but I don’t know how to say it. These past few days, it’s like there’s been something between us. She’s on her phone, I’m on mine; we occasionally argue about Xinyu, and then... nothing.

I keep thinking about what Xinyu said in the car last night. She said I’m always trying to prove something, always busy showing everyone how successful I am. She’s right. I’ve been pretending I’m fine, that I’m in control, but the truth is...

I’m just so tired.

And I realize I’ve been projecting that exhaustion onto her. Onto you.

I don’t know how to start this conversation. But I don’t want to keep going like this.

---

I typed a few words, then deleted them.

Finally, I hit send:

"Xiaomei, do you have a moment? I want to talk to you about something. It’s nothing major, it’s just... I talked to my mom on the phone last night. She asked if something was wrong. It made me realize that I’ve probably been pretending everything is fine lately, but the truth is, you can all see right through me. I’m sorry. I’d really like to have a proper talk with you."

After sending it, I stared at the message feeling like a fool. But at the same time, there was this indescribable sense of relief.

Maybe... maybe this is how it’s supposed to be.

August 13, 2022, past 11:00 PM

I’m sitting in the study; it’s pitch black outside the window. Downstairs, Wang Xiaomei is watching TV, and Xinyu went back to her room a long time ago. Not knowing what else to do, I opened this long-neglected notebook.

I still find it hard to believe what happened this afternoon.

Xinyu asked if we could go for a drive. Just a simple sentence like that. I said yes without a moment's hesitation. Lately, it feels as if there’s been a barrier between us—she stays in her room reading and painting, while I’m out socializing and drinking, and occasionally we sit at the dinner table in silence, brooding over her grades. I thought she had given up on communicating with me long ago.

It was quiet in the car. We drove for a long time without a destination, just like we used to. I wanted to say something encouraging about the college entrance exams or her grades, but the words caught in my throat. I could feel my own anxiety; it was like a fog hanging between us.

Then she said it. "What I need is a father, not a person full of anxiety."

My hands gripped the steering wheel tight. The tears just started falling. I couldn't control it. All these days I’ve been pretending everything is fine—laughing at the office, bragging at dinners, and then coming home to sit alone in a daze. I thought I had grown used to failure, to mediocrity, to the idea that nothing would ever change. But hearing her say that made me realize I had been forcing that "habit" onto her.

I pulled over to the side of the road and told her I was sorry. My voice was hoarse from crying. She sat in the passenger seat and didn't say a word, but she placed her hand on mine. We just stayed like that for a long time.

I don’t know if this changed anything, or if nothing has changed at all. I still have to go to work tomorrow and deal with those endless matters. My mother’s recovery is still the same, and work is still the same. But tonight, in that dark car, I allowed myself to cry in front of her for the first time. For the first time, I didn't have to pretend to be the father who has everything under control.

Maybe that’s enough. Maybe what I need isn’t to change everything, but to allow myself to live as a real person. Someone who gets tired, who gets scared, and who fails.

Her hand is still on mine. It’s a feeling I haven't felt in a very long time.

Xiaomei, are you still awake?

I was sitting downstairs for a while just now. Xinyu asked me what was wrong today, and I didn't just brush her off like I usually do. She told me, "Mom is worried about you, too."

I’ve been thinking about it, and I felt I should ask you.

Have you noticed it too? I haven't really been myself lately. I know that. Running back and forth between everything—work, my mom’s place... sometimes I don't even know where I’m supposed to be.

What do you think? Am I not doing enough?

To be honest, I’m feeling a bit tired right now. Not physically tired, just... I don't know how to describe it. It feels like I’m being pulled in different directions. Work, home, and then my mom’s side. I want to do everything right, but it feels like I’m not doing anything well.

What do you think I should do?

I know how ridiculous this looks, sitting in a shopping cart.

A 42-year-old man, at 11:30 PM, putting together a "Rebirth Kit" for himself. Sleeping pills, soul-soothing books, nutritional supplements—it’s like trying to assemble a new version of myself on an e-commerce platform.

I didn't even really intend to buy them. Or rather, I want to buy them, but I don't believe they’ll actually work.

The sleeping pills might work. I believe that much. My sleep has been terrible these past few months—lying in bed for two or three hours unable to drift off, my brain replaying clips of failure on a loop. The silence during the department merger, that one comment when my photography was rejected, the sound of my mother’s weak coughing over the phone. These things play on repeat in the dark.

But those books? I know I won’t actually finish them. I’ve bought photography tutorials and marketing management books before; they’re all piled in the corner of the study now, pages yellowing. I’m the kind of person who puts a book down after two chapters. I always have been. I used to lie to myself with the excuse of being a "practical learner," but now I just admit it—I have no patience, no depth.

The vitamins and fish oil are even more ironic. I’m pretending that I’m still "taking care of myself." My life for the past two months has been: work, social dinners, home, insomnia, and work again. I rarely exercise, and my diet is a mess. Buying these supplements is like saying, "Look, I’m still trying." But I know it’s self-deception.

I stayed on the checkout page for nearly ten minutes.

My finger hovered over the screen, but I just couldn't press down. Part of me wanted to place the order immediately, wanting to believe that 1,200 yuan could buy a transformation. The other part of me knew that even if all this stuff arrived tomorrow, nothing would change. The sleeping pills might give me two extra hours of sleep at most, the books would still be tossed aside, and the vitamins wouldn't change my career ceiling, my mother’s illness, or the increasingly cold communication between Xiaomei and me.

So I just left the cart there.

Like an unfulfilled promise. Or rather, a promise I know I’ll never keep.

It’s 11:30 PM. I can hear Xiaomei watching TV downstairs, the volume turned low. Xinyu should be asleep by now—she’s been studying late for her exams recently, and sometimes I hear the sound of pages turning in her room. I should go to sleep, but I know I’ll just lie there for another two hours wide awake.

Maybe I should click "Pay."

Maybe I shouldn't at all.

I don't know.

February 21, 2022. 11:30 PM

My fingers have been hovering over the keyboard for a long time. I don’t know what to type.

I checked the recruitment sites again today. That headhunter messaged me again this morning, asking if any of the recent positions caught my eye. I said yes, asked a couple of questions, and then stopped replying. He’s probably given up on me by now.

What am I thinking? Do I really want to jump ship? Or am I just fantasizing that someone will come and rescue me?

Sitting in the office during a meeting, listening to my boss talk about new sales targets, my mind started to drift. I realized I’ve been in this same position for 15 years. When the merger happened back in 2021, I knew—I’ve probably hit the ceiling. I stayed silent at home for three days after that. When Wang Xiaomei asked what was wrong, I said "nothing." But in reality, I was thinking: *It’s over. This is it.*

But I can’t change a thing.

When I open the recruitment sites, I stare for a while and then close them. Open, then close. I’ve been doing this for two weeks. I know exactly what I’m doing—I want to leave, yet I’m terrified to leave. I’m 42. Where else can I go? Who wants a middle-aged man with no real skills, whose only talent is drinking and socializing?

To be honest, what have I relied on all these years? Passion? Networking? Remembering clients' names, telling jokes at the dinner table, handing out cigarettes on the golf course? Those things were assets in my 20s. But now? Now, those things can’t protect me. I haven’t accumulated any professional expertise; I have nothing of depth. I’m just... mediocre. I always have been.

I looked at my daughter’s report card today. Still middle of the pack. I wanted to encourage her, but I didn't dare say too much for fear of pressuring her. The result was that I said nothing at all, my mind wandering again during dinner. Wang Xiaomei shot me a look, a look I know all too well—*What are you thinking about now?* We haven’t had a real conversation in a long time.

My mother’s medical bills are due again this month. My sister is back in our hometown taking care of her. I take time off once a month to go back, and yet I can’t change anything. Looking at my mother in the rehabilitation center, I think about my own future. Maybe one day I’ll be lying there too, and my daughter will be just like me—taking leave every month to visit, feeling utterly powerless.

What am I running from?

Maybe I don’t want to change at all. Change means admitting failure. Admitting that I might have spent the last 15 years heading in the wrong direction. Admitting that I’m not that "success story." Admitting that I’m just an ordinary, unremarkable middle-aged man with no way out, no surprises left, just... this.

My fingers are still hovering over the keyboard.

I should go to sleep. I have to see a client tomorrow. I have to smile, talk, and pretend that everything is just fine.

But I have no strength left.
