Sitting in the study, it’s pitch black outside. Downstairs, Wang Xiaomei is watching TV; Xinyu went back to her room a long time ago. I don’t know how to start writing this.

I sat on the sofa for a long time just now. Just sat there, didn't say a word. She didn't say anything either. I thought it would be awkward, but actually... it wasn't. It was just companionship, I think.

What she said keeps playing over and over in my head: "I don't need you to change anything. I just need you to tell me what you're thinking."

When I heard that, my eyes welled up again. I’ve cried too much these past few days. I was thinking about how for all these years, I’ve been performing a role—at the office, on social media, even at home. I always thought I had to hold everything up, that I had to prove something. But the truth is... she saw through it long ago. She’s been right there all along, just waiting for me to tell the truth.

I’ve been carrying this all alone. This realization came too late.

She didn't ask me to switch jobs, or change my career, or suddenly become a different person. She just wanted me to... speak up. It’s that simple. And yet it took me all these years to understand.

I still have to go to work tomorrow, still have to see those clients, still have to smile. But it’s different now. Now I know that when I come home, I don’t have to perform anymore. I can just sit on the sofa and tell her I’m tired, that I’m scared, that I don’t know what to do.

It sounds like it should be simple, but I’m still a little afraid. Afraid that this honesty might one day become a burden. Afraid that I’ll habitually retreat back behind the mask. Afraid of... a lot of things.

But at least for tonight, we are together. Truly together.

No more staring at our own phones. No more arguing about Xinyu’s grades and then refusing to talk deeper. Just... sitting together. Being seen.

I don’t know what things will be like starting tomorrow. But at least I’m no longer fumbling around in the dark alone.

She’s right downstairs. The thought brings me a sense of peace I’ve never felt before.

November 23, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Just finished showering and I’m lying in bed, but my head is still buzzing. Xiaomei fell asleep ages ago; I didn’t dare turn on the light for fear of waking her.

That meeting today keeps looping in my mind. I’m still wondering if I’ve lost it.

During the meeting this afternoon, Xiao Zhang asked me what success means. I was going to give him the standard answer—performance metrics, promotions, networking, that kind of thing. But the words changed the moment they hit my lips. I heard myself say, "The most important thing is who you spend your time with and who you are responsible for." The conference room went dead silent after that. It was the kind of silence that made me feel a bit lost.

Later, before they left for the day, Xiao Wang and the others told me that what I said really gave them something to think about. I just gave them a smile, but my heart was actually racing.

Lying here now, I’m asking myself—do I really believe what I said?

Or am I just making excuses for myself? Because the department merger has left me sidelined, am I just romanticizing "taking care of family" to make it sound noble? Is this just so I can feel okay about giving up the social obligations, the connections, and the things that used to make me feel important?

But... I also know it’s not all an excuse. Spending these past two months helping Mom with her rehab, watching her recover bit by bit—that feeling is genuinely different. It’s not some hollow sense of achievement. It’s real—she needs me, and I can help her.

The question is, can I keep this up?

I’m running on fumes. Going back to my hometown once a month, handling work, spending time with Xinyu, and still dealing with those business dinners—I say I’m giving up socializing, but I can’t let go at all. That’s just who I am. Without those things, who am I?

What I’m afraid of is that in two months, I’ll start craving that feeling of being "needed" again. Craving the feeling of everyone listening to me on the golf course. Craving the version of myself that felt powerful.

When that time comes, will I just slip back into my old ways? Will I end up feeling guilty toward Mom, Xinyu, and Xiaomei?

Whatever. There’s no point thinking about this now.

Work tomorrow.

But I want to write this down—at that moment today, when I said those words, I meant them. At least in the moment I said them, I was sincere.

I hope I can remember that sincerity.

August 20th, past 9:00 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s very quiet outside. Xiaomei is already asleep, and Xinyu has gone back to her room. I didn’t know what to do with myself, so I opened this notebook that I’ve never used before.

...I still feel like recording today.

On the highway back from Zhengzhou, Xinyu suddenly turned around to look at me and said, "Dad, I know you haven’t been happy lately." My hands jerked on the steering wheel. I thought I had hidden it well—I still smile every day, still go to business dinners, play golf, and joke with my subordinates at the office. But she saw right through me. A 16-year-old girl saw right through a 42-year-old man.

I didn’t answer immediately. As the car kept moving, I was thinking of how to lie to her. And then... I cried. I pulled over at a service area and cried in the restroom. It wasn't for myself, but... I don't know how to put it. It was the realization that I’ve been using her grades and her personality to define my own success. It was the realization that I’ve been projecting all of my fears onto her.

After getting home, Xiaomei and I sat in the living room. For the first time... truly the first time, I said it out loud to her. I said I was scared. Scared that I’ve hit my ceiling, scared that my mother’s health is failing, scared that our marriage has turned into two strangers staring at their own phones. Scared that Xinyu’s life would be ruined by my anxiety.

Xiaomei didn’t say anything; she just sat there and listened. Then I said, "I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places. I’ve been defining my worth through social status, career achievements, and even Xinyu’s grades. But maybe none of that is what matters most."

When I finished saying that, I felt a strange sense of relief. It was like muscles that had been tensed for years suddenly went slack.

But at the same time, I felt guilty. Guilty for the pressure I’ve put on her. I thought of all those times at dinner when I was distracted by her grades, the times I used "other people's children" to provoke her, the times I disguised my own insecurities as expectations for her.

I don’t know what to do. I don’t know what I’m supposed to look like starting tomorrow. I still have to go to work, still have to attend those dinners, still have to go back to my hometown every month to see my mother. But maybe... maybe I can stop trying to prove something.

Maybe I can just be a real person. Someone who is scared, exhausted, and flawed.

I don’t know what that means. But tonight, in this quiet study, I am allowing myself to think this way.

# July 24, 2022, Evening

The car has been parked by the river for nearly two hours now.

My phone keeps ringing; I can see the screen lighting up. Friends’ names, colleagues’ names, and the rehab center where my mother is staying. I haven’t picked up any of them. I don’t know what to say. Or rather, I simply don’t have the strength to say anything anymore.

It was still bright out when I left the office this afternoon. I drove without a destination, ending up at the gates of Xinyu’s school. The gates were closed, but I could see students out on the playground. I just sat in the car for half an hour, imagining her in the classroom. Her grades have slipped again. When Wang Xiaomei told me yesterday, I could sense the disappointment in her eyes. I want to comfort Xinyu, but what would I even say? I’m on the verge of being crushed myself; what right do I have to encourage her?

After leaving the school, I drove here to the river.

I’ve been to this river many times before, usually with friends, full of talk and laughter. Today, I sit here alone, watching the sunset reflect off the water, thinking of nothing at all. Or perhaps, I’m thinking of too much, to the point where nothing is clear anymore.

My mother’s health has taken another turn for the worse. Sister Li called me yesterday to say that Mom is having trouble swallowing lately and they need to adjust her recovery plan. I could tell she was trying to reassure me, but I couldn't take it in. I go back once a month, and every trip is the same—sitting by her bed, watching her struggle to move an arm, while I can do absolutely nothing. My sister is exhausted over there, and I’m exhausted over here, yet Mom just keeps getting worse, bit by bit. Is this just what reality is?

I don’t even want to mention the company. After that merger in 2021, I thought I could accept it and keep doing my job well. Now it seems I can’t accept any of it. I’m just a marginalized middle-aged man, still pretending I have some value. The golf outings, the business dinners, those hollow smiles—I’m truly exhausted.

Wang Xiaomei hasn't been talking to me much lately. Or rather, neither of us knows what to say. She’s busy with school matters, and I’m busy with (or rather, escaping from) work. Xinyu stays in her room reading, and we each stare at our own phones. This is our family now.

Is this really it for me? Am I just going to be flattened by life into a useless person, unable to change a single thing?

I remember when I used to be so passionate, so confident. Back then, I felt like I could achieve anything. Now, as I sit here watching the river flow by, I can’t even remember who that person was.

It’s getting dark. I should go home. But I really don’t want to move.

Maybe it would be simpler if I just didn't exist.

But I’ll still drive back. Because even if I can’t change anything, I still have to go back.

— Shen Linchuan

I compare it to the other photos on my phone—group shots at the golf course, everyone smiling brightly, me in the center looking like the picture of success. But looking at those photos, I feel nothing. Yet, looking at this one of Xinyu, my eyes well up.

I’ve been thinking about it all night. Maybe it’s because, in this photo, I wasn’t performing. There’s no forced smile, no image to maintain. It was just... being there with her, for real. She was doing her thing, and I was watching her. That’s all.

It makes me realize what I’ve been doing all these years. The golf, the dinners, the socializing, the "networking"... I treated these things as achievements. But in reality, I was escaping. January 15, 2022. Sunny.

12:30 PM, Parking Lot.

I’ve been sitting in the car for half an hour now. My hands are on the steering wheel, but I have no idea where to go next. I originally planned to just grab a bite to eat, but now I can’t even make that simple decision.

The morning meeting is still looping in my head. When Mr. Li said, "Welcome to the sales team," I smiled broadly and said, "I’m thrilled to be working with everyone." My voice sounded fine—enthusiastic, professional, no issues there. But what was I thinking at that moment? I was thinking: *this is a demotion.* Even if the title still says "Deputy General Manager," everyone knows what it really means. Moving from a private office to an open workspace; from Marketing Director to some spot in the Sales Department. I could feel people in the office watching me, sizing me up, thinking, "Even Mr. Shen has his day."

I took the initiative to take on two new projects. Before Mr. Li even finished speaking, I rushed in with, "I can handle it." Looking back, my tone was a bit too eager, a bit too sycophantic. It was like I was trying to prove something—that I’m still useful, that I’m still worth keeping around. It makes me feel sick.

When did I become like this? I used to have weight behind my words. Clients listened to me, subordinates listened to me, even my superiors gave me face. And now? Now, I’ve been in my new seat for less than two hours, and I’m already acting humble.

My hands are still on the wheel.

I’m wondering, at 42, what else can I do? Should I actually open those recruitment sites and take a look? But what if I do? Who wants a demoted middle-aged man? I don’t have any real technical skills, nothing of depth. I just know how to drink, how to talk, and how to remember people’s names. Those things work in an office, and they work at a business dinner. But now? Now, those things can’t protect me.

Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along. Mistaking enthusiasm for ability, and networking for competitiveness. Can networking protect me? Yes. Can it protect me for a lifetime? Clearly not.

I still have to go home tonight. Wang Xiaomei will definitely be able to tell something is wrong. She’ll ask, "What’s up?" and I’ll say, "Nothing, just busy at work." Then we’ll both go back to staring at our phones. Xinyu will be in her room doing practice problems for the Gaokao. I should go encourage her, but right now, I can’t even encourage myself.

Is there really no other way out for me?

I don't know. I really don't know right now.

Listening to her talk about her world.

Sometimes I realize that being there is the answer itself.

Sitting in the office, everyone else is gone. Outside the window, the sky is darkening. I haven't turned on the lights; I'm just sitting here.

Li Jian’s phone call is still spinning in my head. He asked why I suddenly canceled tomorrow’s golf game, his voice full of surprise—in fifteen years, I’ve never said no. I heard myself say, "I’m rethinking some things. Maybe I’ve been using all these activities to avoid taking a real look at myself."

The moment those words left my mouth, I was stunned. Was that really me speaking?

But... yes, that’s exactly it.

Since yesterday, it’s as if I’ve suddenly seen through something. I’ve been constantly busy, socializing, talking business on the golf course, playing the role of the enthusiastic, know-it-all guy at dinner parties. But for what?

To prove I’m successful? To prove I’m worth being seen?

I projected all that anxiety onto her. When her grades were bad, I got anxious. When she wasn't outgoing, I felt regret. I never even truly asked her what she wanted. I was just... looking for the things I’d lost within her.

When we pulled over at the service area, I cried. It wasn't a cry of despair, but... the kind of cry that comes from being seen through. She didn't blame me; instead, she put her hand on my shoulder. In that moment, I felt something strange—not being forgiven, but being accepted. Accepted by a sixteen-year-old girl.

Here I am, forty-two years old, always pretending to be strong, pretending to have everything under control. But the truth is, I’m terrified. Terrified of having no achievements, terrified of being seen through, terrified of losing things. So I keep proving, keep staying busy, keep filling myself up with all these activities.

The golf, the dinners, the networking... it’s all an escape.

Not going to the course tomorrow feels strange. A little scary, a little liberating. I’ve already told Li Jian; he probably thinks I’ve lost my mind. Maybe I have, a little. But right now, I need to stop.

I need to be with Xinyu. I need to have a real conversation with Wang Xiaomei. I need to... think about what I actually want.

Not the title, not the connections, not the validation from others.

Maybe just... to be a real person.

But I don't know what that means yet.

But recently, while staying by Mom’s side and just watching her sleep in her room, I realized that some things are worth so much more than a promotion. It might sound a bit sentimental, but it’s the truth.

I also had a heart-to-heart with Xiaomei a couple of days ago—it’s been a long time since we’ve talked like that. I feel like I have a clearer sense of what I want now. Not the kind of "wanting" where you feel you have to prove something, but something real, something with warmth.

Do you have a moment? I wanted to ask how Mom’s recovery is progressing lately. Has she said anything?

— Shen Linchuan

Xiaomei, I’ve been sitting in my room thinking for a while.

I really took what you said to heart. I know it seems like I’ve been constantly worrying all these years, and then passing all that anxiety onto Xinyu. You’re right—I’ve been reflecting lately, and maybe I really have been giving her more pressure than encouragement.

I want to change that. Not for the sake of her grades, but so she can feel that we are truly there for her. It’s like with my photography lately—I’ve realized that when I actually focus on what’s right in front of me, I don’t feel as anxious.

I want to say thank you. Thank you for your patience over the years, and for being willing to sit down and have this talk with me today. I know I can be a bit "last-minute" sometimes, but I want to take this seriously.

This weekend, I’d like to spend some quality time with Xinyu, like those drives we used to take. No asking about schoolwork—just being there with her. What do you think?

[Image: A corner of the dining table, dinner remains uncleaned, the night view outside the window is a blur, and the glow of a phone screen reflects off the dishes.]

Sometimes, silence carries more weight than words.

I spent a long time talking with Xiaomei tonight—about Xinyu, and about us. There were no arguments; instead, there was a sense of calm I’ve never felt before. I realized that all these years, we’ve been constantly talking, but rarely truly listening.

She said, "It’s not about her grades; it’s about making sure she feels that we are truly there for her."

When I heard that, I put down my phone.

Lately, I’ve been taking a lot of photos—with my phone, with my camera—capturing the little things. My daughter’s profile as she focuses on a book, my wife’s silhouette in the kitchen, the sunlight streaming through the window at my mother’s rehab center. Sometimes I scroll through them, not to send them to anyone, but just to remember.

Maybe this is what I’ve been learning lately.

To stop rushing to prove anything. To just be present, to observe, and to record it all.

Goodnight.

Monday, August 8, 2022, 10:15 AM

I’ve been sitting in the office for two hours now. The computer screen is on, but the emails are still the same ones from yesterday. I don’t even know what I’m looking at.

Liu Ming came by this morning to ask about the proposal for that real estate project. I just told him, "Just follow the standard procedure, no need to ask me." He froze for a second. I could tell he wanted to say something, but in the end, he just turned and walked away. I didn't even look up. This is who I am now—a piece of trash sitting in the Marketing Director’s chair, unable to make even the most basic decisions.

Mom fell again yesterday. My hands were shaking when Sister Li called me. Lin said she’d already taken her to the hospital; the X-rays showed no fractures, but Mom was crying in her hospital bed. I heard her sobbing over the phone. I can’t do anything. I’m here, she’s there, separated by hundreds of kilometers and my own endless incompetence.

Xinyu’s grades dropped again on this month’s exams. Wang Xiaomei didn't blame me directly last night, but I could feel it. She was being loud in the kitchen, the clatter of dishes and bowls was piercing. I wanted to go in and say something, but in the end, I didn't. We sat at the dinner table, both staring at our phones. I can’t even remember the last time we had a real conversation.

I used to think I was good at handling all of this. The networking, the passion, that smile that never seemed to fade—those things used to make me feel like a success. And now? Now I look at myself in the mirror and all I see is a fraud. A fraud who talks and laughs at the banquet table but can’t do a single thing right once he gets home.

Maybe I shouldn't even exist.

No, I’m not talking about *that*. I’m just… tired. Tired to a degree I can’t even describe. I don’t know how to keep going. My work is meaningless, and I’m meaningless at home. I can’t change Mom’s health, I can’t save Xinyu’s grades, and I can’t fix the silence between me and Wang Xiaomei.

I don’t even know why I’m still sitting in this office.

I have a client to see this afternoon. I’ll have to find a way to squeeze out a smile.

3:15 AM. Can’t sleep again.

I dug out my college yearbook from the bottom shelf, covered in dust. What was I doing back then? Looking at the face in the photo, I keep asking myself—was I really that charismatic?

I remember back in the Student Union, everyone used to gravitate toward me. I could make any event a success. My roommates said I was a natural-born socialite. I believed them back then. And now? Now, I’m just a middle-aged man who knows how to drink, how to talk, and how to remember people’s names. That’s it.

I just went through and deleted my WeChat Moments. All those posts about work achievements—"Just closed another big deal," "Team performance hit a new record"... looking at them now, they feel sickeningly fake. Halfway through deleting, I wanted to post: "Sometimes I wonder, what am I actually good at?" I even typed it out, but then I cleared it all. How could I post that? Everyone expects me to be "Mr. Shen"—the guy who’s always fine, the guy who can handle anything.

I sent a WeChat message to an old college classmate. A stupid question: "Do you still remember me?" He replied, "How have you been lately?" and I just froze. How am I supposed to answer? Tell him my position was gutted? Tell him I pretend to be busy in the office every day even though my authority is gone? Tell him I’m anxious because my daughter’s grades are failing, I feel powerless because my mother’s health is declining, and my wife and I have nothing left to say to each other?

I couldn't say a word.

The day my authority was stripped, I stayed home in silence for a few days. On the surface, I’m still all smiles to the outside world, but inside I’m thinking—have I wasted these last 15 years? I thought I had some kind of "core competitiveness," but it turns out a single departmental merger was enough to wipe it all out. Those connections, those relationships—they were all hollow. One corporate decision, and I’m nothing.

Besides drinking and small talk, what else can I do?

I really don’t have any professional skills. Nothing deep. Not a single thing I’m truly an expert in. I’m just... an empty shell who knows how to smile, how to talk, and how to remember names.

Wang Xiaomei is asleep. She’s always on her phone lately; we’re like two strangers. I want to talk to her from the heart, but every time I try, the words won't come out. She wouldn't want to hear it anyway. She only cares about Xinyu’s grades and household chores.

What am I even thinking? I’m 42. What can I do? Switch jobs? Who would want me? I have no specialized skills. Keep coasting at City Real Estate? Pretending to be happy, pretending everything is fine every single day?

The twenty-something boy in the yearbook is smiling. Back then, he thought he would always shine like that.

Now I’m just sitting here at 3 AM, flipping through a yellowed book, asking myself: Who am I, really?

When will I stop lying to myself like this?

February 8, 2022. Past 11:00 PM.

The lights are off and I’m lying in bed. Xiaomei fell asleep ages ago, but I’m still wide awake.

The scenes from today’s meeting keep looping in my head. Mr. Li told me, in front of everyone, that my proposal was "not cost-effective." Just that one sentence ruined my entire afternoon. I felt it right there in the conference room—it was like being slapped in the face in public. I could feel everyone’s eyes on me. All I could manage was, "Right, I understand," but my voice sounded like it was coming from someone else.

I snapped at Xiao Wang this afternoon. His report did have issues, but the tone I used… thinking back on it now, I was way out of line. The office went dead silent; everyone started pretending to work. I could tell he was shaken. That’s not like me. I’ve never been that kind of person.

But I just couldn't control it. After being shot down, I needed an outlet, and his report became the target. I know it wasn't fair. I also know he’ll hold a grudge against me for it.

Lying here now, I’m asking myself—what the hell is wrong with me?

I always thought I had a certain set of skills. Networking, communication, getting things done. That’s how I’ve made a living all these years. But Mr. Li’s comment today stripped me down to nothing. I suddenly realized that maybe I don't have any real expertise at all. I can drink, I can talk, I can remember the names of a client's kids—and then what? And then nothing.

When my authority was cut back, I didn't think much of it; I could still smile and say, "No problem, let's keep moving." But now… now I’m starting to panic. I’m 42. If this job really disappears, what else can I do? I don't have any specialized skills, nothing with any depth. Am I just… a social tool?

Xinyu is in the living room reading, Xiaomei is asleep in the bedroom, and I’m lying here thinking about all this mess. I’m supposed to be a father, a husband, a good boss. But today, I was none of those things. I was just a middle-aged man who lost control after being rejected.

I have to go to the office again tomorrow. I have to face Xiao Wang’s startled face. I have to keep pretending like everything is fine.

I’m so tired.

But… maybe I should apologize to Xiao Wang tomorrow. It’s the least I can do.

February 11, 2023    Night

Mom said, "Thank you."

Just those two simple words. I was sitting in her room, looking at the photos on the wall—her during physical therapy, her soaking up the sun in the yard, her laughing with Nurse Li—and suddenly, I felt like crying.

It wasn’t the kind of crying that comes from pain. It was... how should I put it? Like something had finally clicked into place.

For the past few months, all I could think about was that I had to take care of her; it was my responsibility. Every month when I took time off to come back, my head was filled with "what I need to do"—accompany her to rehab, buy her supplements, figure things out with Linlin. Back then, I was exhausted. I’d lie in bed at night with my mind still racing, worrying about how to cover the medical bills and how to balance work and home.

But today was different.

When I took out my camera to take her picture, I wasn't thinking about the obligation of "I must document her recovery process." It was more like... I just wanted to see her get better. I wanted to capture these moments. I wanted her to see herself improving, bit by bit. The feeling was completely different.

As I was framing those photos, I realized I wasn't just completing a task; I was celebrating something. Celebrating that she’s alive, celebrating her slow recovery, and celebrating the fact that we still have time together.

Her eyes lit up when she saw the photos. And then she said, "Thank you."

I suddenly understood that the "sense of achievement" I’ve been chasing all these years—getting promoted at the company, being the center of attention at dinners, winning a round on the golf course—none of it felt as real as this moment.

Back in 2021, when the departments merged, I sat at home in silence for days. Looking back, it seems a bit ridiculous. At the time, I felt like my value was shrinking, my authority was shrinking, like I as a person was shrinking. But now... I don’t know, it feels like I’m actually growing? Not in terms of job title, but something else.

I’m no longer that person who needs to prove himself through something. I’m just a son taking care of his mother, a father accompanying his daughter on a drive to the outskirts, a husband who’s still working through the kinks in the kitchen with his wife. Living like this... it feels quite whole.

My sleep has been much better these past few days. I don’t wake up startled in the middle of the night anymore.

I have to head back to the city tomorrow, but the feeling of going back is different this time. It’s not like I’m escaping something. It’s more like... I know exactly where I’m returning to.

#MiddleAge #Life #Cheers

October 5, 2022, past 11:00 PM

I can’t sleep. Again.

What my boss said this afternoon keeps looping in my head: "Try not to let it affect your work." I could tell it wasn't a suggestion; it was a warning. In his eyes, I’ve already become one of those "uncommitted" people. I can feel that look of his—it’s like a label stuck to my skin. That’s why I blurted out "I’ll find a way" so quickly, so desperately trying to prove something. But find a way? How?

My sister called again today. She said Mom keeps looking for me. "Mom asked why it’s been so long since she last saw you." Just that one sentence made me freeze. I go back every month, but it sounds like I’ve never truly been there. I realize now that I’ve been running—running from the medical bills I don’t understand, from the expectation in Mom’s eyes, and from the pressure my sister is carrying all by herself. I use "being busy at work" as an excuse, but the truth is, I’m just hiding.

Now I have to go back twice a week. I discussed it with Xiaomei, and she agreed, but I could see that look in her eyes—the "you’re finally stepping up" kind of expectation. I feel both forced and guilty. Guilty for all these years of running away, and guilty toward my sister.

But honestly, I’m not mentally prepared. Those business dinners, the golf games, the scenes where I’m the center of attention... I’ve always believed my value came from those things. Now that I have to give them up, I feel an indescribable sense of panic. It’s not that I can’t let go; it’s that... I don’t know who I am without them.

Am I being too selfish?

But I don’t know what to do. I don’t want to truly give up that sense of achievement, yet I’m starting to find it all a bit ridiculous. I’m stuck in the middle, unable to move.

Xinyu asked me today, "Dad, what’s wrong?" I told her nothing, that I was just a bit tired. She gave me a look and didn't say anything else. Even my daughter can see it.

I really don't know if I'm doing the right thing.

August 14, 2022. Night.

Wang Xiaomei was still on her phone as she sat on the sofa. It suddenly hit me just how long it’s been since we really sat down and talked.

Not the "Have you eaten?" or "Is our daughter done with her homework?" kind of talk. I mean *really* talking.

I took the initiative to sit beside her and asked how things were at school. She looked up, and I could see the flicker of surprise in her eyes. Then she started telling me about one of her students, going into such detail that I realized I had no clue what she’s been busy with lately.

I’ve been so caught up in my own head—my mother’s health, work issues, our daughter’s grades... just pacing in circles by myself. It never occurred to me that she’s been under pressure, too. That she’s been carrying the weight of this family just as much as I have.

As she spoke, I noticed her eyes welling up. Then, she took my hand.

We just sat there like that, holding hands, saying nothing.

It’s been so long since I felt something like this. It wasn’t that hollow enthusiasm you find at a dinner party, or the forced smiles of professional socializing. It was... being seen. Being seen by her.

I know this doesn't change anything. My mother is still sick, work is still a mess, and our daughter’s college entrance exams are still looming. But at least... at least I’m not bracing against it all alone.

She’s still holding my hand. I haven't let go.

Maybe I’ve really had my priorities backward all these years.

---

I have work tomorrow. But tonight, I just want to sit here for a while.

August 27, 2022, 7:00 PM, Study Room

...

I sat here for a while and finally decided to write this down. I don't know why, but I have this overwhelming urge to record something today. Maybe it’s because I’m afraid I’ll forget.

Ever since we got back from the service area, something has felt off. Xinyu fell asleep in the passenger seat, and as I drove for hours, her words kept looping in my head. "Dad, you’re always trying to prove something." Those words are stuck in my heart like a nail.

I never thought of myself as that kind of person. I always believed I was working hard for my family, thinking about my daughter’s future. But today I realized that maybe I haven’t been looking at her at all. I’ve been looking at her report cards, her prospects, the version of her I want her to become. Not... her.

In the car, she put her hand on my shoulder. Just a light touch. My eyes welled up instantly. It wasn’t a breakdown; it was more like... the feeling of being seen through. She saw through my act, my anxiety, and the way I’ve been using her grades to fill some hole in my own heart. But she didn't hate me for it. Instead, she... accepted it?

It’s so strange.

I started thinking back on our conversations over the last few months. Every time we eat, I’m asking, "How was the test today?" or "Has your class ranking improved?" I haven't even asked what she’s reading lately or what she’s painting. I have no idea what she actually likes. Or rather, I know, but I haven't truly listened.

Today in the car, I asked her a question I haven't asked in a long time: "Xinyu, what is your dream?"

She froze for a second. Then she said she didn't know. She said it very quietly.

I realized it’s not that she doesn't know. It’s that I never gave her the space to even think about it. I’ve always been telling her what to do and which direction to push in.

...

Everything with my mother, the stress at work, the dwindling conversations with Xiaomei—I’ve piled all that pressure onto her. And I did it in the name of "caring."

Sitting here in the study now, I still don't quite know what to do. The guilt is heavy, but at the same time, there’s this strange sense of... relief? It feels like I’ve been playing a role for years, and today I finally got to stop and catch my breath.

Maybe I don't need to get everything right. Maybe I can admit that I’m scared, tired, and uncertain. My daughter saw through all of it, and she’s still here.

I’m not entirely sure what this means for me yet. But at least... today, I’ve started wanting to change something.

Not to prove how good I am. Just... I want to truly get to know her.

# June 15, 2022 – Past 11 PM

Hiding in the study. Wang Xiaomei is already asleep. The light in Xinyu’s room is out, too.

I don’t know how to write this. My fingers are shaking.

I nearly broke down this afternoon. Xiao Wang just casually said, "Mr. Shen, you look exhausted." Just that one sentence, and I almost lost it in the conference room. I said, "I'm fine," but my voice sounded wrong. Then I rushed into the restroom and sat on the toilet, my eyes burning, my breathing a mess for half an hour.

What on earth am I doing?

The photography thing still hasn't passed. I’ve read that rejection letter from the submission platform over and over—"Work lacks a unique perspective." I know. I know it all. I was never some talented photographer; I’m just a middle-aged man who likes messing around with his phone, deluding himself into thinking he’s doing something creative.

Laughable.

I should have realized it the year the departments merged. Back in 2021, I should have known—Shen Linchuan, this is your ceiling. You won’t be promoted again; your authority will only shrink; everything you’ve built over the years actually amounts to nothing. Networking? Passion? What are those things even worth now?

I’m 42.

Mom called last month saying her leg is hurting again. Linlin said the recovery has stalled. I take time off to go back every month, sitting there watching Mom drag that leg around, and I can’t do a thing. The doctor says she needs better rehabilitation equipment, more sessions—it needs money. My salary is 450,000; it sounds like a lot, but—Xinyu is about to take the Gaokao, and she says she wants to go to university abroad. I’ve done the math on how much a year’s tuition costs. Mom’s medical bills. The mortgage.

What am I doing? I’m swinging clubs on the golf course, telling jokes at dinner parties, pretending everything is fine in front of friends.

I can’t keep up the act anymore.

Truly. I knew it the moment it happened this afternoon—that passionate Shen Linchuan, the one who’s always smiling, always has a solution... he’s dead. Or rather, I just don’t have the strength to play him anymore.

Xiaomei asked me what was wrong. I said nothing, just tired. She just went back to her phone. Xinyu is in her room studying; I wanted to go in and be with her, but I’m afraid I’ll cry. I’m afraid of her seeing me like this.

I’m a failure.

I know it sounds self-pitying, but that’s how I feel. A failure professionally, powerless at home, and my "creativity" is a joke. Do I have any value? What else can I even do?

The face in the bathroom mirror—red eyes, fine lines at the corners, gray hairs. When did I become this person?

I don’t know.

I really don’t know what to do anymore.

June 22, 2022. Night.

Another hint.

During the meeting this afternoon, the boss said, "I hope everyone can take on more responsibility." That tone... I heard it loud and clear. He was talking about me. Taking too much time off. Going back to my hometown once a month—they’ve noticed.

I know what he’s thinking. It started back in 2021 when the departments merged, and now it’s just becoming more obvious. They are slowly squeezing me out. First it was my authority, then their attitude, and finally... I don't dare think about it.

I didn't eat after getting home tonight. I opened my laptop and browsed some recruitment sites. Just a quick look—Marketing Manager, Regional Supervisor... they’re all the same kinds of roles, and the salaries aren't much higher than what I have now. Some are even lower. I’m 42. My education is nothing special. Aside from these 15 years of real estate experience, what else do I have?

I really have nowhere to go.

Mom’s condition got worse again this month. Sister Li was being tactful on the phone last time, but I could tell. The after-effects of the cerebral infarction are aggravating. I take leave to go back every month, spending all that money and energy... but what’s the use? She’s still slipping away, bit by bit...

I don't want to keep writing.

Wang Xiaomei asked if I’d eaten. I said yes. She didn't ask again. She’s on her phone; Xinyu is in her room drawing. I’m sitting in the living room, unable to do anything.

Why am I such a failure?

 I’m still not over that photography thing. I actually thought I could do something different. And the result? Rejected. I thought my passion, my connections, my... everything would help me, but it was all a lie. I don't have any real talent at all. I’m just a middle-aged man who survives on bullshit and social climbing.

And now, I can't even keep up the social climbing.

Am I really obsolete?

42 years old. What else can I do? Change jobs again? Where would I go? Learn something new? Is it too late?

I don't know.

I really don't know.

Subject: Thoughts on the new project

Dear Mr. Wang,

Thank you for your trust in assigning this project to me. After receiving your email yesterday, I spent the evening reflecting on it and spent this morning further organizing my thoughts. I would like to share some of my ideas with you.

My experiences over the past two years have led me to rethink what it truly means to execute a project well. In the past, I might have focused more on speed and numbers, but now I want to approach this project in a new way—one that focuses more on quality and team development. I believe this approach will ultimately bring greater value to the company. Specifically, I want to establish clearer standards during execution, provide the team with more room to grow, and ensure that every stage of the process can withstand scrutiny. I understand that this may require more time and patience, but I am confident it will be worth it.

I have already drafted a preliminary project framework and plan to send you a detailed execution proposal by this Friday. If you have any thoughts on this direction, I would welcome the chance to discuss them with you at any time.

Best regards,

Shen Linchuan
December 18, 2022

# Phone Memo

**2022-11-23 14:30**

---

Xiao Li told me afterward, "Mr. Shen, what you said today really gave me a lot to think about."

I was taken aback for a moment.

Sitting in my office now, I’m still wondering what he meant. It wasn’t the usual "good talk" politeness; the tone was different. He had this look in his eyes—he was serious.

I always thought the things I said in the meeting—about taking care of my mother, changing plans, dealing with uncertainty—were just me explaining why I’ve been taking more leave lately and why I’ve been turning down social invites. I figured people would just listen and move on, treating it like any other colleague’s personal business.

But he said he’d been "thinking about it for a long time."

It leaves me feeling... I don’t know how to put it. A bit gratified? Or maybe just surprised?

Maybe because I’ve been so exhausted lately, what I said ended up being real. I wasn't putting on that "I’m fine, everything’s under control" act. It was just... the truth.

It’s a strange feeling. Compared to the "look how successful I am" talk at business dinners, this feedback feels like... something different.

I don’t know how to describe it.

Maybe I should write this down to look back on later.

# Nov 3rd, past 11 PM

Can’t sleep.

That meeting from this afternoon is still looping in my head. I was thinking about my mom’s rehab schedule—Sister Li said we need to step up her arm exercises this week... I was so caught up in those thoughts that I didn't catch a word of what Zhang Min was saying. By the time I snapped out of it, she was repeating herself in that polite but icy tone: "Mr. Shen, what do you think of this idea?"

I nodded, pretending I followed. It wasn't until I checked my email later that I realized I’d missed the entire strategic direction for the quarter.

Before leaving work, Zhang Min made a point of coming by my office. She said, "We all have things going on at home, but we still need to stay focused at work." She’s right. I could tell she was trying to cover for me, but those words felt like a thorn.

Am I really not cut out for this job anymore?

I just opened a recruitment site and browsed around. Operations Director, Marketing Manager... I looked for a bit and then closed the tab. It hit me—even if I jump ship to a new company, the problem won't go away. I’ll still be thinking about Mom. I’ll still zone out in front of clients. I’ll still be torn between these two identities.

Escaping the job won't change anything.

Right now, I feel trapped between two worlds. On one side is the world I’ve been used to for twenty years—business dinners, golf, networking, that feeling of being needed. On the other side is the world I’ve recently been forced into—Mom’s rehab, monthly leave requests, that sense of powerlessness.

I don’t want to give up the former, but I can’t fully commit to the latter.

Xiaomei hasn't had much to say to me lately; we just sit there staring at our own phones. I want to talk to her about all this, but I don't know how to start. She’d probably say, "It was bound to happen sooner or later." Or she might say nothing at all—just that silent kind of agreement.

What do I actually want?

I want Mom to be okay. I want Xinyu to do well on her college entrance exams. I want work to go as smoothly as it used to. I want to still be able to play golf and grab drinks and talk big with my friends. I want...

But it seems like I can't have all of it at once.

I have to head back to my hometown tomorrow. Sister Li said Mom’s rehab hasn't been great this week; she still can't lift her arm properly. I need to go see her. But there’s an important client meeting tomorrow afternoon that I’ve already pushed back twice.

What am I supposed to do?

Forget it. I'll stop thinking. The sun's already coming up.

September 22, 2022, 9:30 PM

I’m having a bit of trouble sleeping tonight. It’s not the kind of insomnia driven by anxiety, but rather a feeling of things churning inside me that need sorting out.

During dinner, Xiaomei mentioned that Xinyu has become reluctant to talk to us again lately. Her tone wasn't accusatory, but I could tell—she was waiting for me to say something. Instead of changing the subject or getting defensive like I used to, I paused for a moment. In that instant, it suddenly hit me; I’ve been thinking lately that maybe I’ve given her more pressure than encouragement.

When I finally said it out loud, Xiaomei looked at me, her eyes glistening a little. She said, "I’ve been waiting for you to say that."

Those words struck a chord. Not because of how profound they were, but because—she saw me. Not the Shen Linchuan who talks and laughs at business dinners, but the real me, the one who cares and is reflecting.

I thought back to that time we went out taking photos. I took Xinyu to the old district once and captured a shot of her lingering in front of a bookstore. The sunlight was hitting her face, and she looked so focused. I didn't say anything about grades or school applications that day; I just walked with her and watched. She talked a bit more than usual then, telling me about an author she likes.

I realize now that maybe I’ve been caring for her in all the wrong ways. Every time we meet, I’m thinking about how to encourage her, how to push her, how to make sure she doesn't fall behind. But what she actually needs is for me to just be there. Not for her grades, but so she can feel that we are truly present with her.

This realization brings a bit of shame, but also a sense of relief. At least now I see it.

Tomorrow, I want to go take photos with Xinyu. I won't say a single word about the college entrance exams. I’ll just accompany her, look at what she wants to look at, and record it. Maybe that’s what I should have been doing all along.

Xiaomei is asleep beside me. She’s been exhausted lately, too. We’ve both been walking in our own anxieties for far too long. Maybe starting now, we can try a different way.

March 31, 2022, 11:45 PM

I dug out this old, dusty diary. I’m not sure why, but I just felt like writing something today. Maybe it’s because I’m exhausted—so tired that I don’t even have the strength to keep that smile on my face anymore.

My wife is already asleep. Xinyu is in her room reading. The house is quiet, save for the occasional sound of a car passing by downstairs.

I’m sitting in the study, looking out at the night sky. No stars, just a vast expanse of black.

Lately, I’ve been stuck on one question: who am I, really? Not the name Shen Linchuan, but... who am I at my core?

For all these years, I’ve been playing a role. At the office, I’m the enthusiastic, well-connected Marketing Director who gets things done. At dinner parties, I’m the one who livens up the atmosphere and remembers everyone’s name. Even at home... I’m performing. Pretending everything is fine, pretending I’m not actually that worried about my daughter’s grades, pretending there’s no distance between Xiaomei and me.

But this pretending is killing me.

The last time I went back to my hometown to see my mother, I cried in front of her. In that moment, I realized I didn't have the energy to act anymore. It wasn't a release; it felt more like a total breakdown. I sat by her bed, a 42-year-old man, sobbing like a child. Mom just patted my hand and didn't say a word.

Looking back, that might have been the most honest moment of my life.

I need to accept a fact—I might just be mediocre. Not the "humbly calling myself mediocre" kind of mediocre, but truly, utterly, unchangeably mediocre. I’m not getting promoted again. My career has likely peaked. I don’t have any grand achievements or deep, specialized expertise. I’m just a middle-aged man who coasted into middle management on the back of networking and enthusiasm.

But... maybe that’s not the end of the world.

I am Xinyu’s father. Even though our personalities are different, and even though I don’t really understand the books and art she likes, I’m learning to accept her. I drive her out to the suburbs—not for any particular reason, just to be with her. That in itself seems to be enough.

I am Xiaomei’s husband. We don’t have some grand, sweeping love story, but we’ve walked through all these years together. She has her life, I have my circles, but we are still together. Maybe that’s the truth of marriage—not eternal passion, but simply staying.

I am my mother’s son. She has trouble getting around now and she needs me. I go back to see her every month, and even though I feel powerless every time, I still go. That’s what I can do, and maybe that’s all I have to give.

I don’t need a job title to prove my worth. I don’t need to be the center of attention at dinner parties. I don’t need everyone to like me.

It’s enough to just inhabit these roles: father, husband, son.

I put down my pen and look out the window. The sky is still black, but it doesn't feel quite as suffocating.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll still go to those dinners, and I’ll still chat and laugh with my subordinates in the office. But that won’t be everything anymore. It’s just a part of my life, not the whole of it.

I’ve given up on the idea of changing. But perhaps this giving up is actually a different kind of beginning.

# Work Log

**Date:** Sunday afternoon, March 20, 2022

**Weather:** Overcast, a bit muggy

---

Spent some time in the office this afternoon. There wasn't anything urgent, really; I just didn't feel like staying at home.

I dug out that 2015 marketing case study—the commercial plaza project. From the initial positioning to the final conversion, there are still some things in that process worth looking at. Back then, our team worked so closely together, and every stage had a clear, steady rhythm. Looking back now, I realize some of the details I handled then are things my younger colleagues today might not even think of.

It’s not that people today aren't capable, it’s just… we seemed to have more drive back then.

Lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I’ve been running away. I’m always thinking about jumping ship or changing environments, as if a new place would magically change things. But what Mom said the other day keeps playing in my head: "You’ve done your best." It sounds like a comfort, but it actually stings quite a bit.

I’m starting to think that instead of constantly wondering where to go next, I should think about what I can still do in this position. The departments merged and my authority has shrunk—that’s a fact. But that doesn’t mean I have no room to move.

I read a case study today about how a competitor actually improved efficiency through more refined operations despite limited resources. I’m wondering if our marketing department could try something similar. Not a massive overhaul, just making the fundamentals more solid.

For instance, I’ve been dragging my feet on the performance appraisals. Next week, I’ll try to systematize this and create a clear career path for everyone. I should have done this a long time ago.

Also, I plan to organize a set of process documents for the team. Not to show off to upper management, but just to give everyone’s work a bit more logic.

To be honest, this feeling is a bit unfamiliar. I’m not used to sitting down and thinking things through like this. In the past, I always winged it—I’d find a way once the pressure was on. But now… maybe this feeling of "having a plan" isn't so bad?

Still have to head home tonight. Xinyu has been studying for her exams lately; I want to sit with her for a while. Not to ask about her grades, just to be there.

See you Monday.

# March 8, 2022, past 11 PM

Mom is right. I don’t need to prove anything to anyone.

But as I sit here now, I still feel incredibly exhausted.

When I went back to my hometown this afternoon, Mom asked if something was wrong. I intended to smile and say "nothing" like I usually do, but my eyes welled up. I didn't expect to cry in front of her, like I was still a child. In that moment, I lost all control and just poured everything out—the career issues, the age thing, the self-doubt. I told her I’m 42 now, and I might have no way out.

Mom didn’t say anything comforting; she just listened. Later, she said, "You don’t need to prove anything to anyone."

I cried even harder then.

But now that I’m back in my room, the heaviness has returned. The feeling of being accepted by my mother is real, but at the same time, I realize—I’m 42, and I still need to sit in front of my mother and cry, still need her to tell me these things. That in itself says something. It shows how hollow I am inside, and how hollow everything I’ve done all these years has been.

When Lin looked at me from the doorway, I saw something in her eyes. We both knew what the other was thinking, but neither of us said a word. She’s here taking care of Mom, and I’m in the city as a Marketing Director, but the truth is, we’re both exhausted.

I don’t know what to do.

The golf, the business dinners, the laughter on WeChat Moments... those things used to make me feel successful. Now I realize they were just things I used to lie to myself. I have no real skills, no real depth. I’m just a smooth talker. A 42-year-old man who has hit the ceiling and has nowhere left to go.

Wang Xiaomei hasn't been talking to me much lately either. We’re both in our own worlds, occasionally arguing over Xinyu, and then falling back into silence. I don’t know what’s left between us.

I remember myself back in university; back then, I still had so many possibilities. And now? Now, this is it.

Mom’s words keep spinning in my head: "You don’t need to prove anything to anyone."

But do I need to prove something to myself?

I don’t even know anymore.

Linlin, I…

I really don’t know what to do anymore.

Just now at the office, my team proposed a new marketing plan. I listened for a long time, but in the end, all I could say was, "You guys just handle it." The look in their eyes… I could see it. I’m completely useless now.

How’s Mom? How has she been lately? She looked like she’d lost even more weight when I went back last month. I might be a bit late with the rehab center fees this month. The company… never mind, let’s not talk about it.

You’re over there taking care of Mom all by yourself and handling everything. And me? I can’t even keep my own job steady. My daughter’s grades are slipping, and Xiaomei has barely been speaking to me lately. I can’t even… I can’t even step up and take responsibility the way you do.

I’m just a failure. Look at me—42 years old, and I can’t do anything right.

I’m sorry. I know things are even harder for you. I shouldn’t be telling you all this.

It’s just… I’m really starting to break down.

Xiaomei, are you there?

I want to talk to you. Not the kind of talk where we... you know, end up fighting over Xinyu’s grades.

I sat in the car for a long time when I got back today. I didn't come upstairs right away. I was thinking about whether Xinyu has been especially afraid of us fighting lately. I’ve been replaying what she said to me the other day over and over in my head. She said, "Dad, Mom, can you guys please stop always..." — you heard her too.

I don’t know how to put this. Have we been a bit... disconnected lately? I come home every day and just look at my phone, and you do the same. The few times we do talk, it’s always about her schoolwork or household chores. It feels like there’s nothing else anymore.

I’m not trying to blame you. I know it’s not your fault. I’ve been... busy with work events, away from home a lot. But I’m wondering what Xinyu thinks of all this. Does she feel like... our home isn't really a home?

I think maybe we should try, even if it’s just... sitting down and actually talking sometimes. We don’t necessarily have to solve any problems. Just... I don’t know, like how we were when we first met.

What do you think? How have we been doing lately?

I’m serious.

Xiaomei, I talked to my supervisor about something today.

Just now in the office, I took the initiative to propose an idea—whether I could adjust the way I work. I’d work as usual in the city from Monday to Wednesday, and then handle things remotely from my hometown on Thursdays and Fridays. This way, I can spend more consistent time with my mom without letting my work responsibilities slip.

My supervisor said they would consider it. It was a strange feeling at the time. In the past, I probably would have been very nervous, terrified that a request like this would be seen as a lack of commitment. But this time, I was just completely honest—I told them that while my way of working might look different now, I believe I can do an even better job. After I said it, I actually felt a sense of relief.

You know, for the past two years, I’ve been wondering why I always felt like I wasn't good enough in some way. Now I realize it’s probably because I’ve been trying to hide who I really am. It’s exhausting to keep that up.

I’d really like to talk to you about this. It’s not just about work; it’s a new understanding I have of myself. You’ve always been there for me, and I want you to know that my perspective on a lot of things has changed.

When are you free? Maybe over dinner tonight?

Xiaomei, have you noticed… that I seem to be changing lately?

When we were eating just now, I saw the way you stopped your chopsticks and looked at me. I know you were observing me. Actually, I’ve been observing myself too, I just didn’t quite know how to put it into words.

I’ve been thinking about why Xinyu said, "Dad, you’re different lately." I reflected on it for a long time before I finally understood—for all those years, it’s like I was always performing. Performing enthusiasm at dinners, performing success at the office, even performing the role of a "good father" at home. But in reality, I wasn't truly present for any of it.

When I was taking photos of Xinyu today, she asked me why. I suddenly realized that if I were still my old self, I probably would have made up some reason to make it seem like I was being thoughtful. But I didn’t. I just told her straight out—I wanted to see how you look when you’re focused on something. That was it.

She didn’t say anything, but I felt a difference.

I know our communication over the years has become a bit… shallow. Each of us looking at our phones, arguing over Xinyu’s grades but never wanting to actually talk. I think maybe it’s not because we have nothing to say, but because we’ve both been pretending that everything is fine.

I don’t know how long this change will last, or how far I can go with it. But I want you to know—I see it now. I see everything you’ve put in over the years, how you keep the house in perfect order while I’m away on business, the way you worry over Xinyu. It’s like I was always blind to it before.

That look you gave me just now—I saw it.

I was wondering if we could try together… to live more authentically? Not that we have to force a change, just… be more real.

What do you think?

# MEMO

**Date: Saturday Morning, August 20, 2022**

---

I need to write down what happened on the highway yesterday. Not to forget it, but to ensure it never happens again.

Xinyu’s words are still echoing in my head. "Dad, I know you’ve been really unhappy lately. Every time you frown, I know you’re judging my grades."

— I was seen through. And not by a stranger, but by my own daughter.

She said she feels exhausted—not because of her studies, but because she can feel my anxiety. When I heard that, I pulled over at a service area. I sat in the car and cried. Not for myself, but for what I’ve been doing to her.

I need to change. Not her, but me.

**Specific Actions:**

1.  **Don’t ask about grades at dinner** — This is the most immediate one. I’ve realized that every time I sit down to eat, all I’m thinking about is her class ranking or how her last exam went. Stop it. Dinner should just be dinner.
2.  **Listen more, talk less** — My habit is to give advice, lecture, and share my "experience." But she doesn’t need that. She needs to be heard. Next time she speaks, I will shut up. Truly shut up. Ask her what’s on her mind, and then just listen.
3.  **Put the phone away when driving on weekends** — Yesterday, when she put her hand on my shoulder, I realized how long it had been since we had that kind of connection. It’s not because she didn’t want to; it’s because I’m always on my phone, thinking about work, or obsessing over her grades. Next weekend when we go out, the phone stays in the back seat.
4.  **Have a real talk with her** — Not about school, not about future plans. Just... about me. Tell her I’ve been unhappy lately, but it’s not her fault. Tell her I’ve been using her grades to define myself, and that’s my problem. Apologize to her.

---

**Reminder to Self:**

Your value does not lie in "proving" yourself.

I need to read this over and over. Not through my job title, not through my daughter’s grades, and not through three dinners a week and rounds of golf. Those things will pass. But I only have one daughter.

I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places.

In the car, when she said, "I know you’ve been really unhappy lately," what did I feel? I didn't feel judged; I felt seen. I felt accepted. She didn't pull away because of my anxiety; she moved closer.

Maybe accepting my own vulnerability is more important than maintaining a perfect image.

---

**To-do this week:**
- [ ] Find a time to talk to Xinyu alone (not at dinner)
- [ ] Put the phone away while driving this weekend
- [ ] At dinner, only ask about the happy parts of her day—no talk of grades
- [ ] Call Mom—not out of guilt, but because I want to hear her voice

---

I don’t know where this leads, but I know I can’t go on like this.

December 2, 2022, 8:30 PM

I’ve been home for half an hour now. My wife is tidying up in the kitchen, and Xinyu is in her room doing practice problems. I’m sitting on the sofa, still feeling a bit dazed.

On the drive back from my hometown this afternoon, I couldn't stop thinking about what Lin said. She told me, "Having you involved makes me feel a different kind of pressure—it’s no longer the pressure of carrying it all alone, but the pressure of us being in this together. It makes me feel supported."

My eyes welled up the moment I heard it. Truly, I never expected it to feel this way.

All these years, I thought I was "helping," but deep down I knew I was mostly escaping. I’d go back once a month, sit with my mother for a bit, chat with Lin for a few minutes, and then hurry back to the city. It was as if as long as I wasn't physically there, those problems weren't my problems. Lin was there by herself, taking care of Mother and handling all the trivial chores, while I stayed here, continuing with my dinner parties, my golf, and that whole lifestyle of mine.

I haven't even properly thanked her.

Today, we washed Mother’s feet together. Lin was guiding me while I did the work. Mother’s feet were freezing, so I rubbed them for a little longer. Lin suddenly said those words, and we hugged. I haven't hugged my sister like that in a long time. It felt like something in my chest finally let go.

It wasn't that feeling of being surrounded by people at a dinner party. It wasn't that feeling of being patted on the back on the golf course. It was... the feeling of being truly needed. The feeling of being seen.

Lin also mentioned that we could work out a long-term care plan together. I agreed. This time, I wasn't just being perfunctory; I truly want to do this.

On the way back, I was wondering: what have I been chasing all these years? Promotions, networking, being the center of conversation at dinners... but for what? When the departments merged in 2021, I sat at home in silence for days. All that networking, all those dinners—in that moment, they all felt incredibly hollow.

I’m only realizing now that perhaps I’ve been building my self-worth on the wrong things.

Xinyu’s grades still haven't improved much lately. I got a bit distracted again during dinner. But this time, instead of hinting that she should work harder like I used to, I asked her what she’s been reading lately. She said she’s reading *To Live*. We talked for a while, and she said that Yu Hua’s characters don't live to live better; they live just for the sake of living itself.

I felt like crying a little when I heard that.

Maybe I need to learn that living doesn't necessarily mean being more successful than others. Accompanying my mother, supporting Lin, and having real conversations with my daughter—these, too, are the meanings of life.

I’m tired, yes, but it feels different now.

It’s no longer the exhaustion of being chased by something. It’s... the exhaustion of taking responsibility by choice.

It’s a bit strange, but it actually makes me feel more grounded.

# Work Log | 2022-11-23

**Meeting Minutes**
Afternoon team meeting to discuss the Q4 marketing plan. Xiao Li threw a question my way: "Mr. Shen, what do you define as success?"

I froze for a second. I was going to give the standard answer—KPIs, customer satisfaction, that sort of thing. But the words changed just as they hit my tongue.

I said, "The most important thing is who you spend your time with, and who you are responsible for."

Even I was a bit surprised by my own words. The conference room went quiet for a few seconds. Xiao Li nodded and didn't ask anything else.

---

**Personal Memo**
That answer didn't really sound like me. Or rather, it sounded too much like the real me.

I’ve been thinking about things lately. About value, about choices. During that trip home last month, I sat by my mother’s bedside for an entire afternoon. She was asleep, and I just sat there, watching her breathe. What was I thinking about at that moment? It wasn't next month’s sales targets, nor was it my next golf game.

I was thinking: if one day I am the one lying in that bed, who do I want by my side?

I’ve spent all these years talking so much at business dinners, building all this "networking." But what about when I’m truly needed?

It’s not that work isn't important. It’s just that I seem to have had my priorities backward all along.

---

**To-Do**
- Finalize Q4 plan details
- Visit Mom this Friday
- ~~Next Wednesday’s golf game~~ (Rescheduled)

I’m a bit tired. But it’s a different kind of tired.

It took me a long time before I finally opened WeChat.

The screen was cluttered with unread messages—an invitation from my golf buddy Old Wang, social dinner gatherings, even idle gossip from colleagues. I didn't reply to any of them.

My finger hovered over the screen for a long time. Finally, I found a name—an old classmate from university who now lives in another city. We’ve occasionally interacted on Moments over the years, but we aren't close. I don't know why, but I just felt like sending him a message.

I typed a few words, then deleted them. Typed again, deleted again.

In the end, I only sent one sentence:

*I’m just so tired.*

After hitting send, I didn't wait for a reply. I didn't even look at the screen again. I tossed the phone aside and leaned back against the sofa, the ceiling light stinging my eyes.

Downstairs, people were still talking—voices from very, very far away. Wang Xiaomei was in her room; my daughter was in hers. We were each in our own space, like three strangers living in the same house.

I closed my eyes.

My mother’s recovery is still the same. My daughter’s grades are still the same. Work is still the same. Nothing is going to change. And there’s nothing I can do to change it.

I’ve given up.

Lin, I had a long talk with Xiaomei today. She mentioned that I haven't been as restless lately and that I’ve finally started to truly be there for them. Hearing that made me realize that all these years, I’ve been constantly running, never really stopping to look at the people right beside me.

You’ve been back home taking care of Mom for so long, yet I’ve always just rushed back for quick visits, treating them like tasks to be checked off a list. It only clicked for me today that I’ve had the wrong idea about what truly matters. It’s not about how much money I make out there or how many connections I have at dinners—it’s about my presence and my responsibility to all of you.

You’ve worked so hard all these years. Truly. I used to think that as long as I provided financial support, I was doing my duty. But seeing you care for Mom every day has made me realize just how much weight "being there" actually carries.

I don’t want things to just stay the same. When I come back next month, I want to stay for a few extra days and take care of Mom with you. We can figure out together how to make her recovery more comfortable. I don’t want you to have to shoulder all of this alone anymore.

Thank you, Lin. I mean it.

Xiaomei, I drove out to the countryside with Xinyu this afternoon.

She spent the whole time telling me about her painting and that art competition. I only just realized she’s been thinking about applying to art school; she hadn't really mentioned it to us before.

I just sat there and listened without interrupting. She was so earnest, and there was this spark in her eyes. When she talked about wanting to study illustration and visual design, I could really feel that it wasn't just some passing whim.

It made me think that maybe we’ve been so busy pushing her about her grades and "being realistic" that we haven't actually listened to what she truly wants.

She also said something else—that she doesn't want me to give up either. That’s been on my mind ever since.

I feel like we might need to sit down and have a real talk about her plans for the future. Not the usual "you need to study hard for college" kind of pressure, but a genuine effort to understand what she’s after. If art school is her goal, we should be helping her figure out how to get there, rather than trying to talk her out of it.

What do you think?

August 14, 2022, 9:30 PM

Sitting in the study, I can still hear the cicadas chirping downstairs. Xinyu has gone back to her room, and Xiaomei is in the living room watching her teaching videos. I intended to open my work emails, but ended up opening this long-neglected notebook instead.

I don’t know when I stopped writing properly.

While chatting with Xinyu in the car tonight, she said something that I’m still turning over in my mind: "It takes more courage to accept your imperfections than to try and change everything." Words coming from a sixteen-year-old girl. I didn't respond at the time; I just listened quietly as she went on about a book she’s been reading, and how the characters learned to let go of their obsessions.

I realized what I’ve been doing all these years.

Grilling her at the dinner table to get her grades up, attending three or four business dinners a week just to maintain my image at the company, talking shop on the golf course just to prove I’m still relevant... none of it was for anything real. It was all an escape. An escape from the fact that I am, in reality, just a mediocre middle-aged man.

Today, I didn't ask about her exam results. I just listened. I listened to her describe how the protagonist slowly accepted that she would never be a genius, and how she found a certain peace in that acceptance. As I listened, my eyes grew a little damp.

Pathetic, isn't it? As a father, I heard the very words I needed to hear most from my own daughter.

The pressure is still there. My mother’s medical bills are still there. The glass ceiling at work is still there. None of that has changed. But it feels as if something has loosened. I no longer feel like I have to change everything just to prove myself. Maybe things are fine just as they are. Maybe failure isn't something to be ashamed of.

Xiaomei just asked what I was doing in the study, and I told her I was just thinking. She didn't push further—that’s been our way of interacting lately. But today, it suddenly occurred to me that maybe I should have a real talk with her. Not to solve any problems, but just... to talk.

Xinyu’s words are still echoing in my head.

Maybe courage isn't about changing everything. Maybe courage is stopping, admitting you’re tired, admitting you’re imperfect, and then simply carrying on.

I think I’m finally starting to understand.

I’m on the high-speed train. The signal was bad just now, so I only just saw your message.

I’ve already requested time off from my superiors. I’ll be heading out Friday afternoon and coming back Sunday night. That way, I can spend two days a week back in my hometown. I called Sister Li; she said Mom’s been doing okay lately, though her legs have been hurting quite badly some days.

I cancelled this week’s game. My teammates were all understanding—they said if something urgent comes up, that takes priority.

(A long pause)

I understood what you meant by that look in your eyes the other day. I know this can’t be put off any longer. Linlin is definitely under too much pressure being there all by herself. Before, I was... never mind, I was just avoiding it. Going once a month made me feel like I was doing my best, but in reality, it didn't solve anything.

Can we talk more when I get home? I’m a bit tired right now, and my head is a mess.

By the way, have you had dinner yet?

# Mom’s Persistence

**Album created on November 15, 2022**

---

**Photo 1 | Rehab Center Hallway**
Mom is holding onto the parallel bars, moving forward one step at a time. Sister Li is by her side, supporting her waist. The light was a bit dim, and I took this from a corner, so it’s a little blurry. But you can see the way Mom is gritting her teeth and the focus in her eyes.

I took this this morning. She said her legs hurt, but she insisted on practicing anyway.

---

**Photo 2 | Mom’s Hands**
A close-up. Her hands are gripping the bars, the joints a bit swollen. Her nails are trimmed very short—Sister Li probably did that for her. I didn’t plan on taking this; it just suddenly hit me... these hands have spent so many years doing everything for us, and now they are learning how to stand back up.

The composition isn't great, but I like this one.

---

**Photo 3 | In Front of the Rehab Room Mirror**
Mom is looking at herself in the mirror. She’s doing leg lifts, very, very slowly. You can see her furrowed brow in the reflection. Sister Li is beside her, counting the reps.

I asked Mom if I could take a photo, and she said okay. But I could tell she was a bit self-conscious. I told her it wasn’t to show anyone else, just... to keep a record. Only then did she relax a little.

---

**Photo 4 | Mom and Sister Li**
Mom is sitting in a chair while Sister Li massages her legs. Mom’s eyes are closed, her expression relaxed. Out of all the photos I’ve taken since I’ve been back, this is the one where she looks the most comfortable.

Sister Li is truly professional. Every time I visit, I can see that Mom is making progress.

---

**Photo 5 | Sunlight Outside the Window**
The trees outside the rehab center window. I took this while Mom was resting by the window. She said she wanted to see outside but couldn't walk that far. So I... took a photo of the view for her.

Later, when we looked at the photo together, she said the trees looked paler than last year. I didn't expect her to notice things like that.

---

**Photo 6 | Mom’s Smile**
She was drinking water when she suddenly turned and smiled at me. I didn't think twice; I just pressed the shutter.

This is the most natural one in the set. No posing, nothing forced. Just... Mom.

---

**Album Description:**

At first, I thought about just using my phone. But then I remembered the DSLR I bought back in 2018 that’s been gathering dust in the cabinet. So I pulled it out.

The lens is a bit old, and the autofocus isn't particularly fast, but... the feeling of the photos is just different. Maybe it’s because I’m actually looking through the lens, rather than just snapping a quick shot.

None of these photos are perfect. The lighting is less than ideal, and some of the compositions are pretty amateur. But this is what reality looks like. Every day Mom spends in the rehab center is like this—imperfect, but persistent.

I didn't post these to WeChat Moments. I never intended to.

This album is just for myself. Every time I open it, I think about all those moments over the last two years when I was running away, and these moments now, staying by Mom’s side. The two feelings mix together; it’s a bit painful, but also... a bit grounding.

Mom says as long as she can still move, she’ll keep practicing.

I think I should do the same.

# June 28, 2022, 9:00 PM

Sitting in my study, the city lights outside the window are a blur. I just cried in the car.

I don’t know when it started, but I can’t even keep up the act at the bar anymore. Old Wang and the guys asked me to watch the game today, and I went anyway. Sitting in that familiar spot, with the beer, the skewers, and the familiar faces—everything was the same as always. But I realized I couldn't focus at all. The ball was rolling across the pitch, but my eyes were drifting. Old Wang asked me what was wrong, if work was going poorly. I forced a smile—the kind of strained smile where I could feel my face stiffening. They all saw through it. I saw them exchanging looks.

In that moment, I realized I couldn't play the part anymore.

This passionate Shen Linchuan, the one who always has something to say, who can make strangers laugh and remembers everyone’s name—now, sitting among close friends, I felt a sense of total void. What am I thinking? What am I running from?

Driving home, I suddenly broke down at a red light. I cried like a loser, gripping the steering wheel. I’m 42 years old; what am I still crying for? For my photography being rejected? For the department merger? For my mother’s health failing day by day back home? For the growing silence between Xiaomei and me? For Xinyu’s grades? Or for myself—this middle-aged man who has nothing left?

I thought my passion, my network, and my social skills could protect me. But now I see clearly—it was all an illusion. The final layer of skin I used to deceive myself has been punctured. My photography failed, my position has been sidelined, my mother’s condition is worsening, and at home, I’ve become a silent, anxious man. And there is nothing I can do.

Have I really failed?

Or is it that I was never successful to begin with—that I’ve just been numbing myself with smiles and alcohol all this time?

Now, even my friends at the bar can tell something is wrong. I can no longer play that character.

I don’t know how to face tomorrow.
