August 15, 2022, 11:30 PM

I dug up those photos I deleted last year.

I originally intended to look through them again just to keep putting myself down—that’s been my habit lately. But this time was different. Looking at those shots of the old neighborhoods, I didn't immediately think "this isn't good enough" or "I'm not as good as those younger photographers." Instead, I thought... we just see the world differently.

They might see light, shadow, and composition. I see the sense of time in that street, a certain afternoon I spent walking there, something I can't quite put into words. It’s not better or worse, it’s just... different.

Why do I feel the need to compare myself to them?

This thought caught me by surprise. It wasn't that fake self-consolation that follows self-deprecation; it was a genuine... letting go of something.

I lay in bed for a long time tonight. Wang Xiaomei is already asleep. In the dark, I wondered what I’ve been waiting for all these days. Waiting for work to pick up? Waiting for Xinyu’s grades to improve? Waiting for Mom to be able to walk again? Waiting for myself to suddenly become someone else?

Probably none of that will happen. At least not in the short term.

But... I realized I’m not as desperate anymore. It’s not because things on the outside have changed, but because the way I look at them is changing. It sounds like some cliché "chicken soup for the soul," but in the middle of the night, it feels real.

This peace isn't numbness. Numbness is feeling nothing at all. In this peace, I can still feel things, it’s just... they aren't as sharp. It’s like someone turned the volume down.

Maybe tomorrow will still be exhausting. Maybe tomorrow morning I’ll forget everything I’m thinking tonight. But at least for tonight, I’m thinking—maybe tomorrow will be better. Not because anything external will change, but because I am changing the way I look at my life.

It sounds like a small thing. But for me, it’s already a very big deal.

I should sleep. Work tomorrow.

June 2, 2022. Past 11 PM.

I’m sitting in the study. It’s been quiet downstairs for a while now. Xiaomei must be asleep, and the light in Xinyu’s room is off.

I don’t know how to write this. Or rather, I know what to write, but I don’t want to admit it.

During dinner, Xiaomei asked me again if I was sick. I said I was fine, just tired. She wanted to keep pressing, so I snapped a bit and told her to stop asking. Her face went cold instantly, and she turned toward the kitchen. I saw her shoulders—they seemed to be trembling—but I didn't say a word.

Then Xinyu asked if I was mad at her, if it was because of her grades.

When I heard that, I froze. I wanted to ruffle her hair, to tell her that Dad isn't angry, that Dad is just... I don't even know what Dad is. I started to reach out, but in the end, I let my hand drop. I just said, "Just eat your dinner," and walked away.

I really want to know: when did I become like this? I wasn't like this before. I used to be the person who could walk into any room and make people laugh. I remembered everyone’s name; I made people feel seen. But now? Now I’m hurting the people closest to me.

Xiaomei is asking if I’m sick; Xinyu is asking if I’m angry. They are both trying to get close to me, and I’m pushing them away like a wall. I don’t even realize what I’m doing until it’s already done.

I haven't told Xiaomei about the photography project yet. That young photographer’s work was selected, not mine. I spent three months on it, took hundreds of photos, and wrote a long creative brief. I thought this time would be different; I thought I could prove I still had the capacity for innovation. But the result was... nothing.

I’m 42. My career has no prospects, my family relationships are growing colder, and I can’t even pull off a photography project. What do I have left to prove my worth? My passion? My connections? All of that seems so laughable now.

I’ve been lying to myself. I thought I could live my whole life on social skills and enthusiasm, but it turns out that was an illusion. I have no real professional expertise, no real creativity, and I’ve never fully prepared for anything long-term. I’m like a balloon that was blown up, and now the air is slowly leaking out.

The scariest part is that I’m using this deflated state to hurt Xiaomei and Xinyu. They are reaching out, and I am pushing them away. I know this, yet I don’t seem to have the strength to change it.

I’m just sitting here in the dark, not knowing how to face them tomorrow.

June 4, 2022. Past 11 PM.

The train arrives in half an hour. I’ve been sitting here for over an hour now. Outside the window is nothing but pitch-black night. There are seven or eight unread work emails on my phone; I haven't opened a single one.

I haven't been able to get the image of my mother out of my head all day.

She was sitting in that wheelchair at the rehab center, and I couldn't understand half of what she was saying. Her right arm just hung there, limp, like it didn't even belong to her. Nurse Li said she needs "more intensive rehabilitation"—I know what that means. It means she isn't getting better. It means my once-a-month visits aren't changing a thing.

When I held her hand, she looked at me with something in her eyes I couldn't quite read. Was it disappointment? Maybe she knows I’m powerless, too.

Lin said she can’t hold on much longer. She used a tone of exhaustion I’ve never heard from her before. "Bro, I’m really hitting a wall. Mom needs 24-hour care now, and my job..." She didn't finish the sentence, but I understood. She was crying out for help. And what can I give her? I’m hitting a wall, too.

Sitting on this train, there’s just one thought circling my mind: Have I actually failed?

Not that occasional flicker of self-doubt. But real, total failure.

Professionally—my photography project was rejected. The project I thought would prove I still had creativity, that I wasn't obsolete yet, was beaten out by a photographer ten years younger than me. All that time spent learning and shooting, and for what? To be a runner-up. It punctured something, and I’m only realizing it now—I’ve been lying to myself. I thought my social skills, my passion, and my network would protect me, would make me successful. But those things are worthless when real professional mastery is required. I don't have a real skill. I never did.

At home—Xiaomei and I have nothing left to say to each other. During dinner, I started spacing out thinking about Xinyu’s grades. She asked what was on my mind; I said "nothing," and then we both just went back to our phones. My daughter is a high school freshman, her grades are still mediocre, and I’m caught between wanting to encourage her and being afraid of putting too much pressure on her. The result is I’ve done nothing right. My mother is bedridden back home, Lin is on the verge of a breakdown, and me? I’m just sitting here, useless.

Physically—I’m 42. I can feel myself declining. It’s not just physical stamina. It’s the whole of me.

I used to think I was a social butterfly. I could walk into any room and be the life of the party. Now I see that wasn't a talent; it was just a hollow shell. Now that the shell has cracked, there’s nothing inside. No deep interests, no real professional foundation, no long-term preparation for anything. I’m just a middle-aged man scraping by on enthusiasm and connections, and those things are losing their currency.

I’m still smiling at the office. I’m still telling jokes at business dinners. But that’s not me. That’s a mask. The real me is sitting on this train, staring into the darkness outside, asking myself: What value do I have left?

Have I really failed?

Or... have I always been this way?

I don't know.

I really don't know.

February 18, 2023, 9:30 PM

I had a long talk with Xiaomei tonight.

After dinner, Xinyu went to her room to draw. We sat on the sofa, and for once, we didn't just scroll through our phones. She suddenly said, "You’ve changed lately." I felt a flicker of tension, bracing myself for a critique. But she continued, "You’ve become more real. You’ve stopped trying to mask everything with jokes. And the way you interact with Xinyu is different now."

I didn't answer right away. I just sat there, feeling something loosen in my chest.

Then, I told her things I hadn't properly voiced in a long time. I said, "I realized I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places. Those business dinners, those networks, those titles… I thought that was where my value lay. But now I see that my value is actually right here—in these moments with you both."

She didn't say anything, but her eyes welled up.

Later, we talked about Xinyu’s college entrance exams, Mom’s recovery, and the pressures at home. These were topics we used to avoid, but tonight, we didn't. The conversation was honest and warm. We weren't just trying to solve problems; we were facing them together.

To be honest, I don’t know exactly when this shift began. Maybe it started during those nights last year back in my hometown, staying with Mom. Sitting in her room, watching her peaceful face while she slept, it suddenly hit me—life isn't a curve that just keeps going up. I’m no longer that young man on a career fast track, but that doesn’t mean everything is over.

I’m sleeping much better now. That feeling of waking up in the middle of the night with a heavy, suffocating tightness in my chest is mostly gone.

Xiaomei said I’ve "become more real." I think that’s just what it feels like to accept yourself. I no longer need to be the energetic, talkative, well-connected "Mr. Shen" in every situation. I can just be Shen Linchuan—an ordinary middle-aged man, a bit tired, with a few regrets, but with so much worth holding onto.

I have a meeting at the office tomorrow. But tonight, I just want to remember this moment.

**Aug 14, 7:00 PM**

I was sitting next to Xiaomei just now while she was on her phone. It suddenly hit me—I haven’t truly listened to her in a long time.

I asked her how school was today. She looked startled for a second, then started telling me about one of her students. I didn’t interrupt. I didn’t look at my phone. I just listened.

As she kept talking, I finally realized—she’s under pressure too. About Xinyu, and about the fact that I’m rarely home. I always thought I was the only one suffering.

**Things to remember:**
- Stop being so cold.
- Listen to them more.
- Go driving with Xinyu this weekend; don’t be distracted.
- Next time I visit Mom back home, don’t just sit there staring into space.

I’ve already lost too much. I can’t lose the two people in this house, too.

**Starting tomorrow. Starting tomorrow.**

No. Starting tonight.

Xiaomei, I’m still at the office.

I sat here by myself for a while just now, but my head is still a bit of a mess.

To be honest, I even stunned myself with what I said at the annual meeting today. Did you see the video I sent? I watched it once and felt a little embarrassed—it’s really not my usual style.

But truthfully, after saying it, I felt a sense of relief. It was like something I’d been holding in for a long time finally came out.

I’ve been thinking, have we been chasing the wrong things all these years? I tried so hard to be the center of attention at every dinner party, to make my subordinates think I’m impressive, and to figure out how to get the next promotion or raise. But what about you? You spend every day at school grading papers and talking with students, then come home and worry about Xinyu’s schoolwork. I never truly looked at what you were doing.

It wasn't until recently, while helping Mom with her recovery, that I finally understood—the things that actually matter aren't talked into existence on a golf course. It’s companionship. It’s responsibility. It’s exactly what you’ve been doing all along.

I feel like I’m only just beginning to understand you.

I don’t know exactly how to change yet, but I want to change something. I’m not talking about anything extreme like quitting my job tomorrow, but... I want to rethink what it is we’re actually after.

Are you free? Can we have a real talk tonight? Not about our daughter’s grades or who’s buying the groceries—just the two of us.

I need to hear what you think.

# Memo

**Date:** August 13, 2022, just past 11:00 PM
**Location:** Bedroom, lying in bed

---

I need to write this down. I’m afraid I’ll forget.

What Xinyu said today: "Dad, what I need is a father, not a person full of anxiety."

Just that one sentence.

We were in the car, and I had started asking her again about how her revision was going and if her class ranking had improved. She suddenly said that. Her voice was calm, but I could tell she was exhausted.

I didn't respond at the time. I just gripped the steering wheel, unable to say a word.

Later, I asked her why she said that. She said, "Dad, every time you look at me, your eyes are full of worry. I can feel it. But that just makes me even more nervous."

I wanted to defend myself, but I didn't. Because she was right.

I always thought I was encouraging her, but in reality, I’ve been projecting my own fears onto her. I’m afraid she won’t do well on her exams; I’m afraid for her future; I’m afraid that I don’t have the ability to help her. So every time I look at her, that’s the look in my eyes—one filled with my own anxiety.

She’s only sixteen. She doesn't need a father who is constantly anxious.

That sentence is stuck in my heart.

I need to think about how to change. Or rather, I need to think about what exactly I’m so anxious about. Is it her grades? Or is it my own sense of powerlessness?

Write it down. Don’t forget.

# Work Log - December 18, 2022

**8:30 AM**

Just received an email from the Head of Sales. The company wants me to lead a new project—integrating the online and offline customer experience processes. It’s not a promotion, just a new project lead role.

My first reaction, I’ll admit, was a bit of disappointment. I sat in my office staring at the email for five minutes, thoughts racing: *It’s not a promotion. I’m still in the same spot.* Then I thought back to the department merger in 2021, and a wave of frustration hit me.

But I didn’t reply immediately. Over the past two years, I seem to have picked up a new habit—pausing first.

I spent half an hour thinking it over while having my coffee.

What came to mind wasn't "Will this project help me get promoted?" but rather—what is the essence of this project? What can I do differently here?

The old me would have taken on a project solely for the results. Quick wins, impressive data, and a polished presentation at the meeting. Now... I don't know when my mindset started to shift. Maybe it’s from seeing how Sister Li cares for patients at my mother’s rehab center—not for any specific outcome, but because she truly cares. Or maybe it’s because of what Xinyu told me recently about her thoughts on painting; I realized that not everything has to be about "success."

So, I decided to take on the project. But in a new way.

I just replied to the email, writing:

"Thank you for your trust. I would like to approach this project in a new way—focusing more on quality and team development rather than just speed and results. I will be setting new working standards, and I hope we can make this something truly meaningful together."

I felt a bit nervous as I hit send. It felt like I was making a declaration of some kind.

The new working standards are as follows:
- Quality over speed. No rushing; give myself and the team time to think.
- Focus on team growth, not just project outcomes. What everyone learns during the process is more important than the final numbers.
- Protect family time. This is the bottom line. No working until midnight, no being dragged back to the office on weekends.

To be honest, I don’t know if this is the "right" move. I might be seen as not being proactive enough. The project might move slower than others. But right now, I seem to care more about *what* I am doing rather than *how* others perceive it.

It feels a bit strange. But good.

Heading back to my hometown to see Mom tonight. Leaving early tomorrow morning. I don’t want my time this weekend to be disrupted by work again.

---

**11:15 PM**

Adding a quick note from the high-speed train.

Had a brief meeting with the team this afternoon to talk about the new project. I was a bit worried they’d find me strange—suddenly talking about "quality" and "growth."

But surprisingly, a younger colleague (Xiao Zhang, who joined last year) asked: "Mr. Shen, does this mean we’ll have more time to understand the customers' real needs instead of rushing to churn out reports?"

I said yes.

He nodded, looking somewhat relieved.

I realized then that maybe I’m not the only one who’s exhausted. Maybe they are also tired of this "fast, fast, fast" pace.

This project really could be different.

Not because it will get me promoted or make me famous, but because—we can try a different way of living.

I’m a bit tired, but it feels like I’m doing the right thing.

March 12, 2022, 2:00 AM

Closed the browser.

Sitting here in the dark study, my hand still resting on the mouse. I’ve been scrolling through recruitment sites for nearly an hour—Marketing Director, Regional Manager, Business Development... scanning one position after another: salary, location, requirements... and then, a sudden wave of exhaustion hit me. A deep, heavy exhaustion.

So I shut it down.

What was it I just said to myself? Something like, "Maybe I don't need a new job; maybe what I need is a new way of looking at myself." When I said it, I felt a tiny spark of emotion, as if I’d stumbled upon some great secret.

But that feeling was like a ripple on the water—a quick flick of the finger, and then it vanished.

Now I’m just sitting here in the dark, and nothing has changed.

What if I don't jump ship? What then? Stay at City Real Estate, walking on eggshells around my boss, drowning in an endless pile of tasks? And if I do jump? Where would I go? Who wants a 42-year-old middle-aged man with no real technical skills, someone whose only talent is talking big?

What am I even thinking?

My phone screen lit up—a sleep reminder from my wife. She must be asleep by now. Downstairs, my daughter’s room is dark too. She’s probably reading; she’s always been like that. I should go check on her, or at least ask how her schoolwork is going. But right now, I don't even have the strength to walk out of this room.

On the desk lies one of her award certificates from middle school. That was three years ago. Now her grades are just average. I want to encourage her, but I’m afraid of adding to her pressure, so in the end, I say nothing. That’s the kind of father I am.

Then there’s the video of Mom, recorded the last time I went back home. She’s sitting in her wheelchair, a forced smile on her face. I go back every month, and every time I feel utterly powerless. Medical bills, living expenses... my sister is there taking care of her, while I’m here losing sleep over my own pathetic problems.

Maybe I’m just not cut out for this.

Maybe I should have given up on those restless ideas a long time ago. Changing jobs? Changing myself? I have nowhere left to retreat. 42 years old, a mediocre middle-aged man, a Marketing Director with a painted-on smile. This is my endgame.

I have nowhere left to go.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. I have to smile. I have to talk big with clients and make small talk with subordinates, pretending everything is fine. Pretending I’m still that passionate Shen Linchuan.

But I am so, so tired.

March 2, 2022, 11:45 PM

I’m sitting in the car; the engine is still running. There are a few concerned messages from colleagues on my phone, but I haven't replied.

What exactly happened at dinner tonight? I can’t even tell.

It started off fine. I was doing what I always do—working the room, telling jokes, making toasts. But by the third round, the fourth round… I realized I didn't want to say another word. The jokes got stuck in my throat; I could feel them becoming stiff, fake. I was still laughing, but I couldn't hear my own voice.

After that, I just sat there with my glass, drink after drink. My colleagues kept chatting, and I sat there like a piece of wood. They asked me what was wrong; I said "nothing" and kept drinking. I could see the confusion in their eyes—something wasn't right with Mr. Shen tonight.

In the end, it was Old Wang who helped me to the car. I remember him saying, "You’ve had too much," and I didn't argue. Because I knew he wasn't talking about the alcohol.

When I got home, Xiaomei was in the living room. Seeing me like this, she asked, "What is actually going on with you lately?" Just that one sentence.

I said, "Nothing, just a bit tired from work," and went straight upstairs.

I heard her sigh from downstairs.

Now I’m sitting here, and my hands are still shaking. Forty-two years old. I’ve been in this industry for fifteen years, and for what? The department is being merged, my authority is shrinking, and the glass ceiling is right in front of me. I have no professional skills to be proud of. My hobbies—photography, golf—were all half-hearted; I never mastered anything. I’m just a social tool, a tool that knows how to tell jokes, remember names, and liven up a dinner party.

But the tool is broken now.

My daughter’s grades are the same, my mother’s legs are the same, and the money in my account is the same. What can I change? I can’t change anything.

I’ve been lying to myself, lying to everyone. I’m not some "success story." I’m just a 42-year-old man with no way out and no choices left. I used to think passion and networking could save me, but now I know they can’t.

I don’t have the strength to smile anymore.

But tomorrow, I still have to go to the office. I still have to smile.

I don’t know how much longer I can hold on.

# Phone Memo

**[New Life Plan] Starting Next Month**
Time: September 18, 2022, 8:15 PM

---

## Core Decision
Starting October 1st, cut down social dinners from 3 times a week to once a week. Dedicate the remaining time to my family and myself.

---

## Specific Action Steps

**Must-attend social events (Keep):**
- Monthly client maintenance dinners (once a week) — Can’t drop these; they directly impact performance.
- Annual major company events — Mandatory attendance.

**Events to decline (Phase out):**
- Last-minute drinking invitations — Learn to say, "Next time, I’ve got some family stuff to handle lately."
- Dinners with non-core friends — Not every meal is worth the time.
- Watching live matches — I can watch at home; I don't always have to go to the bar.

**Golf sessions:**
- Keep 1–2 times a month (this is genuine relaxation + high-value business).
- Politely decline other invitations.

---

## Time Reallocation

**Use the saved time (approx. 10–15 hours/week) for:**
1. **Accompanying Xinyu** — Drive to the suburbs on weekends. We don't even have to talk; just being there is enough. This period before her College Entrance Exam is critical; I can't be absent.
2. **Photography** — Go to the old districts on weekends to take photos. Actually learn it properly this time; no more giving up halfway.
3. **Proper conversations with Xiaomei** — Not the kind where we're both staring at our phones. Ask her how she’s doing; listen to what’s on her mind.
4. **Visiting Mom back home** — Not just a rushed trip. Quality time.

---

## Potential Challenges & Solutions

**Friends asking "Why aren't you coming out?":**
- Be direct: "I have some family matters to take care of. It’s not that I don’t want to come."
- No need to make up excuses; the truth is simplest.
- Suggest one-on-one meetups instead of large group dinners.

**Colleagues/Clients thinking I’m not "proactive" enough:**
- Monthly client dinners continue as usual; quality remains the same.
- Golf continues as usual; it’s more valuable anyway.
- For everything else, let them get used to it.

**Me missing the "buzz" of a crowd:**
- This is normal. But remember: that feeling is an addiction, not true happiness.
- I can relax alone at home; I don't have to be in a bar.
- Time with my daughter is likely more meaningful than any drinking session.

---

## Reminders to Self

**Why am I making this change?**

I’ve been running. Running fast, running busy, running so hard that no one can tell I’m actually exhausted.

But I’ve reached a point where I’m tired. And I’ve realized that I’ve dropped something very important along the way—my daughter, my wife, and even myself.

What have these social dinners given me? Noise, a sense of being needed, a network. But are these things really worth the time I’ve sacrificed with my family?

I’m not trying to completely change who I am. I’m still that person who loves socializing and talking. But I need to learn when to stop. When to say "no."

**This isn't giving up; it’s choosing again.**

---

## Execution Timeline

- **Before Sept 25**: Start politely declining non-core dinner invitations.
- **Oct 1**: Officially implement the new plan.
- **Every Sunday Night**: Reflect on whether I stuck to the plan this week.
- **One Month Later (Nov 1)**: Evaluate the results and see what needs adjusting.

---

**Final Word:**
Maybe I won't be perfect. Maybe there will be times I still crave that feeling of being surrounded by people. But this time, I want to try and see what real "presence" actually feels like.

December 15, 2022, 10:00 PM

I’m still trying to process what happened tonight.

After dinner, Xinyu went back to her room to draw, leaving Xiaomei and me on the sofa. I thought we’d just scroll through our phones like usual, but she suddenly asked if something was on my mind lately. I said no, and she just smiled—that kind of helpless, knowing smile. Then she said something I can still hear clearly: "You aren't so restless anymore. You’ve started to actually *be* here with us."

I froze for a second. Restless? I hadn't realized it was that obvious. But she was right; something really has changed in me lately. I told her, "My value comes more from my presence and my responsibility to you both." I surprised even myself when I said it, but it felt incredibly real.

Then we started talking. Really talking, not just the usual routine small talk. She told me how exhausted she’s been these past few years, worrying about Xinyu’s studies and managing everything at home alone. She said sometimes it felt like I wasn't even there. I just listened. I didn't try to defend myself, because it was the truth. I wasn't there—not mentally, anyway.

We talked about Xinyu. Xiaomei is worried that her introverted personality will make things hard for her. I said something I never expected to say: "I realized I’ve been measuring Xinyu by my own standards. But she has her own rhythm." Only after saying it did I understand that all these years, my expectations for my daughter were actually just me looking for another version of myself.

In the end, we made a decision. Starting next week, we’ll have at least one deep conversation a week, just like tonight. Not to solve problems, but just to truly be with each other.

She’s asleep now, and I’m sitting in the study writing this. I’m tired, but it’s not that hollow kind of exhaustion. It’s... a meaningful tired?

I don’t know how long this change will last, but tonight, I felt something different. Maybe this is what I’ve been looking for all along.

No longer the Shen Linchuan who commands the room at a bar, but... a real husband, a real father.

Thinking about it that way, it feels pretty good.

11:47 PM

I... I really can't hold it together anymore.

I'm sorry.

Can we... can we just sit down and have a real talk?

I’m not looking for excuses. I know I’ve been a mess lately—I haven't been performing at work, and I haven't handled things at home well. But I really...

# Work Log - August 25, 2022

**Meeting Minutes: Marketing Department Weekly Meeting**

Time: 10:00 AM – 11:30 AM
Attendees: All Marketing Department staff, Zhang Tao (Sales Department Representative)

---

## Meeting Summary

Discussed the Q4 promotion plan for the new residential development. Zhang Tao proposed an idea: leveraging the educational resources surrounding the community as a key selling point, specifically targeting families with children.

In the past, my approach would have been to immediately point out the flaws in the plan (lack of data support, imprecise target audience) and then quickly provide my own solution for everyone to follow. It’s faster that way, and it demonstrates my expertise.

But today, I didn't do that.

Instead, I asked Zhang Tao a few questions:
- Why do you think educational resources will be the core competitive advantage for this property?
- Have you researched the actual needs of our target customers?
- If this direction is correct, how should we validate it?

He started thinking for himself and brought up things I hadn't considered—he mentioned his own experience choosing a school for his daughter, noting that many parents do indeed base their home-buying decisions on school districts. This gave him the confidence to further develop the idea.

Throughout the process, I didn't interrupt him or rush to provide answers. I simply asked a new question whenever he got stuck.

The result: Zhang Tao refined the logic of the proposal himself, and the team discussion became much more in-depth. The final execution plan was actually his idea plus a few supplementary points I suggested, rather than a passive execution of my own plan.

---

## Personal Reflection

This attempt felt... different.

In previous meetings, I was always "performing"—showing off how fast I could react, how comprehensive my thinking was, and how much control I had. My subordinates cooperated, but I wasn't sure if they truly agreed or were just following orders.

Today, I realized that when I slow down and truly listen, things become more vibrant. Zhang Tao wasn't being managed by me; he was thinking. This feeling...

It’s a bit like what Xinyu said to me in the car on the way back this afternoon. She saw right through me—that I’m always trying to "prove something." It was painful to hear at the time, but thinking about it now, she might be right.

I need to change some things. Not just my management style, but perhaps... something deeper.

I’ll continue with this approach in the next meeting and see if I can build a healthier team dynamic.

---

**Note:** Follow up on the progress of Zhang Tao’s proposal next week; prepare to report the new promotional strategy to upper management.

1:30 AM.

Can’t sleep. It’s that feeling again—tossing and turning, mind a cluttered mess. I finally just got up.

I’m sitting in the study, scrolling through photos on my phone. It’s been a long time since I’ve looked at them like this. Photos from 2015 after I bought the DSLR, and others taken on my phone over the last couple of years. Looking at them one by one, they feel almost foreign.

My daughter in the old district, wearing a red dress in front of a Republic-era building. That was last spring. I remember she didn’t really want to go that day; I had to drag her out the door. She looks a bit grumpy in the photo, but looking at it now, she actually looks lovely. I never really took the time to look at this one properly.

And then there’s Xiaomei. A photo from our wedding day. She’s laughing so happily, her eyes sparkling. When did we start just looking at our own phones? I can’t remember the exact day. It just happened slowly, bit by bit, until it became... this.

I started adding captions to these photos. Nothing poetic, just simple notes—"Xinyu, Spring 2021, she didn't want to go but went anyway," "Xiaomei, 1998, our beginning," "The day Mom was discharged, in her wheelchair but still smiling."

I stopped for a long time when I got to the photo of my mother.

Xiaomei suddenly appeared at the study door. She wasn't wearing a jacket, her hair was a bit messy, and her eyes were still heavy with sleep.

"What are you doing?"

"Organizing photos," I said, looking up. "I realized I’ve never really looked at them."

She didn't say anything. She just walked over and sat down beside me. Very close. Closer than we’ve been in a year or two. She took my hand.

Just like that. No extra words. But I felt something. Something I hadn't felt in a long time.

"Are you okay?" she asked.

I didn't answer right away. I was thinking about whether I actually was okay. I don’t know. But right now, at this moment, having someone ask me that, having someone sit by me and hold my hand—it makes me feel like maybe things aren't so bad.

"I’m trying to see things clearly," I said. "About myself. About what I’ve been doing all this time."

She didn't press for more. She just sat there, continuing to hold my hand.

We sat like that for a long time. In silence.

Maybe this is what change looks like. Not some grand resolution or plan, but just... stopping. Taking a real look. Admitting I’ve been running away. And then... moving forward. But this time, for real.

Maybe.

I’m still thinking. I’ll probably keep thinking for a long time.

But at least for now, I’ve stopped pretending I have the answers.

# October 13, 2022, around 7:00 PM

Sitting in the car, parked downstairs. I don’t want to go up.

I just got back from my hometown, and my mother’s face is still right there in front of me. I can’t quite put the feeling into words. It’s probably... the feeling of watching someone slowly vanish. She was sitting in her wheelchair, her eyes still clear, but her body... I don’t know how to describe it. Linlin said she’s lost more weight lately, and looking at her, it’s true. And all this time, visiting once a month, I actually thought I was doing my duty.

Mom smiled when she recognized me, but that smile only made me feel worse.

Linlin cried. In the kitchen, she told me, "I really can't hold on much longer." I’ve never heard her say that before. We sat there as she cried and told me about Mom’s recent situation—when she eats, when she needs help, how she often can’t sleep at night... As I listened, I realized I knew absolutely nothing about any of it. When I visited before, I’d just sit with Mom for a bit, chat about nothing in particular, and then leave. I thought that was enough.

I told Linlin I was sorry. My voice cracked; I didn’t expect that to happen.

She said, "It’s hard for you too." That made me feel even worse. She’s there carrying the whole burden alone, yet she’s the one comforting me.

On the drive back, I kept wondering what on earth I’ve been doing these past 15 years. Taking time off every month to go back felt like I was fulfilling an obligation. But in reality? In reality, I was escaping. I was avoiding seeing the true extent of my mother’s decline, avoiding the guilt I felt toward Linlin, and avoiding what I truly should be doing as a son.

I discussed it with Xiaomei and decided to go back twice a week from now on. She didn’t say much, but I could see a sense of "finally" in her eyes. I felt a certain pressure, a sense of being forced, but I also know it’s the right thing to do.

The problem is... I don’t know how to adjust. The business dinners, the golf, the friends... I can’t let go of those things. Or rather, I’m reluctant to let them go. But thinking about them now, it all feels so hollow.

When I’m the center of attention at a dinner party, I feel like I have value. But that kind of value... what does it mean compared to the tears in Linlin’s eyes?

I don’t know who I am anymore.

Or rather, I’m starting to doubt who I’ve been all along.

I have work tomorrow; I have to pretend everything is normal. But I really don’t want to move.

New desk, new perspective. Spent the whole morning getting settled into the new workspace and syncing with the sales team on upcoming projects. To be honest, the open-office layout took a little getting used to at first, but everyone’s been so welcoming and the vibe is great.

Got a client meeting this afternoon. Let’s keep the momentum going! 💪

Xiaomei, I’m at the office, and I wanted to talk to you about something that happened today.

During a team meeting just now, a young employee asked me what professional success means. Instead of giving the usual scripted answer, I paused for a moment and ended up talking about things like honesty, presence, and being real. The room went completely silent; one girl even started crying.

It was only after I finished that I realized those words were actually meant for you. And for myself.

Over these past few years, it feels like we’ve had less and less to say to each other. We’re always on our own phones, and while we’ve argued a few times over Xinyu, we haven’t truly talked. I’ve been thinking about it, and I think it’s because I’ve been so busy maintaining an image—the passionate director at work, and the husband who can handle everything at home. But the truth is, I’ve been feeling lost and exhausted too.

I finally understand now that I don’t have anything left to prove. What I want is to stay honest in my work, to truly be there for you and Xinyu at home, and to be authentic with myself and with both of you. That is what success looks like to me now.

I know this might seem a bit out of the blue, but I want to invite you to think about it with me—what is it that actually matters to us? Not the house, not Xinyu’s grades, not my job title... but what?

When we have dinner tonight, can we put our phones away and just really talk?

Linlin, I...

I'm sorry.

I know how exhausted you are over there. Mom’s situation, the medical bills, the caregiving... I know all of it. I originally planned to come back this month to help you out for a longer period, but things at the company... never mind, that’s no excuse.

I’m... I’m at my breaking point, too.

The boss called me out in front of everyone in the office this morning, saying I’ve been taking leave too often. I didn't even try to defend myself; I just stood there. My hands wouldn't stop shaking. All my colleagues were staring at me. I just said, "Okay, I'll be more careful," and walked back to my desk.

I sat there for half an hour, unable to do anything. Just staring at the screen.

Xinyu’s grades dropped again; she’s still just middle-of-the-pack. Wang Xiaomei has been on my back the last couple of days, saying I’m putting too much pressure on the kid. But... how am I pressuring her? I just want her to do a little better. I don’t have a choice.

And what about Mom? I go back every month, but every time I feel... I feel like nothing changes. She’s still the same. What can I do? I can’t change anything.

Linlin, I admit it. I’m a failure. I’ve failed in my career, and at home... I can’t protect anyone. Even you have to carry everything on your own over there. And me? I can’t even take care of myself.

I don’t know what to do anymore.

I really don't.

I'm sorry.

November 29, 2022, 9:00 PM

I just finished the dishes. Xinyu has already gone back to her room. Xiaomei is in the living room on her phone; I’m sitting next to her, but I haven't turned on the TV. I’m tired—the kind of tired where you can’t fall asleep.

During dinner, Xinyu asked me, "Dad, do you feel like you’re successful now?" I froze for a second. She was eating her rice, looking at me very earnestly. I didn't know how to answer.

To be honest, my first instinct was to say, "Successful? How am I successful?"—that’s always been my routine: be humble, then change the subject. But I didn't do that this time. I looked at her and suddenly realized she wasn't asking about my title at the company or how much money I make.

I said, "I don't know." Then I paused and added, "But what I do know is that I’m doing what I believe is right."

As soon as the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself. It didn't sound like something I would usually say.

After I said that, Xiaomei suddenly took my hand. She just held it, saying nothing. How long has it been since we’ve touched like this? Not a routine hand-hold, but a real... grasp. I could feel her hand was a bit cold, but firm.

I thought about our conversations over the years. Buying groceries, our daughter’s grades, who forgot what. Each of us staring at our own phones. The occasional argument over parenting, followed by a mutual silence. I’ve spent all this time looking for a sense of belonging at business dinners, while she stayed home cleaning up the mess I left behind.

Now, holding my hand, she said, "I’ve seen the change in you."

I didn't cry, but for a split second, I really felt like I might.

We talked for a while after that. I said I just need to learn how to find a new, more balanced lifestyle within the existing framework. She nodded and said that was enough.

But sitting here now, I’m still a bit uncertain. Can this change last? Have I truly let go of the craving for those dinners and social achievements, or is this just a temporary compromise? When my golf buddies call me next month, how will I answer?

I’ve always felt that my value came from how others saw me—how well I remembered people’s family members, how popular I was at dinner parties, how vast my network was. Now Xinyu is asking if I’m successful, and Xiaomei is holding my hand saying she sees my change... maybe success isn't that at all.

Maybe success is just showing up where you’re supposed to be and putting your heart into what you’re supposed to do.

But to be honest, I’ll still miss those moments. The feeling of being needed, being valued, being at the center of the crowd. I don't know if I’m truly ready to give it up.

Maybe I don't need to give it up entirely. Maybe I just need to learn when to put down the phone and when to hold my wife’s hand.

Work tomorrow. Mom’s follow-up appointment is next Wednesday. There’s still so much to do.

But tonight, I’m going to sleep.

# Diary

August 23, 2022, 9:30 PM

Sitting in the study. It’s dark outside. Xiaoyu is still in her room, and Wang Xiaomei is on her phone. I don’t know what to do.

A moment ago, the three of us were sitting together in the living room. I was about to turn on the TV like I always do, but Xiaomei suddenly asked me what’s been going on lately. I said, "Nothing." She just looked at me—that look... I can't explain it. Then Xiaoyu looked up at me too.

And I just... broke.

Not crying, exactly, just... suddenly unable to speak. I told them I’ve been running away. From what? I can’t even say for sure. My career, maybe, and things with my mother, and... I don’t know. I told them I’ve been shifting all that pressure onto Xiaoyu, using her grades to prove that I’m still "doing okay," to prove that I haven't wasted all these years.

Xiaomei’s eyes welled up. She said, "I’ve seen it for a long time."

Just that one sentence. I suddenly realized how long she’s been watching me. All these years, laughing at business dinners, talking shop on the golf course, yet running away whenever I was home. And she was always watching. Always waiting.

What was it Xiaoyu said then... she said I’m "always trying to prove something." A sixteen-year-old girl saw right through my forty-two years of life.

I don’t know what to do now.

I’ve always defined myself by my social skills. By my job title. By my daughter’s grades. What if I stop? What if I stop trying to prove things? Then who am I?

Maybe I just need to stop. But I don’t know what comes after stopping.

I’m scared.

But maybe... maybe this fear itself is what’s real. Maybe I can just sit here and admit that I’m exhausted, admit that I don’t have the answers, admit that I’ve messed a lot of things up.

Xiaomei is in the next room now. I want to go say something to her, but I don’t know what. I want to tell her I saw the tears in her eyes. I want to tell her I’m sorry. But those words feel like they’re coming too late.

Or... maybe it’s not too late?

I don’t know.

I’ll just sit here for now.

Xiaomei, are you there?

I wanted to talk to you about something. I was in the car with Xinyu just now, and she suddenly asked if I could go to an art exhibition with her. You know, she rarely ever asks me to do things with her. I was... a bit stunned.

Then it hit me—have I been putting too much pressure on her lately? Every time we eat, all I can think about are her grades, and I think it shows on my face. She’s so introverted and sensitive; she must have felt it.

I cried in the car. Right in front of her. I told her I was sorry.

I know I haven't really talked to you properly lately, either. We’re both always just on our phones, occasionally arguing about Xinyu, but we never actually sit down and talk. I’ve realized that I might be spreading my own anxiety to the whole family.

I don't know how to change things. To be honest, I’m not in a great place myself. Work is... never mind, that’s not important. What matters is that I want to try and do better. Not for any grand reason, just... I don’t want things to be like this anymore.

Are you free? Can we have a real talk tonight? Just the two of us. I want to hear what’s been on your mind lately.

# Diary

Saturday, January 28, 2023, 11:00 PM

I sat in the study for a long while just now, the lights off, just letting the darkness be. Wang Xiaomei knocked and asked if I wanted anything to drink. I said no. She didn’t say anything else, which was good.

That meeting today is still spinning in my head. This afternoon, Xiao Chen asked me what professional success meant. It was such a simple question, but I froze. Everyone in the conference room was looking at me; I could feel it.

I paused for a long time. Maybe ten seconds or so, but it felt much longer. What was I thinking about during those ten seconds? I was thinking about that night back in 2021 when the departments merged, and how I stayed silent at home for three days. I was thinking about the sense of helplessness I feel every month when I go back to my hometown to visit my mother. I was thinking about the way Xinyu looks lately when she’s painting in her room—she never asks me if she’s "good enough." I was thinking about why I’m still in this position—whether I’m truly content, or if I just lack the courage to change.

And then I said those words. "To be able to maintain honesty in your work, to maintain presence in your family, and to stay true to yourself. That is success."

The whole room went silent after I said it. I saw Xiao Chen’s eyes redden slightly. I don’t know what he was thinking. But in that moment, I knew I wasn't giving him a textbook answer; I was confirming something—confirming that I actually believe it.

These weren't words I said just to sound wise. These are words I have actually lived through this past year.

I used to spend all my time thinking about how to get promoted, how to look successful on social media, how to make people think I was someone of consequence. And now? Now, I go back to my hometown every month and sit in my mother’s room, watching her sleep. On weekends, sometimes I just drive around with Xinyu, no destination in mind. Xiaomei and I still spend time on our phones, but we’ve started to actually talk about things.

Two years ago, I probably wouldn't have considered any of this an achievement. But now I know that these are the things that are real.

I don’t know if this represents a permanent shift or if it’s just the phase I’m in right now. But at least tonight, I believe what I said. I wasn't pretending.

Work continues tomorrow, and I’ll have to keep dealing with all those things. But it feels different. It’s not a feeling of forced acceptance, but rather that something has truly been let go.

I think I can sleep a bit more soundly now.

# Memo

**Sept 6, 2022, 7:30 AM**

Another night of poor sleep. My dreams were a blur of Xinyu’s report cards and the image of Mom back at the rehab center.

I woke up around 3:00 AM and just sat in the living room. Xiaomei was still asleep; the light in Xinyu’s room was out. I looked at the unread work messages on my phone and suddenly... I felt tired. The kind of tired that goes bone-deep.

I opened my notes, wanting to write something down.

—

Maybe I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places.

What I can say, what I can do, how broad my network is... none of that matters. Truly.

My value isn't in what I can say, but in what I can see, and what I can accompany.

—

Last week in the car with Xinyu, she was staring out the window. I tried to say something to cheer her up, but she just said, "Dad, can you just be quiet for a moment?"

In that moment, something clicked.

I’ve been performing. Performing at business dinners, performing in the office, even performing at home. I thought my enthusiasm could solve everything, that it could make everyone comfortable. But actually... I was just running away.

Running from what?

Running from the fact that I don’t actually know what I want. Running from the possibility that I’m not as successful as I thought. Running from the fact that I’m getting older, and I haven't truly lived yet.

—

The last time I saw Mom, she held my hand and wouldn't let go. She couldn't speak, but I could see it in her eyes. She didn't need me to *do* anything; she just needed me to be there.

I spent fifteen years in real estate, yet I never truly spent time with the people right beside me.

—

Maybe I need to stop.

Not stop working, but stop this "performance."

Stop pretending I have all the answers.

Stop using enthusiasm to mask the emptiness.

Just... sit there. Watch. Be present.

Is that enough?

I don't know. But I’m starting to believe that this is what’s real.

September 28, 2022 | Evening

I said something on the court today. The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a flicker of regret, followed by a sense of relief.

Lao Wang asked if I’d been busy lately. I told him no, I’d just been thinking about some things. He said I seemed different—not as... "rushed" as I used to be. I paused for a second, and then it just came out: "I used to spend way too much time maintaining 'connections.' I actually missed out on a lot of things that truly matter."

I felt a bit nervous saying it, worried he’d think I was trying to be profound or that I was dismissing our friendship. But he didn't react like that. He just smiled and said, "I like the current you better."

It sounds like a simple enough sentence, but I sat in my car thinking about it for a long time. In the past, that feeling of being validated came from how successful I appeared, how wide my network was, or how much convenience I could provide for others. That kind of satisfaction was instant, like a hit of something sweet. But it was also hollow.

This feeling now is different. Lao Wang said he likes the way I am now because I’m not "performing." I’m just sitting there—sometimes silent, sometimes speaking the truth, sometimes admitting I don't know. This kind of validation... how should I put it? It carries more weight.

I’ve been taking a lot of photos lately. My daughter’s profile as she reads in her room, my wife’s back in the kitchen, even my mother sitting by the window at the rehab center. No one else sees these photos, but when I look at them, I feel a certain sense of fulfillment. This satisfaction doesn't need anyone to say, "Great shot." It just exists.

I’m starting to think that maybe I’ve been defining myself the wrong way all along. Not through the eyes of others, but through... I don’t know how to say it, just things that are real.

When I got home tonight, Xinyu was in the living room doing her homework. Instead of asking about her grades like I usually do, I asked her what was on her mind. She looked up at me and said, "Nothing much, just a bit tired." We just sat there, each doing our own thing, but not buried in our phones like we used to be. That kind of company—it felt like enough.

 I’m a little tired, but it’s not that anxious kind of tired. It’s a grounded kind of tired.

Tomorrow is another day.

# July 12, 2022, 10:45 PM

I’ve given up on worrying.

That’s what I told Xiaomei today. She asked me what was wrong, and that’s all I said. She didn't press further; she just gave me a look and went back to her phone. Maybe she’s given up, too.

The call came from Mom’s rehab center. They said her recovery hasn't been ideal this week and the mobility in her legs has declined again. I just listened, feeling nothing. Nurse Li was on the other end waiting for me to say something, so I just gave a muffled "mm-hmm" and hung up. I should have asked more questions, but I honestly didn't know what to ask. Asking wouldn't change a thing.

Xinyu’s monthly exam results came out—down another five points. Xiaomei brought it up at the dinner table, and I just kept my head down and ate. She probably wanted to discuss what to do, but I really didn't want to talk about it. Talking is useless. What can we even do? Push her? She’s already exhausted. Don't push her? She’s already anxious enough herself. So we just sat there, each eating our own meal like strangers.

My friends have been asking again these past couple of days when we’re going to play golf or when we can get together. I haven't replied. I don't know how to face them. They’ll ask how I’ve been, and what am I supposed to say? Tell them I have no real authority left at the company? Tell them my mom can barely walk? Tell them my daughter’s grades are slipping? Or should I keep pretending everything is fine and tell jokes with a smile? I’m just so tired.

I’m sitting in the living room, Xiaomei is in the bedroom, and Xinyu is upstairs. We’re all in the same house, yet it feels like we’re living on different planets.

Maybe it would be better if I didn't exist. I mean it. It’s not some melodramatic thought, it’s just... I truly feel that if I weren't here, Mom would have Lin to look after her, Xiaomei would have one less burden, and maybe the pressure on Xinyu would ease up. This "Marketing Director" title of mine is a joke now anyway; my subordinates stopped taking me seriously a long time ago.

I’ve given up on worrying. Because worry has given up on me.

I don't know what to do about tomorrow. But I don't want to know, either.

Monday, March 28, 2022. Night.

I’ve been sitting in the office for a long time. Everyone else has left. Half the lights are off; only the one above me is still on.

I just called Sister Li. There wasn’t really anything specific to discuss; I just wanted to make sure Mom is getting the best care possible. Sister Li said something on the phone that I’m still turning over in my mind.

She said, "Your mother mentions you all the time. She’s so proud of you."

I didn't say anything at the time. My voice caught in my throat. Sister Li probably noticed, but she didn't push it; we just sat in silence for a moment.

Proud? I keep wondering why Mom would be proud of me. I haven't achieved anything. My role at work has been downsized. Two years ago, I was thinking about jumping ship, but now even that thought is gone. My income is just average, my daughter’s grades are mediocre, and things at home are a mess. I even forget to buy groceries half the time.

But Mom says she’s proud of me.

Maybe... maybe I’ve been misunderstanding something all along.

For all these years, it feels like I’ve been trying to prove something. Proving my competence at the company, proving I’m doing well to my friends, proving I’m a responsible person at home. But the "pride" Mom talked about doesn't seem to be about any of that. She was talking about... me visiting her often? Me caring about her on the phone?

I promised I’d come home more often. As I said it, I realized how long it’s been since I’ve done anything with real heart.

Sitting here in this darkened office, I suddenly feel exhausted. Not a physical tiredness, but the kind of exhaustion that comes from... letting something go.

Maybe I don’t need to prove anything anymore. Maybe for a mother, having a son who comes home often, remembers her, and cares for her—maybe that’s enough. Maybe that is success.

I opened the diary in my drawer, wanting to write something down. My fingers rested on the pen for a long time.

In the end, I only wrote one sentence: Maybe I need to redefine what success means.

Then I closed the diary and sat in the dark, feeling a sense of relief. It’s not that anything has been solved; it’s just that... I’ve stopped fighting.

February 10th, past 11 PM

I can’t sleep. I’ve been lying here for over an hour, tossing and turning, but that job posting is still stuck in my head.

This afternoon at the office, when no one was around, I opened Lagou. I don’t even know why, but I searched for "Marketing." I saw a Marketing Manager position at an e-commerce company. Annual salary: 300k. I’m at 450k right now.

I clicked on it. Read through the job description. And then I just sat there, my cursor hovering over the "Edit Resume" button. It stayed there for several minutes. I could feel my hand shaking.

I didn't click it. I closed the page.

Now, lying in bed, I’m still thinking about it. Why couldn’t I click it? It’s only a 15% pay cut, and at least it’s still a manager level. But I just… I can’t accept it. I’m a Marketing Director. How can I go back to being a manager?

But I also know there’s no future for me in my current position. My authority has been stripped, the budget has been slashed—what am I supposed to do? Keep pretending everything is fine? Keep organizing team-building events, picking up the tab, and laughing radiantly at business dinners?

What exactly am I afraid of? I’m afraid of the demotion. Afraid of admitting I failed. Afraid of having to start over at 42. Afraid that even if I go to a new company, I’ll be stuck as a manager and never make it back to Director.

Or maybe I was never cut out to be a Director in the first place?

I always thought my strengths were my network, my social savvy, my ability to handle people. But now? Without the authority, are those strengths even worth anything? What else do I have? Do I have any actual professional skills?

Wang Xiaomei is asleep. Her breathing is steady beside me; everything is so peaceful. I wish I could just drift off like that. But my mind won’t stop racing.

I have to go to the office again tomorrow. I have to smile. I have to pretend everything is under control.

I am so tired.

What she sees isn't what I see. I’ve walked down that street countless times and never noticed that flowerpot. While she was capturing it on her phone, I was standing right next to her watching a live ball game.

Xinyu said some things to me recently. I only just realized how much pressure I’ve been putting on her. I’ve been feeding my own anxiety, and she’s the one paying the price for it. When I think about that, I just...

Never mind. I don’t know how to express it. I just wanted to tell you that maybe I need to change some things. Not work-related things—me.

When do you get off work? Maybe we could have a real talk. Not the kind where we’re both staring at our phones, but a real conversation.

I know this sounds a bit sudden. But I feel like I owe you and Xinyu a real person, not someone who’s always putting on a performance.

Xiaomei, do you have a moment? I’d like to talk to you about something.

Actually, it’s about something that happened at the office this afternoon. A young employee asked me what success means. I gave him an answer without thinking much about it, but after he left, I sat alone in my office for a long time.

I told him that the most important thing isn't your network, your title, or how impressive you look at business dinners. It’s about who you spend your time with and who you are responsible for. I said that what I value most is being there for my family when they need me.

It sounded so right when I said it, but looking back now, I realize I haven't actually lived up to that over the years.

Do you remember? We’ve spent less and less time just sitting together lately. I’ve been so busy socializing and maintaining connections, and when I finally get home, we both just stare at our phones. When it comes to Xinyu, we always end up arguing, but we’ve never truly sat down to have a real conversation. I don’t even know what she’s been drawing lately or what new books she’s into.

I’m not saying work isn’t important, it’s just… I suddenly realized that I’ve been using "being busy" as an escape. An escape from communicating with you, from being there for Xinyu, and from facing the fact that Mom is getting older. I’ve been trying to fill those gaps with a sense of achievement and validation from friends, but the truth is, nothing is actually being filled.

My head is a bit of a mess right now. Part of me still wants to keep living this way because it’s familiar and comfortable. But another part of me feels like something is fundamentally wrong.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this? Or do you feel the same way?

Do you think we should have a real talk? Not to argue, but just… to really talk.

I’ve only recently come to realize that companionship is the most enduring form of love.

It’s not about grand gestures; it’s about just sitting together—one reading, the other daydreaming, with the occasional bit of small talk. Sometimes we say nothing at all; we’re just there.

Moments like these don't seem like anything special at the time, but looking back, it feels like these ordinary moments are what life is truly made of.

I used to think I had to achieve something big, but now I realize that simply being there for the person beside me is more than enough.

Goodnight, everyone.

# Tuesday, March 22, 2022 – Sunny

While driving this afternoon, I think I finally heard what Xinyu was truly trying to say.

Every time we’ve driven together before, I’ve been preoccupied with how to break the silence—playing music, asking about school, even trying to tell some stale old jokes. But she would just stare quietly out the window, and I could feel her waiting for me to stop. It made me feel a bit miserable, like I was failing as a father.

But today was different. I didn’t try to fill the void; I just drove. Suddenly, she started talking on her own about a series she’s been drawing lately—something about dreams. Her eyes lit up, and she traced compositions in the air with her hands. I’ve never heard her speak like that before.

I don’t know why, but in that moment, the words just slipped out: "Xinyu, I had dreams when I was young, too, but I gave up on them later. I don’t want the same thing to happen to you."

The moment I said it, I regretted it. It was too heavy; not the kind of thing you should say to a tenth-grade girl on a suburban road in the middle of the afternoon. But she didn’t turn around. She just kept looking out the window and said very softly, "Dad, I won’t give up. And I don’t want you to give up, either."

My hands gave a little tremor on the steering wheel.

I don’t know what she meant. Does she know what’s going on at the company? Can she see how exhausted I’ve been lately? Or was it just a daughter’s simple tenderness toward her father?

But it doesn’t matter. What matters is that in that moment, I felt seen. Not as Shen Linchuan, the high-energy Marketing Director; not as the man with the plastered-on smile at business dinners; but as the real, weary, regretful me.

I didn't say another word on the drive back, but my mind was a mess. Something I haven't felt in a long time was churning inside me—not anxiety, nor that hollow sense of resignation. It felt like... hope? Or maybe just the emotion of a father being encouraged by his daughter?

I’m not sure.

But I’m thinking that maybe it’s not too late for a change. Maybe I shouldn't just give up like this. Maybe...

I haven't figured it out yet.

Work continues tomorrow, as do the social obligations and the meetings. But this afternoon, it feels as though I’ve been reminded of something.

I don’t know how long this feeling will last.

[Image: A golf course with sun-drenched green fairways and the city skyline in the distance]

Rarely do we get such perfect weather. Had a great round with the guys today.

The highlight was when Old Wang joked that I’m "turning into a total family man." Instead of brushing it off with a joke like I used to, I looked him in the eye and told him straight—I don’t see it as a loss; I see it as a gain.

Over the last couple of years, I’ve slowly come to realize something. I used to think life was all about climbing the ladder, being seen, and constantly proving myself. But now, since I’ve stopped fighting with myself, everything feels so much lighter.

The biggest change lately is being able to truly show up for my family. Taking my daughter for a drive on the weekends without worrying about the next business dinner, or visiting my mom back home without that nagging sense of anxiety. That’s a feeling I never once found in the office.

I guess this is what people mean by "finding your peace." 😄

May 27, 2022. Past 11 PM.

The lights are off. I’m sitting in the study. My wife is asleep, and the light in my daughter’s room is out. It’s quiet downstairs.

I deleted all the photos.

I was still smiling during that meeting this morning. "This is the right choice; a fresh perspective is important"—my hands were shaking as I heard myself say those words. My colleagues must have noticed. I could feel their eyes on me. That look of pity.

At noon, I hid in the restroom and went through them one by one. My photos, and the photos taken by that 25-year-old kid. I looked at them for a long time. He really is good. Full of energy, imaginative, possessing that... I don’t know how to put it, just something *alive*. And my photos? Stiff. Mediocre. Lifeless.

Just like me.

I deleted them one by one. Watching them vanish from my phone, what was I supposed to feel? Regret? Anger? Nothing. Just empty. So empty.

My wife asked how my day was. I said it was fine. She didn't ask further. We haven't had a real conversation in a long time. She looks at her phone, I look at mine. This is our life.

I wrote a sentence in my Notes app and then deleted it. Now it’s come back to me: "I am no longer someone capable of innovation."

Maybe I never was.

When I bought that DSLR back in 2015, I thought I’d become a photographer. And then? It just became something I did casually on weekends, and most of the time I just used my phone anyway. I thought it was a "passion," but it was just self-deception. Just like how I overestimated my own talent.

I’m 42. My career has no future—that department merger in 2021 was proof enough. I’ve hit the ceiling. Things at home are a mess, too. My mother’s health is failing, and going back once a month doesn't change a thing. My daughter’s college entrance exams are coming up, and her grades are still just average. My sister is back in our hometown taking care of Mom, and I’m here... what exactly am I doing here?

I always felt I had something special. Social skills, passion, a network. Those things actually mattered in my twenties. But now? Now I’m just a middle-aged man barely scraping by on these illusions. These things are losing their power. A 25-year-old kid can surpass me with ease.

Am I really obsolete?

It’s not "really." I just am.

I’m sitting here in the dark because I don’t want to get up. I don’t want to go to the bedroom or see my wife’s face. I don’t want to wake up tomorrow morning and go to the office. I don’t want to smile anymore.

But tomorrow, I’ll still smile. I’ll still go to the office, joke around with my subordinates, and pretend everything is fine. That’s who I am. That’s what I’ve always done.

I have never been real.

Not even now.

Xiaomei, there’s something I need to talk to you about.

I just got back from the rest area. On the drive back, I had a long talk with Xinyu. She said something that I haven't been able to stop thinking about: "Dad, I know you’ve been really unhappy lately."

I had no idea she could tell. I thought I was hiding it well.

As we kept talking, I realized she doesn't want to study abroad at all. I always thought that was her dream, but the truth is, I was the one who kept obsessing over it until it became something she *had* to achieve. She made it very clear—she wants to go to university here, close to home. Hearing that... I don't even know how to describe how I felt.

I pulled over at the rest area for a while. I told her I was sorry. I told her that I’ve been using my own fears to hijack her life. Saying those words out loud made me realize what I’ve actually been doing all these years.

She put her hand on my shoulder. I cried. It wasn't about my own problems; it was for the harm I might have caused her, and also... the feeling of finally being understood by her.

I’m a bit of a mess right now. It feels like I’ve spent my whole life trying to prove something—through my work, my achievements, her grades. But maybe we’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places?

I’m not sure how to tell you all this. But I wanted to ask, have you felt something similar? What’s your take on this?

I’m not sure what to do next yet. I just feel like some things need to change.

Linlin, I’m back in my room now.

I sat here for a while, but I still felt the need to say this to you one more time. It’s not that I didn’t say enough earlier, it’s just... I’m afraid I’ll find another excuse to avoid this topic tomorrow.

I’m sorry. Truly, I am so sorry.

I always thought that as long as I came back every month, I was fulfilling my responsibilities. But listening to you talk about those details today, I realized I haven't truly "seen" you at all. You’re there all by yourself, washing Mom, cooking for her, and helping her with her rehab every single day. I didn’t even know how many steps she can take now, or how much more hair she’s been losing lately. I just came and went in a hurry, like I was checking off a task.

I owe you so much.

I’ve been thinking these past few days about what would have happened to Mom if you weren't there. I don’t have the courage to follow that thought to the end.

I’ve already discussed it with Xiaomei. Starting next week, I’m adjusting my work schedule. I’ll leave the office early on Mondays and Thursdays to try and get back here. I know it might not be enough, but it’s what I can do for now. I’m also going to ask Nurse Li for specific caregiving instructions; I don’t want to be clueless anymore.

Don’t be so quick to say "it’s okay" or "you’re busy too." I don’t want to hear that this time. What I want to hear is... what kind of help do you need most right now? How has Mom been lately? Has she said anything?

Let’s take care of her together. Not me "helping" you, but us doing it together.

I mean it this time.

— Shen Linchuan

Sometimes I wonder what I’m actually good at.

# June 12, 2022, 11:45 PM

My wife has already gone to bed. I’m sitting here in the study, phone still on, with Lin’s voice message playing on a loop in my head—"I really can't hold on anymore."

I listen to it over and over, like a form of self-punishment.

During dinner, Xiaomei said I should consider moving Mom to a better rehabilitation center, that it would take some of the pressure off Lin. I snapped back at her immediately: "I’m just trying to help, is that so wrong?" My voice was harsh. She didn't say another word; she just gave me a look—a look filled with nothing but disappointment.

Looking back now, I know how ugly I sounded. But at that moment, I just... how do I put it? I just felt this desperate need to push back. It felt like she was accusing me of something. But she wasn't; she was just stating a fact. A better rehab center does cost more money. And we already don't have enough.

What exactly am I "helping" with?

I go back once a month, sit with Mom for a little while, and then what? Then I come back here. Lin is there every single day—feeding her, wiping her down, dealing with all the things I don't even want to face. When she says she "can't hold on," I can hear that she’s truly on the verge of a breakdown. But what can I do? I’m stuck here—the job, my daughter, the mortgage, my wife... I’m trapped.

That photography thing is still weighing on me, too. I spent over six months on those photos, thinking I had something special, only to have them rejected with a single sentence. I didn't even dare to ask for the specific reasons. I know why. They weren't good enough. I’m not good enough.

I'm 42 years old, and I'm still sitting here lying to myself. I thought I had talent, that I had potential, but the truth is I have nothing. I’m just another middle-aged man coasting on talk. I should have seen it clearly when the departments merged, but I didn't. I kept telling myself it was nothing, that I still had a chance.

And now?

Xinyu’s grades are still the same. I want to help her, but I don’t know how. I don’t even dare to ask too many questions at dinner for fear of pressuring her. But my silence is a pressure in itself, isn't it? She can feel my anxiety.

I really can't do anything right.

Lin’s voice message is still there. I want to call her back, but I don’t know what to say. What can I say? "Just hang in there a bit longer"? "I’ll find a way"? They’re all lies. I don’t have a way. I can’t even save myself, so how can I save her? How can I save Mom?

Xiaomei was right. I want to help, but I can’t. That’s the part that cuts the deepest.

Maybe I really am a failure. Not the dramatic kind, but this kind—the kind where everything looks fine on the surface, while everything is actually, slowly, falling apart.

I don’t know what value I have left. I really don't.

Just finished the meeting. Still in the office.

Xiao Wang said something that took me a moment to process. He said, "You seem different lately. You used to be so eager to prove yourself, but now it feels like you're actually listening to us."

I froze for a second. I just said, "Yeah, I’m learning a few things." He smiled and went back to presenting his proposal.

But my mind is still racing.

He’s right. In the past, I’d spend meetings thinking about how to jump in, how to look like an expert, or how to make quick decisions just so people would think I was decisive. Now... I don’t know when it started, but I just want to hear what they’re actually thinking. Not to argue—just... to listen.

It feels strange.

Relieved? Yeah, a bit. Like I’ve let go of something. But at the same time, I’m a little wary—am I just performing "change" now? Am I just playing the role of a "better self"?

Confused. Just confused.

I don’t know if this counts as progress. Or if it’s just another way of escaping.

We’ll see next time.

---

Going home tonight. Xinyu has been studying for exams lately. I should remember to ask how she’s doing. Not about her grades. Just... ask her.

December 10th, past 11 PM

Just finished my shower and I’m sitting in the study. It’s quiet outside. Wang Xiaomei has been asleep for a while, and Xinyu is asleep in her room too. I originally planned to scroll through my phone for a bit, but suddenly, I don’t feel like looking at anything.

I still feel like writing something down.

The events from today’s annual meeting keep looping in my head. Thinking back now, it all feels a bit surreal.

During the rehearsal this afternoon, my prepared speech was right there—the same old stuff. Talking about this year’s achievements, the team’s hard work, next year’s goals. I could recite it with my eyes closed. Standing on that stage, I kept thinking: another year, another routine.

But when I actually got up there... I don’t know how to put it, I just suddenly didn't want to follow the script anymore.

I looked at the people sitting below—my subordinates, some new hires, and that young intern. The lights were so bright. I opened my mouth to say the first line, and I just froze. I actually froze.

Then, I said something else. I said... I said that my value doesn't just come from my professional achievements, but also from the time I spend with my family and my responsibilities to them.

As I said that, my throat tightened. Honestly, my eyes got a bit misty. I paused for several seconds; I bet the people below were a bit bewildered. I’ve never been like that in public.

I went on to say a few things about family and companionship. It wasn't very coherent; I even repeated some words. But it was... it was real. It wasn't the kind of thing you say just to sound humble or profound; it was what I was actually thinking.

I didn't wait for applause after I finished. I just walked down.

Later, a young employee—that new intern—came up to me during the tea break. He told me he was moved by my speech and that he was reflecting on his own choices. He said he always thought success meant promotions and raises, but after hearing what I said...

I didn't know how to respond. I just patted him on the shoulder.

But in that moment, I felt something. It wasn't the kind of achievement I’m used to. It wasn't that "I spoke well, so I earned the applause" feeling. It was... how do I put it... the feeling of being seen? No, that’s not it. It was the feeling that "I influenced someone with my authentic voice."

It felt very foreign.

What have I been living on all this time? Connections, forced enthusiasm, smooth talk at dinner parties, remembering the names of other people’s kids. There’s nothing wrong with those things, but... today, I didn't use any of them. I just stood there, my voice trembling slightly, my eyes a bit wet, saying things that weren't necessarily clever, but were true.

And yet, someone was touched.

Sitting here now, I’m wondering: is this what Mom has been doing all along? As a mother and a wife, she doesn't need to perform at dinner parties; she just... exists. She’s present. She’s responsible.

I feel like I’ve been running away from that identity. I felt I had to be that enthusiastic, successful, well-connected Shen Linchuan. But today I realized—maybe I don’t have to rely on those things to influence others. Maybe being real is enough.

This sounds a bit sentimental. But right now, I truly believe it’s right.

Work again tomorrow. Still have to be the Marketing Director. But things feel a little different.

I don't know how to describe it. It’s... a tired feeling, but a different kind of tired.

I understand the task. However, I need to point out an issue:

According to the instructions in Part 1, I need to know:

Please tell me what event Shen Linchuan experienced on March 16, 2022, so that I can create a WeChat Moment that aligns with his character based on a real scenario.

For example:
