- The specific time and location of the event?
- His immediate feelings at the time?

With this information, I will be able to write an authentic, natural WeChat Moment that fits his current psychological state.

Sometimes companionship is just that. You don't need to say a word; you just sit there.

Took Xinyu out for a drive this afternoon. She was the one who asked to come along, and I agreed without a second thought. The whole way, she gazed out the window while I drove. We hardly spoke, but for some reason, that was enough.

I used to think I always had to be doing something with her or talking about something—as if I had to fill every silence. Now I realize that sometimes, silence is the best kind of company.

She’s still as quiet as ever, and I’m still the talkative one. But at least now, I’ve learned when to just shut up.

Xiaomei, I just got an email. The sales department wants me to take on a new project—integrating the online and offline marketing systems.

To be honest, my first reaction was a bit disappointing. It’s not a promotion; it’s just a project lead role. In the past, I probably would have turned it down outright or felt miserable about it all day.

But I sat there for half an hour and really thought it through. Actually, this project is quite interesting—I get to build something from scratch without being tied down by existing frameworks. I’ve already emailed them back saying I’ll take it, but I want to do it in a new way.

Instead of burning myself out to hit deadlines, I want to focus on doing quality work and making sure my team actually learns something in the process. I figure if I do it this way, I won't have to spend three or four nights a week at business dinners just to maintain "connections." I can save more time for the family.

I know this sounds a bit sudden. But lately, spending time with Mom and Xinyu has made me realize that some things are more important than "success." Maybe I really have hit a glass ceiling, but it doesn't seem that scary anymore.

What do you think?

October 21st, past 11 PM

Sitting in the study, it’s pitch black outside the window. While I was showering just now, my wife knocked on the door to say Xinyu had gone to sleep. Only then did I realize I hadn’t spent any real time with her all day.

I’m still thinking about that conversation on the highway today.

When Xinyu asked, "Dad, what’s been going on with you lately?" my gut reaction was to say, "Nothing, just busy with work"—the same script I’ve used for over a decade. But this time, the words didn't come out. I paused for a long time, gripping the steering wheel tight, before finally saying, "I’ve been running back and forth between two places lately, and I’m starting to lose track of where I’m supposed to be."

I stunned even myself when I said it.

She didn't push further; she just stared out the window. After a while, she said, "I know. Mom is worried about you, too."

In that moment, I felt exposed. I always thought I was hiding it so well—smiling when I should, talking when I should. My subordinates think I’m easygoing, my friends think I’m enthusiastic, and my daughter is supposed to think I’m invincible. But it turns out they see right through me. My exhaustion, my fragmentation, my confusion—it’s all written on my face.

I patted her shoulder and didn't say anything else.

Thinking back now, that gesture probably said more than anything else could. I gave up on explaining; I gave up on maintaining that "Father always has the answer" persona. It was just a pat on the shoulder, as if to say: You see through me, and I’m tired, too.

These past few days, I’ve been wondering what exactly I’m holding onto. Every month I go back to my hometown to see my mother, watching her struggle through recovery in the rehab room, and the powerlessness I feel is like a heavy stone. I want to help her, but all I can do is sit with her, ask Sister Li how she’s doing, and then come back. Then I go back to smiling in the office, attending dinners, and pretending everything is fine.

But my golf buddies don't know what's on my mind, and at those business dinners, I can't exactly say, "Actually, I’m terrified right now." I’m afraid I can’t keep up this life of running back and forth, afraid my mother’s health will keep declining, afraid of Xinyu’s college entrance exams, afraid that I’ve already hit the ceiling at the company... There are too many things to fear.

The hardest part is that I’ve always felt my value lies in my connections, in that sense of importance at those dinners, in people saying, "Mr. Shen is such a master of networking." But when my mother is lying in a hospital bed, those things feel as thin as paper. Sitting by her bedside, I suddenly understood the meaning of true helplessness.

And yet, I can’t let go. I still think about next week’s golf game, I still take the business calls, and I still calculate in my head how many rounds of golf I’ve missed this month. It makes me feel hypocritical and selfish.

I don't know who I am anymore. I’m not that marketing director who’s always smiling, but I’m not entirely that middle-aged man worried sick about his family either. I’m stuck in the middle, unable to let go of either side, and unable to do either well.

My daughter knows. My wife knows. I’m the only one still pretending.

But today on the highway, I finally stopped pretending. I was scared in that moment, but also a little... relieved?

I don't know where this is heading.

Time to sleep. Tomorrow is another day.

March 20, 2022 Afternoon

The office is quiet. I’m sitting at my desk; the computer screen is glowing, but I haven’t looked at it for half an hour.

After the morning meeting ended, it suddenly occurred to me that the 2015 marketing proposal was still in the cabinet. I don’t know why I wanted to dig it out. Maybe I just wanted to see what I used to be like.

I was actually a bit nervous as I opened the folder. Those case studies, the data, the creative concepts... Looking at them, I suddenly realized—I used to do good work. Truly good. Not just "decent," but the kind of work that showed real vision and execution.

I stared at it for a long time. Looking at one particular page, I could even recall how I felt back then—that rush of excitement from turning an idea into reality. Back then, I would polish a concept over and over; I’d wake up in the middle of the night with a sudden breakthrough.

Then, I opened the recruitment sites. It’s become a habit—checking them at least a few times a week, like looking for an emergency exit. But this time was different. Looking at those job descriptions, I suddenly felt exhausted. Where would I even jump to? To another company to do the exact same thing? Or just to keep running away?

I closed the tabs.

After sitting there for a long while, I finally admitted a truth to myself: maybe the problem isn't a lack of ability. Maybe the problem is... my perception of myself has become distorted.

When did it start? After the department merger in 2021? Or even earlier? I’ve been telling myself, "I’ve hit the ceiling," "I can’t do this anymore," "I’m too old, there are no more opportunities." But are these things actually true? Or have I just repeated them so many times that I finally started believing them?

When my mother cried in front of me the other day, she said I still have so many possibilities. At the time, I thought she was just trying to comfort me. Now... I’m not so sure.

I don’t know what to do next. I don’t have a clear plan or any grand goals. But at the very least, I’ve decided to stop trying to escape. Maybe I should find new meaning in the position I’m in right now. Maybe I should take a hard look at what I’ve truly lost, and what I truly have left.

It sounds vague. But it feels more real than browsing job boards.

I’m going home tonight. Xinyu is preparing for her monthly exams; Wang Xiaomei said she’s been reviewing well lately. I think maybe I should really spend some time with her. Not the anxious kind of "being there," but... actually being present.

I don’t know if this counts as the start of a change.

March 5, 2022, 9:15 PM

I’ve been sitting in the study for an hour now. The noise downstairs died out long ago.

I heard Wang Xiaomei knocking just now. She wanted to come in and talk. I know what she wants to say—she’s seen exactly how I’ve been lately. But I just didn’t want to open the door; I didn't even respond. She stood outside for a while and then went downstairs. I heard her sigh.

Why do I treat her like this?

She’s just trying to help. But I said those words: "There’s nothing to talk about, everything is fine." The moment I heard myself say it, I knew it was a lie. Everything is not fine. We both know it. I just can’t bring myself to say it out loud.

Xinyu must have heard us. I heard her bedroom door close. She’s such a quiet child; she must be sitting in there right now, feeling completely lost. Once again, I acted like a jerk in front of her.

I really don’t know when it happened, but the "something" between Xiaomei and me is just… gone. It wasn’t a big fight, there’s no one else—it’s just gone. We’re like two strangers, staring at our own phones, having the occasional argument over our daughter’s grades, and then retreating to our separate rooms. I used to be so passionate, so well-spoken. How did I get to a point where I can’t say a single sincere word to the person closest to me?

Maybe I chose the wrong path from the start. Not the wrong woman, but the wrong path. I could have… forget it, I don’t even know what I "could have" been.

I’m 42 now. No deep professional skills, no real hobbies, and no way out. I knew it the day the departments merged: I’ve hit the ceiling. I got this far on passion and networking, but those things can’t protect me now. When the company needs to cut costs, my passion is worthless.

I don’t even know how to fix any of this. The rift between Xiaomei and me has grown too deep. My daughter’s grades are still slipping. My mother’s health is deteriorating day by day. I go back to my hometown to see her every month, and every time I feel so powerless—what can I even do? I can’t do anything.

I can laugh and joke with friends at a bar, I can act like a success on the golf course, but it’s all fake. When I come home, I’m a loser—a loser who can’t take care of his family, can’t communicate with his wife, and can’t give his daughter any encouragement.

I just remembered, my boss pulled me aside again this afternoon to talk about "work adjustments." There was a hint of… pity in his voice? Or was it disappointment? I’m not sure. But I know he’s evaluating my worth, and I’m no longer valuable.

Maybe I should have given up on these illusions sooner. I’m just a mediocre man. This is my endgame.

It’s silent downstairs. Xiaomei must be asleep. Or maybe she’s still in the living room, waiting for me to come down. But I can’t. I don’t even know what to say.

I’m just so tired.

September 8, 2022, Evening

Xinyu said I’ve been different lately.

I was sitting by her bedroom door, watching her paint. She didn’t look up, but that one sentence made me freeze. I wanted to say something, but in the end, I just said, "Yes, I’m learning to see you." She kept painting, and I kept sitting there.

I don’t know why, but my eyes felt a bit hot.

This afternoon, I took the initiative to ask if I could take a few photos of her painting. She asked "Why?" and I didn't give some polished reason like I used to. I just said, "I want to see how you focus on something." After I said it, I even stunned myself for a moment. I realized that in the past, I took photos because I wanted to post them; I wanted others to see "how talented my daughter is," to prove something. But this time... I really just wanted to look.

Just wanted to look.

It sounds so simple, yet for me, it feels like a massive thing.

Lately, I’ve been wondering what exactly I’m running away from. The work functions, the golf, the dinners, the jokes, the networking... I filled every gap with these things, as if stopping would mean being swallowed by something. I used "passion" to prove my worth and "success" to mask my fear. But the truth? The truth is I wasn't seeing anything clearly.

I hadn't even truly looked at my daughter.

She says I’m different. But to be honest, I don’t even know if this is just another performance. I am "changing," but is this "change" itself just another form of escape? Am I just trying to prove myself again by "being a better father"?

I don’t know.

But at least today, I didn’t pick up my phone to check work messages. At least today, I sat by her side and said nothing, just watching her brush move across the paper. Her eyes were so focused, possessed by a quietness I had never truly looked at before.

Maybe I need to learn not to prove anything. Not to change anything. Just... sit here. Watch. Accept.

The process is exhausting. But maybe this is what’s real.

[Photo: A suburban road outside the car window, sunlight dappling the pavement through the trees, with the faint outline of hills in the distance]

Sometimes you realize that companionship doesn't always need words.

Spent the whole afternoon driving with Xinyu today. She talked about her recent paintings the entire time, so earnest and focused. I didn't rush to offer advice or encouragement like I used to; I just sat there and listened. When she got to the parts that made her happy, she’d turn to look at me, her eyes shining.

It felt like that was enough.

I’m not sure when it started, but there’s always been a bit of a distance between us. Today I finally realized that sometimes, listening is more important than speaking.

Maybe I’ve been overthinking it all along.

#Companionship #Weekend #ListeningToHer

# MEMO

**2022-05-27 11:45**

I’m no longer someone who can create.

I just finished looking through that young photographer’s portfolio in the restroom. There’s something in his photos. A sense of life. Angles I simply don’t see.

I’ve been shooting for fifteen years. I bought the DSLRs, watched the tutorials, spent my weekends wandering through old neighborhoods. I thought I was building something. Now I realize I’ve just been repeating myself. The same compositions, the same understanding of light, the same—mediocrity.

He’s fifteen years younger than me. He’s only been working for two years. And yet, he already sees things I will never see.

What was I thinking? Forty-two years old and still fantasizing that I’m a creative person. Still using "hobbies" to lie to myself. Photography, golf, driving around... they’re all just escapes. I’ve never truly gone deep into anything. I’m just a middle-aged man who survives on small talk and remembering people’s names.

Back when the departments merged, I thought maybe I could pivot. Maybe I could do something different. Now I know—I can’t. This is who I am. I’m the guy who smiles, who socializes, who remembers the names of the clients' kids. That’s it.

Mom is back in our hometown. Xinyu’s grades still aren't improving. The way Xiaomei looks at me has changed again lately. I can feel it.

Have I really failed?

Or is it that I’ve been a failure all along, and it just took me forty-two years to see it clearly?

Just back from my hometown. Mom was in good spirits today and spent some time sitting out in the yard.

Lin Mei has been taking such great care of her these past few days; I’ve seen it all. I used to always tell myself I was too busy, but the truth is, I’ve been running away for too long. I’ve made up my mind: from now on, I’m going back home more often every week.

Some things are far more important than we realize.

2:30 AM. Can’t sleep.

Downstairs, Wang Xiaomei’s snoring is faint; it’s good that she can sleep. The light in Xinyu’s room has been out for a long time. It’s just me sitting on the sofa, staring at my phone screen in the dark.

I scrolled through the job boards again.

"Marketing Director, under 35 preferred"—my finger hovered over the screen for several seconds. My mouth twitched. I looked at myself in the mirror; 42 years old, the wrinkles at the corners of my eyes looking particularly sharp under the cold glow of the phone.

I scrolled down. Another position, salary listed at 280k. I muttered to myself, "Is this all I’m worth?"

The sound was a bit too loud, and I stopped abruptly, afraid of waking the family.

But the truth is, I want someone to hear me. I want someone to tell me this isn't real.

During the day at the office, I smile radiantly. In meetings with my new superior, I say, "No problem, leave it to me." In the afternoon, I even organized a small team dinner and picked up the tab. My colleagues all say I’m still the same old Mr. Shen, full of energy. No one can tell that my private office has been turned into an open workspace, or that the title on my business card has changed from "Marketing Director" to "Deputy General Manager of Sales."

No one can tell that I’m actually falling apart.

Xinyu saw me sitting in the dark today and asked what was wrong. I told her I wasn't sleeping well. She frowned, a look of worry in her eyes that made me feel even worse. My daughter is only 16; she shouldn't have to worry about me. I’m supposed to be the one who protects her.

But right now, I can’t even protect myself.

What am I actually good at?

Besides drinking and chatting, besides remembering the names of clients' family members, besides telling mindless jokes at dinner parties... what else can I do? What have I been doing for the last 15 years? Why didn't I build up something real?

It’s not that I haven't thought about this. I started feeling it back in 2016. But back then, I was still lying to myself, saying it was just temporary exhaustion. Later, when the departments merged, I stayed silent at home for days. Wang Xiaomei asked what was wrong, and I said nothing. So she stopped asking.

Now, "nothing" has become the reality—nothing can hurt me anymore because I have nothing left to lose.

Golf, watching the games, dinner parties... these things once made me feel successful, well-connected. Now I realize they were all illusions. Permissions revoked, office taken back, budget slashed. It all proves I’m not that important. I’ve been deluding myself all along.

Mom is back in our hometown; her legs still don't move well. I go back once a month and sit by her bed, not knowing what to say. She asks how work is, and I say it’s great. I’m lying to her. I’m lying to everyone.

Maybe I should find a place to just be alone. No need to smile, no need to pretend, no need to say "No problem, I’ve got this."

But I can’t. I have a daughter to raise, a mother to care for, and a mortgage to pay. I have to keep smiling.

When the sun comes up, I have to go to the office. I have to pretend everything is normal.

Maybe this is just my life.

The phone screen lit up again. Another position, even lower annual pay. I closed the job site.

Time to sleep.

Spent the weekend in the countryside with Xinyu. The weather was gorgeous. She spent the whole ride reading in the back while I just drove aimlessly.

We stopped at a service area for a bit, and she said she wanted some milk tea. As we sat there, she asked if work hadn't been going well lately. I said I was fine, but she just smiled and said, "Dad, you can't fool me."

At that moment, I realized that she had seen right through everything I was trying to hide.

She fell asleep on the way back, her head resting against the window. I drove in silence, lost in thought. Maybe I’ve been looking for answers in all the wrong places.

Right now, I just want to be there for her and listen to her talk. Everything else doesn't seem that important anymore.

July 6, 2022 Afternoon

I don’t know how to write this. My hands are still shaking.

At two o'clock this afternoon, Mr. Wang told me off in front of everyone, saying my frequent leaves of absence were dragging down the department's efficiency. Everyone was watching me. I could feel it. I didn't even look up at him; I just said, "Okay, I'll be more mindful," then turned around and walked back to my desk.

I couldn't even hear what he said after that.

I’ve been sitting at my desk for half an hour now. The report from this morning is still on the screen; I haven't changed a single word. My colleagues are all working, though someone glances over at me every now and then. I know what they’re thinking. That "passionate" Marketing Director—the boss who always offers to pick up the tab and remembers everyone’s name—just got chewed out like that and didn't even push back.

I honestly didn't have the energy to push back.

My mother’s medical bills are due again. Linlin called last week to say Mom has started drooling again and the rehab isn't going well. I have to take more time off to go back next week. Mr. Wang said I take leave too often—he’s right. I do. I can’t be in two places at once.

Xinyu’s report card came out last week. Her math grade dropped another five points. Wang Xiaomei said I’m putting too much pressure on her. Maybe she’s right. I don’t even know how to talk to my daughter anymore. Last weekend, we drove out to the countryside and sat for two hours in almost total silence.

I used to think I was good at handling these things. Networking, communication, problem-solving—those were the things that made me feel valuable. And now? I can’t handle anything.

There’s still a WeChat message from Sister Li on my phone. She says Mom’s been in a bad mood this week and asked when I can come back. I haven’t replied. I don’t know how to reply.

Maybe I really am a failure. And not just at work. I can’t protect my mother, I can’t help my daughter, and I couldn't save my marriage. My passion, my smiles, my "friends"—it all seems so pathetic now.

I’m sitting here, staring at the screen, and my hands are still shaking.

I don’t even know why I’m still going.

# Diary

**July 1, 2022, 9:00 AM**

On the high-speed train. I’ve turned off my phone.

I caught a glimpse of myself in the mirror yesterday and it gave me a fright. My eyes were sunken, my face was as pale as paper, and my cheeks had hollowed out. I poked my face with my finger; the skin felt loose, lacking any elasticity. Is this what failure looks like? This is what I look like now.

I left before Xiao Mei got home. I left her a note, my handwriting so messy I could barely read it myself. She’ll probably frown again when she sees it. I don’t feel like explaining anything anymore.

That photography project keeps spinning in my head. Rejected. I spent two months on that proposal, spending every weekend running through the city's old neighborhoods, taking hundreds of photos. I thought this time would be different; I thought I was finally making something meaningful. Then they shot it down with a single sentence: "Lacks a professional perspective; the concept isn't deep enough."

I just laughed. Lacks a professional perspective. I’ve studied photography—bought a DSLR in 2018, watched the tutorials, went to the workshops. I thought I knew something. But it seems like nothing ever truly sank in. It’s just like everything else I’ve done all these years—it looks like I’m doing it, but in reality, there’s nothing there.

I should have seen it clearly when the departments merged. That wasn't an "adjustment"; that was being exiled. My authority is shrinking, my voice is losing its weight. But I kept smiling, kept organizing dinners, kept pretending everything was fine. That’s how I’ve survived all these years—relying on enthusiasm, on networking, on having a silver tongue.

And now? All of that is losing its spark.

Mom’s health has taken another turn for the worse. When I went back home last time, her right hand was shaking even more. Lin said she’s been talking less lately, sometimes just staring into space. I sat by her bed wanting to say something, but nothing came out. What can I do? I can’t even save myself; how am I supposed to save her?

I’m 42. It’s not a new fact, but today it suddenly feels heavy. I have no specialized skills, no deep-seated interests, and I’ve made no long-term preparations for anything. I’ve spent my life getting by on last-minute fixes, bursting with solutions only under pressure, and then continuing to stumble forward in chaos. That might be an advantage for someone in their twenties, but for a 42-year-old...

This is what a loser looks like, I guess.

The train is picking up speed. I close my eyes, but nothing comes to mind. Just a blank.

I don't know what else I can do.

Xiaomei, I sat downstairs for a while.

After saying those things just now, I’ve been thinking... Honestly, I should have said this to you a long time ago. It’s not something I just realized today; it’s... I’ve always known, I just never had the courage to say it out loud.

You were right—you always knew I was afraid. I thought I was hiding it well, but even Xinyu saw right through me. It wasn't until I was crying in the car that I finally understood: everything I’ve done all these years—the dinners, the socializing, even the expectations I placed on Xinyu—it was all an escape. I was using those things to prove to myself that I still had value.

I don’t know what to do next. But I know I can’t go on like this anymore. Not for work, not for anything external, but because... I want to be a father Xinyu actually wants to be close to. I want to have real conversations with you, instead of us just staring at our phones.

Maybe we’re both exhausted. Maybe we can learn together that it’s okay not to be perfect.

Thank you for not walking away today.

# January 25, 2022, 11:47 PM, Parking Lot

I’ve been sitting in the car for half an hour now. The engine is off, and the glow from my phone screen is the only thing lighting up my face.

Today was fucking exhausting.

That young guy, Zhang Hao, came to see me this afternoon. He wanted to do short-video marketing—TikTok, Xiaohongshu, all that stuff. I just asked him one question: "How much actual profit can short videos bring to the company?" He stammered for ages without an answer, so I stopped listening.

Sitting here now, it finally hit me—I’ve become exactly the kind of person I used to hate. The kind who just says, "This won't work, that won't work." When did I turn into this?

I used to be the one with ideas and passion. Back in college, I was the Student Union President; I’d stay up all night buzzing over an idea for an event. And now? Now, my first instinct when I hear a new idea is to run the numbers—how much money can this make? Is it worth it?

I killed Zhang Hao’s enthusiasm. I saw the disappointment in his eyes.

The dinner tonight was even worse. Eating with clients, constantly toasting, telling jokes, manufacturing an "atmosphere." I could feel myself performing. It was like wearing a suit that doesn't fit, just barely holding it together. After five or six rounds of drinks, I suddenly ran out of energy and just stared at my phone for a while. My colleagues kept laughing, but I couldn't join in.

What was I thinking about? I was thinking—I’m 42.

That sentence feels like a curse. I’m 42, I have no real specialized skills, no savings, and no Plan B. What if the company actually lets me go one day? Where would I go? What could I do? Go to another real estate agency as a salesman? Or go apply for some marketing position with my head down, competing with 30-year-olds?

I’ve looked at recruitment sites. I really have. But every time I see those job requirements, I just want to close the tab.

My wife hasn't been talking to me much lately. Or rather, we talk, but it’s all surface-level. "What are we eating today?" "How are our daughter’s grades?" That’s it. I want to tell her something deeper, but I don't know where to start. I’m afraid she’ll see how panicked I actually am.

Xinyu’s college entrance exam is a little over a year away. Every time I see her report card, I think—what if she doesn't get into a good university? What if she wants to study abroad? Where would I get the money? My mother’s medical bills are ongoing; my sister Lin is back in our hometown taking care of her alone. I go back once a month, and every time, I feel powerless. What can I do? I can’t do anything.

I’ve been sitting in the parking lot for half an hour just because I don’t want to go home.

It’s not that I don’t want to see my wife and daughter. It’s that I don’t want to look at their faces and pretend everything is fine. I’ve already been performing at the office all day.

Maybe I should seriously consider jumping ship. Maybe I should sign up for a course and learn some actual skills. Maybe... maybe I should have done something a long time ago instead of just coasting on "passion" and networking.

But I’m just so tired.

Tomorrow, it starts again. Keep smiling, keep toasting, keep pretending that Shen Linchuan is still that enthusiastic Marketing Director. Nobody needs to know what I’m thinking.

I have to go home now.

# November 15, 2022, Night

I was just sorting through some photos, and I’m at a bit of a loss for words to describe how I’m feeling.

I spent the morning at the rehab center with Mom for her training session. I brought my DSLR along. To be honest, I’ve had this camera for nearly four years, and it’s basically been gathering dust for the last year or so. I don’t know what I was thinking today, but I just decided to take it out. Maybe I wanted to document something—Mom gripping the therapist’s hand, slowly lifting that leg, her face a mix of tension and intense focus. Sister Li was beside her, cheering her on: "You’re doing great, Sister Zhou, let’s try one more."

I spent the whole afternoon shooting like that.

Now, sitting in my study looking at these photos, I realize—they aren’t "beautiful" at all. The compositions are messy, the lighting is harsh at times, and in some shots, Mom even looks a bit disheveled. But as I keep looking, I feel... how do I put it? It’s not the kind of feeling you get from getting likes on WeChat Moments. It’s something very different. It’s like I’m witnessing something real.

I named the album "Mom’s Persistence."

Then I kept wondering, why didn't I post them to Moments? Usually, I’d share something like this, right? I’d write a caption like, "Accompanying Mom through rehab; here’s to every bit of perseverance." It would get plenty of likes. But I just... didn't do it.

I put my phone down.

It made me feel a bit uneasy. Not that I regretted not posting, but I suddenly realized what I’ve been doing all this time. In the past, whenever I took photos—of golf, dinners, business trips—it was always for the sake of posting them. The camera was just a tool to show others "the kind of life I’m living." But these photos today... I just want them to exist, whether anyone sees them or not.

It’s a strange feeling. And a bit of a guilty one.

Because it hit me that for the past four years, the "life" I’ve been documenting with my camera hasn't really been "life" at all. It’s all been carefully curated snippets designed for display. And now, I’m doing something completely different—I’m recording for myself, not for validation from others.

But to be honest, I don’t know if this is a good thing or a bad thing.

Lately, it feels like I’m losing something. Those dinner parties, the social obligations, the time spent with friends on the golf course—those things gave me a sense of fulfillment and presence for years. Now I’m giving up a part of that to do these "real" things. But I’m not entirely ready to let go of the rest. I can’t bear to part with it, yet I feel like a hypocrite.

Mom needs me. That’s real.

But I’m also scared. Scared of what I’ll become if this continues. Scared that I won’t be able to keep this up for long. Scared that one day I’ll be pulled back by that social life and that sense of achievement.

Or... maybe I shouldn't have left it at all.

I don’t know. I really don’t know right now.

It’s just that these photos, this afternoon at the rehab center, made me feel something different. Not better, not worse, just... different. A bit more real. And a bit more powerless.

Whatever, I’ll stop thinking about it. Work tomorrow.

# Memo

**Date: August 25, Afternoon**

---

Stop proving.

These two words have been looping in my head. Ever since the drive back from the service area, they haven’t stopped spinning.

She said I’m "always trying to prove something." My 16-year-old daughter, sitting in the passenger seat, said it so calmly. It wasn’t an accusation; it was just a statement of fact. But it stung more than any accusation ever could.

Why? Why do I always feel the need to show everyone how successful, capable, and well-connected I am? The golf, the business dinners, the forced laughter in the office… is it all just to prove something?

Her grades. I’ve always been obsessed with her grades. Not because I actually care about what she’s learning, but because I care about how others perceive me. "My daughter is just an average student"—how heavy has that sentence felt in my heart? She can feel it. That’s why she started getting anxious. It wasn’t her anxiety; it was mine, and I infected her with it.

When did I become like this?

---

Maybe value doesn't need to be proven at all.

In that moment, she put her hand on my shoulder. I didn’t say anything, and I didn’t offer any advice. I just cried. A 42-year-old man, crying in a service area parking lot. It wasn’t about my career, or the department merger, or any of those hollow things. It was the realization that I might have hurt the person who matters most.

She didn’t leave. She just sat there.

What was that? Acceptance? Forgiveness? Or was she just waiting for me to finally wake up?

---

**Things that need to change:**
- Stop pretending to be strong in front of her.
- Next time we talk, actually listen; don’t rush to give advice.
- Stop treating her grades as my own personal achievement.
- Maybe… tell her that I’m scared and uncertain, too.

---

**Questions:**
- If I stop defining myself by my social status and career success, who am I?
- What part of me is still real?
- Why did it take me so many years to understand this?

---

I’ll think more tomorrow. My brain is exhausted.

But I feel something shifting. I don’t know if it’s for better or worse.

But at least… it doesn’t feel so fake anymore.

I scrolled through WeChat and found the photo I just took of Mom. She was sitting in the rehab center’s sunroom, a clear smile on her face. I stared at it for a while, then pulled up my chat with Wang Xiaomei.

---

**[Image]**

Xiaomei, look at this.

Mom is doing so well today. Sister Li said this has been her fastest week of recovery yet.

When she saw these photos, she actually said "thank you" to me. You know?

It just hit me—since last year, it feels like we’ve both been so caught up in our own things. I’ve been focused on how to take care of her, and you’ve been focused on Xinyu’s schoolwork. But really, we’ve been doing the same thing all along.

Just being there for them, I guess.

I was thinking, maybe we should be the same way with Xinyu. Instead of pushing her for grades, we should really try to see what she’s doing and what’s on her mind.

I’m feeling a bit emotional, not sure how to put it into words.

Are you free this weekend? Want to go see Mom together?

I’d like to bring Xinyu along. She seems a bit worn out lately, too.

February 1, 2022  11:37 PM

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying here for nearly two hours, my mind still racing. I might as well get up and jot this down on my phone, rather than keep tossing and turning and waking up Xiaomei.

Looking at those likes and comments, friends asking "What’s up, man?" or "Hit me up if you need to talk," I just replied with a "Haha, it’s nothing, just feeling a bit sentimental."

When the departments merged, I stayed home in silence for three days. Xiaomei must have seen right through me. But she didn't ask, and I didn't say a word. Now, sitting at my desk in the open-plan office, I can feel the people behind me watching. Moving from a private office to this... it feels like falling from grace, back down to the rank and file. I tell myself it’s no big deal. I keep smiling, keep saying "No problem, I’ve got this," but my voice sounds hollow.

I’m 42. What is there left for me at this age? I checked the job boards, but I closed the tab after two seconds. Where would I go? Could I even start over? I have no real skills, no specialized expertise. I’m just a middle-aged man who’s good at talking.

Xinyu’s mock exam scores dropped again recently. I started spacing out thinking about it during dinner. I could feel the way Xiaomei was looking at me. She was thinking, "You’re obsessing over work again." God knows I want to focus on my daughter, but my head just can’t hold that much. My mother’s recovery isn’t going well either; Sister Li mentioned last time that her spirits are low, and it hurts just to hear it. I have to take time off to go back next month, which means more medical bills to face.

Why did I even post that status?

Now I know—I was trying to prove I still have depth. I was saying, "Look, I’m not just a guy who drinks and chats; I’m capable of reflection." But even that is a lie. The real me is exactly that guy who drinks and chats, and now I’m on the verge of losing even that.

I have to see a client tomorrow. I have to smile, be enthusiastic, and act like everything is fine.

I’m tired. But I’m not allowed to be.

— Shen Linchuan

March 12, 2023, past 11:00 PM

The stuff from the court is still spinning in my head.

Lao Li’s comment was meant as a joke—"You’re turning into a real family man"—a jab I’ve heard countless times before. Usually, I would’ve fired back with a joke of my own, changed the subject, and kept pretending everything was fine. But today, I didn’t. I just looked him in the eye and said, "I don't see that as a loss."

The moment the words left my mouth, even I was a little stunned.

It wasn't actually a sudden thought. I’ve been thinking about it for days; I just hadn’t said it out loud. I didn’t dare say it at home, fearing Wang Xiaomei would think I was hinting at something. I didn’t dare say it at the office, fearing my subordinates would think their Director had lost his edge. But on the court, maybe because I’d been playing all afternoon and was relaxed, it just slipped out.

I explained a few things to Lao Li. I told him I’ve realized I’ve been chasing things that maybe I never even wanted. Promotions, networking, being the one with the loudest jokes at dinner parties... those things used to make me feel successful, but looking back now, what were they really?

Over the past few months, I’ve realized that the things I truly care about have shifted.

When I went back to my hometown last month, Mom was sitting in her wheelchair, and I combed her hair. Her hair is so thin and soft; I had to be incredibly careful. In that moment, I suddenly understood what it means to be "needed." Not the workplace kind of needed—"Director Shen, we need you to handle this"—but truly, someone feeling at peace simply because you are there. That’s a feeling fifteen years of toasting at high-end bars never gave me.

Xinyu has been different lately, too. I’ve stopped constantly trying to make her more outgoing and have started appreciating her quietness instead. Last weekend, we drove out to the suburbs. She was reading in the passenger seat, and I just drove. Neither of us said a word. That kind of companionship felt more real than any social event I’ve ever attended.

As for Wang Xiaomei... we still spend time on our own phones, but I’ve started noticing the details. Her patience when checking Xinyu’s homework, the way she keeps the house so organized on weekends. There might not be any grand, sweeping romance between us, but these everyday things feel quite warm now.

I don’t know if this is just a midlife realization, or if I’ve finally stopped fighting with myself.

But I do know one thing: I no longer need social achievements to prove my worth. My value is right here—in the companionship, in the responsibility, and in the small, real things I can give my family.

After Lao Li heard me out, he was silent for a while. Then he said, "Fair enough. Sounds like you’ve really figured it out."

I smiled. Maybe I really have.

Or rather, I’ve finally allowed myself to stop proving anything.

December 24, 2022, past 11:00 PM

I just stepped out of my daughter’s room. She’s already asleep.

At dinner tonight, I asked her what she’s been drawing lately. Just a simple question like that. In the past, I always asked about her grades, her ranking in the monthly exams, whether she was studying hard enough... She would always give short answers and keep her head down, focused on her food.

But today was different. She suddenly opened up, talking about an illustration series she’s working on regarding urban loneliness. Her eyes lit up; she spoke quickly, even looking at me a bit nervously to see if I was actually listening. I listened intently and told her I wanted to see her work.

She thanked me for asking about her drawing. She said it made her feel like I actually wanted to get to know *her*.

I almost burst into tears.

Just because I asked one question. Just because I didn’t ask about her grades.

I told her, "I realized I might have been too focused on your grades before. But now, I want to know more about you as a person."

I was a bit shocked at myself when those words came out. Because I finally understood—what I’ve been doing all this time. I’ve been applying the logic of "success" to my daughter. Networking, demonstrating competence, chasing achievements... these things made me a Marketing Director in the office, but at home, they were destroying the most authentic connection between me and my daughter.

Wang Xiaomei sat across from me, her eyes welling up. She didn't say anything; she just watched me and our daughter.

After dinner, my daughter took the initiative to pull me into her room to show me her drawings. They are truly good. There’s something in them that belongs only to her, something I had never seen before. How did I miss it until now?

On the golf course, I can remember every client’s name, their children’s ages, and their favorite wine. I can make ten strangers laugh at a dinner party. And yet, I had never truly looked at my own daughter’s art.

The irony is staggering.

Maybe that’s why I’ve been so exhausted lately. It’s not because of how busy work is, but because I’ve been playing a role. I play it in the office, I play it at dinners, and I was even playing it at home. It’s exhausting to perform.

But tonight, I wasn’t performing. I just sat there, listening to my daughter talk about her creations, looking at her art, and admitting my past regrets.

This feeling... it’s more fulfilling than any successful business negotiation.

I don’t know what this means for the future. But I know I don’t want to miss out anymore.

Maybe I’m finally starting to understand what real success actually is.

Just got back from the bar. Took a shower and now I’m sitting in the study; it’s quiet outside. Xiaomei is probably asleep, and the light in Xinyu’s room is out.

I don’t know how to start writing this. My head feels like a tangled mess, but I have to get it down, otherwise I’ll just have to keep pretending everything is fine tomorrow morning.

I shocked even myself the moment it came out. Those words, coming from my mouth? I never say things like that in front of friends. But once it was out, I couldn't stop—about the department merger, the budget cuts, the way that young new supervisor looks at me...

One friend said, "Don't overthink it, it's just temporary." I heard myself reply, "You don't understand. I'm 42. There’s no way back." In that moment, I truly felt something inside me shatter.

Later, I went to the restroom alone and stood in front of the mirror for a long time. The person in the mirror—hair thinning a bit, deep wrinkles at the corners of the eyes, a hollow look in the gaze. I repeated it once more: "42 years old, no way back." As if I were confirming something.

I’m wondering, what exactly am I afraid of? Am I afraid of people seeing that I’m actually not that great? Afraid of discovering that for the past 15 years, I’ve just been coasting on talk and a few connections? Afraid of realizing that besides drinking, chatting, and memorizing clients' family members' names, I really have nothing substantial to show for it?

When I got home, I glanced at Xinyu’s room. She’s been studying for the college entrance exams lately; her grades are still middle-of-the-pack. I want to cheer her on, but I’m afraid of putting pressure on her. What I actually want to say is—kid, don’t be like me. Don’t wait until you’re my age to realize you might have taken the wrong path. But I didn’t say it. What right do I have to say that?

Xiaomei was on her phone in the living room. We just nodded at each other and went to our separate rooms. It’s been a long time since we’ve had a real conversation.

Mom’s medical bills are due again. Linlin mentioned last time that Mom is having even more trouble getting around lately. Every time I go back to my hometown, I feel so powerless—what can I do? I can’t even take care of my own life.

I always thought I was hot stuff. At the company, I’m the enthusiastic one, the well-connected one, the one who gets things done. I’m always at the center of the conversation at every gathering. But what is all that? What does any of that guarantee?

Now, I don’t know.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll still go to the office as usual, smiling as I greet my colleagues, acting full of ideas in meetings. But I already know that’s not the real me. The real me is right here—sitting in the middle of the night, telling the mirror, "42 years old, no way back."

I’m so tired.

But I have to keep going. There’s no other way.

# Work Log - September 24, 2022

**Meeting Minutes: Q4 Marketing Plan Discussion**

Met with Zhang Hao this morning to discuss the first draft of his community marketing plan. The overall framework is solid, but there are glaring gaps in the execution details and data support—mainly, the logic behind budget allocation is unclear, and the target audience analysis is quite superficial.

In the past, I probably would have just taken it over, rewritten it myself, and handed it back. But I didn't do that today.

I asked him, "Where do you think these numbers came from?" As he started explaining, he caught the problem himself—he had used last year’s data without considering this year’s market shifts. We went over the recent transaction records together, and he gradually realized that the audience positioning needed adjustment.

Later, he said, "If we change it this way, it’s going to be a lot of work." I replied, "Yes, but this way, you’ll know exactly what to do next time." He gave a small smile, as if he understood what I meant.

**Reflections:**

This process took me twice as long as usual. However, I found a different kind of satisfaction in doing it this way. It wasn't the typical "I solved the problem" rush, but rather the experience of watching him gradually discover the issues and think through the solutions on his own.

I told him honestly, "Helping you learn how to do this is more meaningful to me than just fixing it myself." Even I was a bit surprised by those words as they came out. But it’s the truth.

Maybe this is what I’ve been looking for—not proving how capable I am, but genuinely helping the people around me grow. It sounds a bit idealistic, but in that meeting room, I truly felt it.

I asked him to reorganize the plan this afternoon and submit it on Monday. I will review it thoroughly, but I will no longer do the thinking for him.

---

*Note to self for next time: Management isn't about being the fastest; it's about enabling the team to do the work.*

# Monday, September 5, 2022

I didn't sleep well last night. It wasn't insomnia, but a strange kind of alertness—lying in bed with yesterday’s scenes playing on a loop in my head.

When I called Mom, she asked, "You sound different. Did something happen?" Just that one sentence, and I suddenly burst into tears. Sitting there in my office, crying into my phone. In that moment, I realized I hadn't truly talked to her in a long time. Every call had been me reporting in—how work was going, Xinyu’s grades, how many rounds of golf I’d played lately. It was like delivering a performance review. And Mom could hear it.

I told her everything that’s been going on. No hiding, no sugarcoating. I told her what my daughter said to me in the car. I told her about that feeling of being seen right through. Mom listened until I finished, then stayed silent for a long time. Finally, she said, "Maybe sometimes, you could just slow down."

Such a simple sentence. Yet it felt like something I’d never heard before.

I’ve been trying to prove something. That thought spun in my head all night. Proving I’m successful, proving I’m capable, proving I’m a good father, a good son. Using my achievements, my title, my connections, those business dinners and golf games. Subconsciously, I’ve even been using Xinyu’s grades to prove myself. She was right—I’m always trying to prove something.

But to whom?

When my daughter put her hand on my shoulder, she wasn't asking me to prove anything. When Mom asked, "You sound different," she wasn't either. They just saw through me, and then they accepted me. It’s a foreign feeling.

I don’t know what this means. I don’t know how I’m supposed to change. Lying in bed last night, I thought back to the department merger in 2021. I stayed silent at home for days back then because I felt my value had been diminished. Looking back now, that fear has always been there—the fear of losing the things that "prove" who I am. The fear that without the title, the network, and the shiny achievements, I am nothing.

Maybe I am nothing. And maybe that’s okay.

But I’m still not quite ready to believe that.

When I got up this morning, Wang Xiaomei had already left for school. Xinyu was still asleep. I sat in the living room with a cup of tea, realizing it had been a long time since I’d just sat quietly like this. No phone, no plans, nothing to prove.

Just sitting.

It felt a bit surreal.

***

I need to have a real talk with Xinyu. Not about grades, not about the future. Just... a conversation. To listen to what she has to say. Maybe I should tell her that I’ve seen through myself. That I feel guilty for pushing my anxiety onto her.

But I don’t know how to say it yet.

# June 18, 2022 23:47

Can’t sleep. Again.

My phone is still on. That forum thread has a dozen more replies. I should have deleted my account, but I didn’t. I keep reading those comments over and over, like some form of self-torture—"Nope, just accept your fate," "Buddy, at your age, stop overthinking it," "Same here, I’ve already given up"...

Why did I even post that? What was I thinking?

Anonymity is supposed to be anonymous, yet I still feel exposed. It’s as if someone is holding a lamp, shining it into the darkest corners of my life. 42 years old, career hitting a wall, heavy family pressure, utterly useless—I’ve nailed myself to a cross with those few words. Now I look at them as if I’m staring at a corpse.

My wife is already asleep. She’s been sleeping well lately, or maybe she just doesn't want to be awake to face me. It’s been a long time since we’ve had a real conversation. During dinner, I thought about Xinyu’s grades again and wanted to say something, but in the end, I just ate in silence. She glanced at me, and there was something in that look I can't quite put into words.

The shopping cart on my computer is still open. Sleeping pills, vitamins, *Overcoming Anxiety*, *A Midlife Crisis Survival Guide*... 1,200 yuan in total. They’ve been sitting there for three days, but I haven't clicked "order." Why? Do I really want to change? Or am I just lying to myself—*Look, I’m trying, I’m doing something.*

But I’m doing nothing.

It’s been almost a year since the department merger. Back then, I stayed silent at home for three days; my wife thought I was sick. I didn't tell her that my authority had been stripped, that I might have reached my dead end. I told myself it was fine, that I still had golf, friends, and a network. That those things were enough.

But then there was the photography thing...

I submitted my photos to a small exhibition. I thought... I really thought I had something special. I spent two months shooting those old neighborhoods. I thought it would prove something—prove that I’m not just a man who survives on smooth talk.

Rejected. Not even a single comment.

In that moment, I understood. I have no talent. I never did. I’m just a middle-aged man using passion and smiles as a mask, and that mask is peeling away bit by bit.

My mother’s medical bills went up again this month. Sister Li said her recovery isn't going well and she might need more rehab sessions. I visit her every month, and every time I feel powerless. She sits in her wheelchair, her eyes still clear, but her body is no longer hers. I should do something, but I can’t do anything. Except pay.

What can I do?

I really can’t do anything.

Maybe those people in the replies are right. Just accept it. This is my life now. A failed middle-aged man, a stagnant career, a crumbling home life, even my dreams get slapped in the face by reality.

...

I don’t know why I’m writing this. No one’s going to read it anyway.

I have to go to the office tomorrow. I’ll have to chat with my subordinates with a smile and attend social dinners. I’ll act as if nothing happened. Because I’m very good at that—pretending everything is fine.

But tonight, in this place where no one is watching, I admit it.

I’m really tired.

Team meeting at 2:00 PM.

I originally planned to stick to the old routine—me leading the direction, everyone else nodding along. But sitting there in the conference room, I suddenly lost interest. Maybe it’s because I’ve been exhausted lately, or maybe I’m just fed up.

I decided to switch things up. Instead of throwing out a proposal right at the start, I just asked everyone: "Where do you see the pain points in this project? What are your thoughts?"

The room went silent for a few seconds. Usually, at moments like this, everyone waits for me to speak.

But this time was different. Xiao Wang spoke up first, sharing some thoughts on the client's requirements—they were actually quite practical. Then Xiao Li followed up with some additions. In the end, even Sister Zhang, who is usually the quietest, chimed in.

The whole time, I just listened. Occasionally, I’d ask, "Why do you think that?" and then go back to listening.

No interruptions, no rushing to correct them.

By the end, they had mapped out a pretty solid approach on their own. It was actually better than the plan I had in mind.

I took some meeting notes; nothing out of the ordinary. It’s just that the quality of the discussion seemed higher this time. The team's engagement felt different.

I don’t know if it’s just my imagination.

At dinner, I sat at the table and took a bite of food, suddenly unsure of how to start. Wang Xiaomei was across from me, looking down at her phone, while Xinyu was in her room doing her homework.

I set down my chopsticks.

"Xiaomei, there’s something I want to tell you..."

She looked up, a hint of wariness in her eyes. It had been too long since we’d started a conversation this way.

"During a meeting today, a subordinate brought up an idea. My instinct was to shut it down immediately, but instead, I just asked him, 'Why do you think this is the right direction?' Just a simple question like that."

I paused, not quite knowing how to go on.

"Then he told me, 'Director, today’s meeting felt different. You were actually listening to us.'"

I looked her in the eyes. "I realized that I might have been faking it all along. Using enthusiasm to mask the fact that I wasn't truly listening."

She didn’t respond right away; she just watched me.

"I don’t know what it means yet, but... I think I’m starting to understand something."

I picked up my chopsticks again but didn't continue eating. The room was quiet, save for the scratching of Xinyu’s pen against paper.

"I just wanted you to know."

I didn't expect her to say anything. Maybe she would go back to her phone, or maybe she would ask for more. But at least, for once, I wasn't pretending that everything was just fine.

# Phone Memo

**Time: October 21, 2022, 22:47**

---

What Xinyu said on the highway keeps playing over and over in my head.

She asked me, "Dad, are you really tired too?" I wanted to say no, I wanted to say I could handle everything, but the words got stuck in my throat. In the end, all I could manage was, "Yeah, Dad’s tired too."

She didn’t say anything else, just looked back down at her phone. But I could feel her relax a little.

It’s as if... she doesn't need me to pretend to be invincible.

The thought feels a bit strange. I always believed that’s what a father should be—always having a solution, always stable, always providing a shoulder for his daughter to lean on. My dad was like that when I was in high school; I never saw him frown, no matter how hard things got. I’ve been trying to follow in his footsteps.

But it seems that’s not what Xinyu needs most.

She needs to see a real person, not a persona.

—

Mom’s medical bills are going up again this month. Sister Li mentioned they might need to add physical therapy. Linlin is holding things down back home by herself; I go back once a month, but it never feels like enough. Things at the office have ramped up since the merger, too—the way my boss looks at me has changed.

With everything piling up, sometimes I really don’t know how much longer I can hold on.

But maybe this is just who I am right now. Not the man who always has a plan. Just a 42-year-old middle-aged man, juggling caring for his mother, being there for his daughter, grinding away at work, and maintaining all those social dinners and connections. Sometimes I’m exhausted to the point of collapse, and sometimes I’m scared.

Maybe being seen by my daughter for who I am is more important than being idolized by her.

—

Back at it tomorrow.

# March 25, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Lights out, lying in bed. Wang Xiaomei has been asleep for a while; her breathing is steady and even. I’m staring at the ceiling, and that one sentence keeps circling in my head.

"Director, today’s meeting felt different. You were actually listening to us."

When Xiao Zhang said that, I froze for a second. They were all looking at me with this look... it wasn't flattery, and it wasn't avoidance. It was just... the feeling of being seen?

Fuck. I suddenly realized something that cuts deep.

I always thought I was listening. I nod during meetings, I remember their names, I ask "what do you guys think," and I pick up the tab at dinners. I thought that was listening. But in reality... I’ve never truly listened. I’ve just been performing the role of a "good boss." I’ve been using enthusiasm and networking to mask the fact that I never actually knew what they were thinking.

Fifteen years. I’ve spent fifteen years coasting like this. Using smiles, memorizing people’s stories, and maintaining this "I care about you" posture. But I never actually stopped to understand *why* someone thought a certain way, instead of rushing to agree or disagree.

During today's meeting, when Xiao Li proposed that risky plan, I didn't immediately say, "That won't work." I just asked, "Why do you think this is the right direction?" And then... I actually listened until he finished. No interrupting, no thinking about how to respond—just listening.

When he finished, the entire atmosphere in the conference room changed.

I’m only realizing now that I’ve been lying to myself all these years. I thought I was a good person because I’m warm, because I have connections, because I don’t nitpick over small things. But I’ve never truly looked at them. I was only looking at myself—checking if I was likable enough, if I was successful enough.

What Mom said to me that day hurts even more now. She said, "You’re always running." I cried at the time, but I didn't fully understand what she meant. Now I think I do. I’ve been running so fast, using enthusiasm, social obligations, and smiles just to keep myself from stopping to see who I really am.

Maybe... maybe this is what I need to change. But I don't know where to start.

I’ve given up on the idea of switching jobs, because I know that no matter where I go, I’ll be taking this problem with me. Maybe this is just who I am. Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along.

But maybe... maybe this time, I can try not to.

It’s just... I really don't know how.

# Diary

September 1, 2022, past 11:00 PM

I bought this notebook half a month ago, and it’s been sitting in my office drawer ever since. Today, I finally opened it. I’m not sure how to begin.

Maybe like this.

Lately, I’ve been stuck on a question—what exactly have I been trying to prove?

Last week, while driving to the outskirts with Xinyu, she said something out of the blue from the passenger seat. She said I’m "always trying to prove something." I didn't catch her meaning at first and even wanted to argue. But later, when we pulled over at a service area, I cried. Alone in the car, the tears just came.

What am I trying to prove?

That I’m a success? That I’m worthy of being seen? Or... that I’m a good father?

But it feels like I’ve pushed all my anxiety onto her. Her grades, how outgoing she is, whether she fits the image of a "daughter" I have in my head. I haven't even truly looked at her.

At dinner tonight, I didn't scroll through my phone like I usually do. I asked her what she was reading. She looked up, a bit surprised. We talked for half an hour. I didn't actually catch everything she said, but that didn't matter. What mattered was that I was listening.

Wang Xiaomei glanced at me with a look that seemed to say... *finally*.

I want to change. But even in this change, I find myself performing. It’s ironic. I can feel myself "acting like a better father," "becoming more introspective." It’s as if the change itself has become another performance.

Maybe that’s what I need to admit. I don’t know how to stop performing. I don’t even know who I am once the act stops.

But tonight, sitting here writing these words, I feel a strange sense of peace. Not because everything is resolved. Quite the opposite—the problems are still there. My mother’s health, the bottleneck at work, the distance between Xiaomei and me... it’s all still there.

But I’m not running away anymore.

Maybe that’s enough. Maybe value doesn't need to be proven at all. Maybe I can just be... a real person. Someone who gets scared, gets tired, and feels uncertain.

That day, Xinyu put her hand on my shoulder. She didn't say anything; she just left it there.

Thinking back on it now still makes me want to cry.

I’ll continue tomorrow. I don’t know what I’ll write, but at least... I’m writing.

After gathering my thoughts, I decided to message Lao Wang, one of my closer friends from the golf circle. We’ve known each other for over a decade; he’s done business and understands the corporate world, so he should be able to relate to my current situation.

qa_items.question: Mr. Shen, I’ve noticed your attitude toward all those business dinners and "vanity projects" has changed quite a bit since the beginning of the year. You seem a lot more relaxed now—not as wound up as you used to be, right?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? At the beginning of the year, I was honestly a bit obsessed. I felt like I had to keep toasting people and cracking jokes just to maintain that "President Shen" persona, but deep down, I was incredibly insecure—to the point where I didn't even dare join my friends for a get-together after playing ball. Lately, I’ve been consciously letting myself loosen up. I’ve stopped trying to play the "man who can do it all" and started actually listening to what others have to say. Honestly, this feels much more grounded than that forced enthusiasm I used to put on.

evidence.statement: Shen Linchuan appeared exceptionally warm and talkative at the dinner, frequently raising his glass for toasts. However, his colleagues could sense he was "putting on a performance"—a classic sign of him overcompensating for his inner anxiety.

Instead of joining his teammates at the bar after the game as he usually did, Shen Linchuan excused himself, claiming he was tired. He drove through the city alone—a deliberate retreat from the social scene that reflected his growing exhaustion with maintaining his public persona.

When a subordinate proposed a risky idea, Shen Linchuan didn't dismiss it immediately. Instead, he approached it with genuine curiosity, asking, "Why do you believe this is the right direction?" This marked his transformation from superficial enthusiasm to authentic listening.

choices.text: Actually, it was attending a few funerals for people my own age that really hit me hard. I used to feel like I had to keep spinning like a top, thinking success meant wowing the crowd at high-end forums. Now, I’d much rather spend my time pruning the bonsai in my yard or fishing by the river all afternoon. This quiet solitude has made me realize that there aren't actually that many people watching me; there’s no need to keep myself wound so tight.

This sense of being grounded makes me feel so much lighter.

qa_items.question: Over the past two months, seeing the ups and downs in your relationship with Xinyu, looking back on this time, have you had any new realizations about what it means to be a good father?
qa_items.answer: Well… how can I put it? These past two months have been a total rollercoaster. I used to think being a father meant shielding my daughter from everything and pointing her in the right direction, but it turns out all my "doing it for her own good" anxiety was actually just weighing her down. It wasn't until I broke down and cried in front of her that I realized she’s far more perceptive and gentle than I ever imagined. Now, I’ve learned to just shut up and listen. We went for a drive to the countryside the other day, and as I listened to her talk about her dreams of painting, I realized I don’t need to fill the silence. As long as I’m there supporting her, she shines on her own. This feeling of being "truly seen" by my daughter feels so much more grounded than the "successful father" persona I used to put on.

evidence.statement: After the conversation, Shen Xinyu lowered her head to eat, a glimmer of hurt in her eyes. Shen Linchuan’s dismissive words had wounded his daughter, yet his response was continued coldness and avoidance—he turned to his phone to escape his wife’s questioning.

Shen Xinyu took the initiative to ask, "Dad, are you unhappy?" breaking Shen Linchuan's long-standing mask of silence. For the first time, instead of continuing to hide his feelings, he spoke candidly to his daughter: "Yes, Dad hasn't been very happy lately."
Shen Linchuan drove his daughter to the outskirts. This time, instead of trying to fill the silence or change her, he truly listened as she shared her thoughts on painting and her dreams.

choices.text: To be honest, I’m deeply moved. I used to be so busy socializing and networking, thinking that as long as I brought home enough money, I was fulfilling my responsibilities. In reality, I was ignoring the family's most genuine needs. Over the past few months, I’ve turned down many unnecessary drinking sessions and started trying to truly be a part of her life. I remember her birthday last month—I cooked a meal for her myself. Even though the food tasted average, we sat and talked for a long, long time that night. I finally realized that what a child wants isn't some lofty material security, but the simple, grounded feeling of sitting down together for a home-cooked meal. This sense of returning to the purity of family makes me feel more successful than being a director ever could.

Actually, the biggest change in me lately is learning how to "let go." I used to feel like I had to arrange everything for her, even going as far as privately checking in with her teacher about her social circle. It wasn't until I stumbled upon her notebooks filled with future plans that I realized she is actually more independent and strong-willed than I am. Now, I no longer force the idea of getting into a top-tier university on her. A while ago, I took her to a youth art salon, and watching her converse confidently with those artists, I realized that as a father, the best role I can play isn't a coach, but an audience member who hands her a warm cup of tea whenever she needs it.

I’ve been reflecting a lot over the past two months and realized that I used to care far too much about "saving face." This honesty has actually brought us closer. It made me realize that a father doesn't always have to be an infallible superman; being an empathetic friend is far more important.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this... That day, Xinyu suddenly asked me if I was unhappy, and she even said she was scared her mom and I would get a divorce. It felt like a bolt from the blue. I always thought I was hiding it so well, but it turns out I’ve let my daughter carry all that fear. At that moment, I just broke down and started crying right in front of her. I realized that if I kept toughing it out and continued this cold war with Xiaomei, this family would truly fall apart. That’s why I decided to stop trying to hold everything up on my own and try to tell them the truth.

When I got home, I flipped through her photo albums from the past few years and realized just how much I had missed. At that moment, I couldn't hold it in any longer. Coming home to see the liver supplements Xiaomei left for me and the "keep going" sticker Xinyu put on the fridge, it hit me that this overexertion wasn't just torturing me—it was torturing my family, too. Now, I’m trying to slow my pace and stop forcing myself to be the "key player." Ironically, I feel much more at peace.

To be honest, this shift has been a bit of a reluctant one. When I first took over those two new projects in the sales department at the beginning of the year, I was truly determined to turn things around. I’d finish a cigarette in the garage every day before heading upstairs, just to get myself into the right headspace for being "Mr. Shen." But eventually, I realized that no matter how hard I tried to review the data and follow up, the logic of the market had fundamentally changed. Especially after that big client’s proposal got shot down recently, coupled with Xinyu opening up to me at dinner about how lost she feels about the future—seeing her look so helpless made me suddenly feel that the "survival of the fittest" theory I’d always clung to was actually quite hypocritical. Now, I’ve stopped obsessing over that VP title and started learning to discuss the family’s financial planning with Xiaomei. I’m no longer trying to carry the world on my shoulders, and honestly, it feels a lot less suffocating.

I’ve pretty much come to terms with it now. I’ve stopped forcing myself to play the part of the "successful man." Even though there’s still a lingering sense of powerlessness, at least I don’t have to act all day anymore.

qa_items.question: Lao Shen, over the past six months or so, I’ve watched you go from "toughing it out" while juggling your mother’s care and a career bottleneck, to now appearing so much more at peace. How did you navigate this internal journey?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this... To be honest, at the very beginning, I truly felt like I was on the verge of a breakdown. I was 42, and looking at myself in a bar restroom mirror, the words "no way out" were all I could think about. I couldn't sleep for nights on end. Then, I happened to call Sister Li, the nurse looking after my mother, and she told me that my mom had always been proud of me. In that instant, my eyes just welled up. I suddenly realized that the "success" I had spent the first half of my life desperately trying to prove didn't actually matter that much to my family. Now, even though the problems are still there, I’ve stopped fighting with myself. Being able to go back to my hometown more often to be with my mom—it’s given me a sense of peace I didn't have before.

Shen Linchuan's voice choked up over the phone as he promised, "I'll come home to see her more often"—a shift from a sense of helplessness to a proactive commitment.

qa_items.question: Lao Shen, think back to that night when Xinyu asked if you were happy. If you had just brushed her off with your usual "it's nothing," what do you think your relationship would be like now?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, looking back, I sometimes feel a lingering sense of dread. If I had still been wearing that "Marketing Director" mask that day, Xinyu might have truly kept her fear of us getting a divorce buried inside forever. If that had happened, the distance between us would have only grown, leaving her to suffer in silent anxiety while I continued to sink deeper into my midlife crisis. It was only because I stopped trying to tough it out and let her see my vulnerability that we finally began to truly connect. That kind of heart-to-heart is worth far more than any business dinner or sales target.

When his daughter said, "I'm afraid you guys will get divorced," Shen Linchuan felt a massive jolt. He realized that his personal crisis had begun to cause real harm to her, and this sparked the motivation he needed to change.

If I had actually gone down that path, I might never have known what was truly on her mind. That kind of "care" bought with money would have only turned us into complete strangers living under the same roof.

That invisible wall between us would likely have stayed standing until the day I died.

qa_items.question: Lin Chuan, you’ve been pushing yourself through all these social obligations for the better part of the year, and you’ve gone through so much upheaval in between. Do you feel like your perspective on "success" has changed from what it used to be?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? These past six months have been a real wake-up call for me. I used to think success meant being the life of the party, a "smooth operator" in everyone’s eyes, so I kept the mask on and played the part, no matter how panicked I felt inside. But after everything that happened—especially seeing my mom’s illness and Xinyu’s tears—I realized how powerless that "passion" of mine really was in the face of reality. Now, I feel that allowing myself to live authentically, even if it means admitting I’m tired or that I’ve failed, is more important than any empty reputation—as long as I can hold my family’s hands.

He mechanically muttered, "Understood, I'll be more mindful," before turning to leave—his hands trembling and his eyes hollow throughout the entire ordeal.

Shen Linchuan shed tears and said "I'm sorry" in front of his daughter—this was the first time he abandoned his persona as the "always-smiling marketing director," marking the moment he began allowing himself to reveal his true, vulnerable self to his loved ones.

But this series of unexpected events made me realize that you can't really fight against fate. I’m no longer fixated on grand goals. To me, success now is about maintaining a sense of inner peace. Being able to have a warm meal on time, having the energy to read a few books I love, and no longer being led by the nose by cluttered desires—that is what I’m after now.

To be honest, I used to think that success was all about having enough resources and connections, which is why I worked so hard to maintain all those relationships. But the upheavals of the past six months have made me realize that circles built on mutual interest are actually the most fragile. Now, I’ve come to believe that true success is having a "hard skill"—something you’re genuinely good at that no one can take away from you. Even if life is a bit more modest, as long as I have peace of mind and don't have to go around begging for favors just to meet some performance targets, that, to me, is the ultimate liberation.

Sigh, I’ve really gained a lot of perspective over the past six months or so. Now, I care more about the accumulation of self-worth rather than feeling the need to prove myself to anyone.

qa_items.question: I see you’ve finally started opening up to Xiaomei and your daughter. Looking back at those months when you carried everything on your own shoulders and just sat staring into the darkness—how has your mindset changed since then?
qa_items.answer: Ugh, don't even get me started. Back then, I was really stuck in a rut, just overthinking everything. I felt like I had to be the pillar of the family and keep up this facade of being "successful," but all it did was turn me into a ticking time bomb. I almost pushed the people closest to me away. I’ve finally come to my senses now. Admitting that there are things I can't handle and acknowledging the limitations that come with middle age is actually much more of a relief than trying to tough it out. Even though my situation hasn't changed, I’m no longer just stewing in my own frustration. That feeling of "actually being present" has given me a real sense of peace.

qa_items.question: Brother Chuan, I still remember that WeChat post back in February that you hesitated over and eventually deleted. If you hadn't hit delete and had actually posted that question—"What am I actually good at?"—do you think people would have laughed at you, or would you have received some unexpected support?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’ve thought about this quite a few times. I even had my finger hovering over the "post" button, but I chickened out in the end. I’ve always felt that in everyone’s eyes, Shen Linchuan has to be that all-powerful "President Shen" who’s always in control. If I actually put those midlife crisis thoughts out there, I bet a lot of people would think I’d lost it or that I was being unprofessional. But looking back now, maybe a few old buddies would’ve sent me a DM saying, "Hey Shen, I feel the same way." After all, we’re all grinding through this stage of life; who hasn't had moments of self-doubt? It’s just that I’ve always been so obsessed with saving face—I’m terrified that once that mask is ripped off, I’ll never be able to put it back on.
