qa_items.question: Linchuan, I’ve noticed how down you’ve been since your mother’s condition took a turn for the worse back in June. But lately, you seem different. What was it that made you decide to snap out of that sense of helplessness and truly step up to take on the responsibility of caring for her?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, that sense of powerlessness almost crushed me. Back in my hometown in early June, seeing my mother unable to move the right side of her body or even speak clearly, I felt like an absolute failure. It wasn't until I heard my sister, Shen Lin, say she was at her breaking point that I suddenly snapped out of it. I realized that if even I gave in to self-blame and avoidance, this family would truly fall apart. My career might be at a standstill, and my photography projects are being outperformed by the younger generation, but I’ve come to realize that for my mom, simply being there to hold her hand and talk to her is where my greatest value lies right now. So, I’ve decided to stop obsessing over titles and proving myself. For now, I just need to be the pillar this family needs.

evidence.statement: Shen Linchuan saw that his mother's condition had clearly worsened—her speech was slurred, and the mobility in her right limbs had declined. "I'm really reaching my breaking point, Shen Lin," his sister said, her voice heavy with exhaustion and resignation. "Mom needs round-the-clock care now, and it's starting to take a toll on my job." It was a subtle cry for help. But as Shen Linchuan listened to her words, only one thought flashed through his mind: "I'm reaching my breaking point, too."

qa_items.answer: So you caught me... To be honest, sometimes I feel like that car is the only place where I can actually catch my breath. At the office, I have to be the "ever-enthusiastic Mr. Shen"; at home, I’m faced with Xiaomei’s nagging and my daughter’s silence. It’s only during those thirty minutes, sitting in the car with the engine off, staring blankly at the steering wheel, that I don’t have to play a role. It’s a complicated feeling—knowing that home is right upstairs, yet being unable to move. It’s as if as long as I don’t open that car door, all those mid-life anxieties can’t quite catch up to me.

Instead of heading to the bar with his teammates after the game as he usually did, Shen Linchuan excused himself, claiming he was tired. He drove around the city alone—a deliberate retreat from the social scene that reflected his growing exhaustion with maintaining his public persona.

It feels like stolen freedom—brief, but at least in that moment, I’m not anyone’s subordinate, and I’m not anyone’s son.

It’s not that I’m trying to avoid anyone; it’s just that I’ve been smoking heavily lately, and I don’t want the smoke to bother the kids at home. qa_items.question: Linchuan, I’ve noticed you seem to have a pattern at social events: you’re incredibly high-energy for the first half, but once the drinks have been flowing for a while, you just shut down and spend ages staring at your phone in silence. Is it because keeping up that "Director" persona just wears you out?
qa_items.answer: You saw right through me... To be honest, sometimes I really feel like I’m just putting on an act. Making toasts, cracking jokes—it works, it gets the room going—but in the middle of the performance, this sudden sense of emptiness just hits me. Especially now that I’m in my forties; with the shake-ups at the office and the constant pressure of taking care of both the kids and my parents, the anxiety can just wash over me like a tide right when things are at their liveliest. I end up just staring down at my phone, hiding in that tiny screen for a moment. Nobody realizes that the "Director Shen" everyone sees—the life of the party—is actually panicking inside.

Shen Linchuan got dead drunk at the dinner party. He went from being the life of the party to passively playing along, before finally falling into a total silence that required his colleagues to help him into the car—a clear sign that his emotional self-control had utterly collapsed.

How come lately, even when we’re just hanging out with the guys or watching a game, you look like you’re struggling just to force a smile?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, I guess there’s really no hiding anything from you. Back when the departments merged at the beginning of the year, I was panicking inside, but I felt I had to keep up the whole "Director Shen" persona—it was all for show. But lately, everything at work and at home has just piled up. My mother’s health is failing, and Xinyu’s college entrance exam results are a real gut-punch. I’ve realized my social battery is completely drained. That night at the bar watching the game, sitting there among my old friends, I suddenly felt this overwhelming sense of emptiness. Even the corners of my mouth felt like they were weighted with lead; I just can’t put on the act anymore.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, I guess I’ve finally hit my breaking point. I used to think a father had to be a pillar of strength—someone who stands tall even if the sky is falling. So, even when I was pacing the room at midnight, sick with worry, I’d still put on an act for Xinyu, pretending to be that invincible dad. But lately, with the demotion at work, my mother’s serious illness, and then the issues with Xinyu’s grades, all the pressure hit me at once as if they’d planned it. I realized that my knack for "enthusiastic socializing" is completely useless against all this. Looking into her worried eyes at that moment, I felt like I just didn't have the energy to keep up the act anymore. That bone-deep sense of powerlessness... I really couldn't hide it anymore.

qa_items.question: Brother Shen, looking back at the ups and downs you’ve faced both at work and at home over the past year, what does that "social butterfly" label you’ve always been so proud of mean to you now?
qa_items.answer: Sigh... to be honest, that title used to be my greatest source of confidence, but now it feels more like a mask I can’t peel off. This past year, I’ve been pushing myself to the limit—toasting people at dinners, cracking jokes—but inside, I was terrified, constantly feeling like I was just putting on an act. It wasn't until that day I sat in my car and broke down in tears in front of my daughter that I realized something: instead of being the Marketing Director who’s always "on" and working the room, I’d much rather be a regular dad who can actually tell his family, "I'm tired." All those connections made over drinks couldn't save me from my anxiety, but finally giving myself permission to "mess up" once in a while? That’s what actually gave me some peace of mind.

qa_items.answer: I used to think being a good man meant being like a superhero—no matter how rough the storms were outside, I had to come home with a bright smile and keep all the mess at bay. But it wasn't until the day I broke down in front of Xinyu and she took my hand that it finally hit me: true responsibility isn't about pretending to be invincible; it’s about being truly "present." More than an always-right but cold Marketing Director, what they really need is a real person—someone who shares their anxieties, someone who gets tired, and someone who knows how to apologize. Now I feel that being honest about my own powerlessness has actually given our family a chance to grow close again.

qa_items.question: Linchuan, if a buddy around your age—someone currently suffocating under the weight of work and family drama—came to you for a drink and wanted to know how you pushed through that sense of powerlessness, what would you say to him?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this… I’d start by having a drink with him. After all, it’s taken me the last six months to slowly crawl out of that feeling like the sky was falling. I used to think I was well-connected and silver-tongued, like I could handle anything. But when the company restructuring, my mother’s illness, and my daughter’s failing grades all hit at once, I realized just how powerless I actually was. Back then, I spent night after night lying awake, thinking this was as good as my life was ever going to get; I started to just "resign myself to fate." But now, my mindset is much steadier. I’ve learned to accept that helplessness and stopped forcing myself to change the unchangeable. I’d tell him: don’t keep yourself wound so tight. Admitting that you’re a "failure" once in a while isn't that scary. For us middle-aged folks, life actually becomes livable once you embrace your own ordinariness.

In the end, I decided not to submit my resume.

A former boss of mine, who used to mentor me, started his own consulting firm and reached out the other day to see if I’d be interested in joining him. Although the role offers a lot of authority and a broad scope of responsibility, the compensation package is too heavily weighted toward commissions, with a base salary lower than what I’m making now. After thinking it over, I felt the risk was just too high, so I didn't dare take him up on the offer.

I did spend a long time eyeing several senior management positions at some education and training companies, even going as far as printing out their job descriptions to study them in detail. But when I finally sat down to tailor my resume, I realized their requirements explicitly stated "under 35." With my age being what it is, I wouldn't even make it past the automated screening. All I could do was look on with envy.

qa_items.question: Linchuan, it’s been a tough six months for you. Back at the start of the year, you were always cooped up in your car by yourself, but now you’re willing to open up to Xiaomei and the kids. What do you feel has been the biggest shift in your mindset?
qa_items.answer: Honestly, I used to think a man had to be like "Superman"—carrying everything on his shoulders and desperately maintaining that "all-powerful director" persona. But after everything I’ve been through these past six months, I’ve realized that "pretending everything is fine" is actually the most exhausting part. Last month, when I broke down in tears in front of Xinyu in the car, I suddenly felt a sense of release. Admitting that I get tired and scared actually made me feel more grounded, and for the first time, I could truly hear what my family was saying.

He mechanically muttered, "Understood, I'll be more mindful," before turning to leave—his hands trembling and his eyes hollow throughout the entire ordeal.

But over the past six months or so, I’ve had a change of heart. Last week, I took the initiative to grab a drink with an old comrade-in-arms and just let it all out. What he said really woke me up: nobody is made of steel. Now, I no longer push Xiaomei away when she tries to help with things around the house. Having someone to share the load makes me feel like this hurdle isn't so impossible to clear after all.

I feel like I’ve rediscovered a sense of "insensitivity." I used to be incredibly self-conscious, overanalyzing every look or comment someone threw my way. Then I started working out; last month, I kept up a running routine for over two weeks. In the process of sweating it out, I realized that the workplace politics and cold shoulders don't actually matter. Now, I can face the gossip and rumors with a calm mind. I’m no longer living just to prove myself to others. This feeling of being in control of my own rhythm has, ironically, made me feel more powerful than ever before.

Actually, I’ve just learned to find my inner peace. I used to think that success meant making a fortune and standing out from the crowd, so I was constantly obsessed with performance metrics, turning myself into nothing more than a cold, hard number. Now, I’ve learned to draw a line between work and life. After work, I turn off my phone and focus on building LEGO sets with Xinyu. That long-lost sense of tranquility has made me realize that the true essence of life lies in the ordinary.

qa_items.question: Lao Shen, looking back at the ups and downs of this past period, I remember how hopeless you felt when things went sideways at work—you even felt like there was no turning back at 42. Now that things have settled down, do you have any new perspectives on those words, "success" or "resigning to fate"?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this... Looking at myself in that bar mirror back then, it really felt like the world was ending. But after everything I’ve been through—especially that day when Mr. Wang chewed me out in front of everyone and I didn't even have the energy to push back—I’ve actually come to terms with it all. Maybe I really have "accepted my fate" now, admitting that I’m just an ordinary middle-aged guy with no great talents. But I’ve realized that as long as I can protect my daughter, my wife, and my sick mother back home, and not mess up in those roles, that’s its own kind of "success" for me. Honestly, I don't feel so suffocated anymore.

At the very least, I’ve reclaimed a sense of control over my own life.

qa_items.question: Hey, Linchuan, looking back to June when you were browsing those sleeping pills and self-help books online—if you had actually gone through with the order and bought them, do you think that summer would have felt any different?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I was really struggling back then. I remember staring at that 1,200-yuan shopping cart for the longest time. Looking back, even if I had actually bought those supplements and books, it probably would have just been for peace of mind. After all, that sense of powerlessness—that feeling of wanting to change but not believing I actually could—isn't something a few bottles of vitamins or a couple of self-help books can fix. At the time, I felt like everything was spiraling out of control, but what I really lacked wasn't medicine; it was the courage to face reality. I’m just glad I didn't let myself get completely sucked into that vicious cycle.

I kept feeling that if someone could just give me a concrete plan and tell me exactly what to do each day, I wouldn't have been so paralyzed with indecision in front of all those books and medications. That urge to escape was really just a desperate search for some breathing room, a way to get my brain, which was grinding to a halt, back up and running again.

choices.text: Ugh, don't even get me started. When I heard Xinyu say that, it felt like I’d been struck by lightning. But I forgot that children are the most sensitive of all; she saw right through our coldness and all those moments where we were just going through the motions. Seeing her trembling like that made me realize how incredibly selfish my avoidance has been. I can't let my midlife crisis keep draining my daughter's sense of security. So, that night, I didn't hide in the study. Instead, I sat Xiaomei down and poured out all the feelings of powerlessness I’d been bottling up for so long. We haven't solved everything yet, but at the very least, I don't want the two of them to keep suffering because of me.

That day, Xinyu suddenly pressed all the pocket money she had been saving for a long time into my hand. She whispered that if we didn't have enough money, she could stop her piano lessons—as long as we didn't separate. My heart ached intensely at that moment. I realized that my recent depression and neglect of the family had made my child feel so small and insecure. I saw that I had failed in my responsibility as a man. So, I turned off my phone and sat with Xiaomei on the balcony for a long time. We talked about the hardships we faced when we first got married, and we promised each other that no matter how tired we are in the future, we will always make time to communicate.

To be honest, I feel quite ashamed. It was only when I happened to stumble upon Xinyu’s diary that day that I saw it was filled with her fear of our cold war; she even wrote that she’d be willing to let her grades slip just to get our attention. At that moment, it hit me with a shock that my so-called "maintaining the status quo" was actually pushing my daughter into an abyss. I realized we couldn't go on ignoring each other like this, so I took the initiative to suggest a family camping trip. Inside the tent, for the first time, I opened up to Xiaomei about the frustrations I’ve faced in my career over the years, hoping that we could give our child a normal environment to grow up in.

Actually, that day Xinyu secretly drew a picture of the three of us standing back-to-back, and the sight of it stung me. I had always thought that as long as I sent money home on time and ensured they lived a comfortable life, I had fulfilled my responsibilities. But that drawing made me realize that what a child needs isn't a cold Marketing Director, but a father who can laugh with her. So, I canceled my business dinner that evening and made a point of buying Xiaomei's favorite flowers. qa_items.question: I’ve noticed lately that after social events or before coming inside, you often sit alone in the parking lot or in your car for a long time. What’s usually on your mind during those moments?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, you caught me... To be honest, sometimes I’m actually terrified of the moment I open the front door. Sitting in the car with the engine off, everything suddenly goes quiet, and my head just fills with all this mess: my awkward position at work after the department merger, my daughter’s mediocre grades, and my mother’s health back home. In those thirty minutes, I don’t have to play the role of the high-energy marketing director, and I don’t have to be anyone’s pillar of strength. I just want to completely zone out for a while, even if it’s just staring blankly at the steering wheel.

qa_items.question: Linchuan, I’ve noticed you have this habit—whenever your family or us guys check in on how you’re doing, you always brush it off with a "nothing's wrong" or "just a bit tired." Is it because you don't want everyone to worry, or do you just feel like talking about it won't change anything?
qa_items.answer: Sigh... honestly, it’s pretty embarrassing being seen through like this. It’s not that I think speaking up is useless; it’s just that after all these years, I’ve grown so used to being the one who "has it all under control." You see, I’m a director at the company and the pillar of my family—if even I admit something is wrong, what are Xiaomei and my daughter supposed to do? Sometimes when I can’t sleep and I’m just sitting on the sofa in the middle of the night, my daughter comes over to ask what’s wrong, and all I can say is that I’m just having trouble sleeping. To be honest, my mind is a mess. I’ve always felt that if I ever voiced it, this "mask" of mine would completely shatter. That sense of powerlessness... it really hurts.

But then, halfway through the excitement, you’ll suddenly go quiet and just stare at your phone. In those moments, do you feel incredibly drained, like you’ve just been putting on a "performance"?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, you saw right through me. To be honest, I’m just exhausted. That day at Jufu Restaurant, I went through five or six rounds of toasts until my throat was practically parched, but in that split second, I suddenly felt it was all so pointless. It was like being an actor on stage who suddenly forgets his lines and just wants to find a corner to hide in. I’m actually pretty panicked inside. I’m in my forties now; after the company restructuring, my authority was scaled back, and at home, Xinyu’s grades are a constant worry. I have no choice but to keep performing, acting as if as long as I remain the same enthusiastic Shen Linchuan, my life won’t come crashing down.

qa_items.question: Lao Shen, looking back at all the ups and downs of this past year—from the corporate restructuring at the start of the year until now—do you feel like your understanding of what it means to be 'middle-aged' has changed quite a bit?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? This past year has truly been a roller coaster. At the beginning of the year, I was still grinding away in front of the bar mirror, feeling like there was no turning back at forty-two and staying wound tight every day just to prove myself. It wasn't until that massive breakdown and cry in the car with Xinyu that it finally clicked: middle-aged people don't have to be "supermen." Admitting that you get tired and that you can fail is actually much lighter than lugging around that fake "Director" persona. Now, I’m much more willing to embrace the real me—the one who occasionally just can't get a handle on life.

qa_items.question: Brother Chuan, I’ve seen you go through a lot of ups and downs lately. If a guy your age was stuck in a career rut right now, with a mountain of family drama on top of it, and came to you to vent—how would you give him a hand?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’d probably start by giving him a pat on the shoulder and telling him that "accepting your fate" doesn't mean giving up—it means learning to embrace things as they are. I used to feel like at 42, there was no turning back; I had to keep up this "successful man" facade, and it ended up giving me chronic insomnia. It wasn't until I had a good cry in the car with my daughter a while back that I finally got it: at our age, allowing yourself to be an ordinary person who gets tired and scared is more important than anything else. Stop trying to fix everything. Just try opening up to your family—even if it’s just admitting you can't handle it all. Once you do, that weight on your chest will finally lift.

In that moment, I suddenly realized I wasn't alone. For people like us, the worst thing we can do is turn ourselves into an island. Once you strip away that layer of bravado and have a heart-to-heart with your buddies, you'll see that everyone is wading through the same mud. That sense of "we're in this together" is actually the most powerful thing there is.

Once your body starts moving, the knots in your head begin to untie. Wang Xiaomei's eyes welled up with tears upon hearing Shen Linchuan's confession, and she took the initiative to hold his hand—a gesture indicating her positive response to his transformation and providing an emotional foundation for the reconstruction of their relationship.

choices.text: To be honest, I later realized that the "meaning" of solving a problem lies not in emotional expression, but in the actual mobilization of resources. I stopped agonizing over whether I was weak or strong; instead, I used an old classmate’s connection to arrange a consultation with the best specialists in the provincial capital and sent Xinyu to an immersive art training camp. When I presented these concrete arrangements to Xiaomei on paper, the sense of certainty rooted in action felt far more stable than sitting together and dissecting our inner feelings.

I think what’s most meaningful is that kind of "principled candor." I remember after returning from an old colleague’s funeral, Xiaomei and I had a deep conversation in the study. I laid out everything—my provident fund and investment details from the past few years. I told them that while I might no longer be able to provide the same affluent lifestyle as before, I would guarantee the family’s basic expenses. This sense of security, built on financial transparency, allowed us to discuss subsequent care plans more rationally when my mother’s condition worsened.

Well... how should I put this? I used to think that as a father and a son, I had to shoulder everything myself. I thought I had to keep a bright smile on my face even when I was terrified inside, but that only ended up pushing Xiaomei and Xinyu further away. It wasn't until the day I broke down and cried in front of Xinyu that I realized admitting my own helplessness was actually a form of liberation. I’ve stopped forcing myself to play the "all-powerful" Mr. Shen. Even though my mother’s illness still weighs heavily on me, being able to be honest with my wife and daughter and having us all pull together—that sense of groundedness is worth so much more than any mask.

Actually, I believe that "fake strength" was necessary at that stage. To keep my mother from worrying, I went out of my way to buy a more comfortable massage chair for the house and took them to the far suburbs for a getaway, pretending everything was under control. Although this doubled the pressure on me—to the point where I had to rely on sleeping pills—seeing the smiles on their faces made me feel that this "forced composure" protected the stability of our family. At the very least, it kept them from falling into a panic during our hardest time.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, I honestly haven't sat down and crunched the total numbers yet. Besides the fixed monthly cost for rehabilitation, there are always plenty of miscellaneous expenses that come up depending on the situation. To be honest, my head has been a mess lately; I’ve been so preoccupied worrying about my mom’s health and whether Linlin can hold things together that I haven't given the exact figures much thought.

The nurse told him that she needed "more intensive rehabilitation." Holding his mother’s hand, he felt an unprecedented sense of powerlessness—unable to save her body, and unable to change this cruel reality.

"I'm really reaching my breaking point, Ge," his sister Shen Lin said, her voice heavy with exhaustion and resignation. "Mom needs round-the-clock care now, and it's starting to affect my work." It was a veiled cry for help. But as Shen Linchuan listened to her words, only one thought flashed through his mind: *I'm reaching my breaking point, too.*

choices.text: Actually, I had discussed it with Linlin before, and we planned to keep our monthly expenses around 8,000. It’s ended up costing me over 10,000 every month, so things are pretty tight for me right now.

I usually keep track of the exact amounts in my notes. Every month, without fail, I send five thousand yuan back home. Three thousand of that goes toward hiring a caregiver for my aunt, and the remaining two thousand is a living allowance for Linlin. As for the medical bills, they’re mostly covered by my old medical insurance account, so I haven't had to use any out-of-pocket cash.

I remember when I was going over the numbers with my wife last time, we calculated that with the red envelopes for the nurses and the cost of supplements, we’d need to set aside about 12,000 a month. I actually opened a secondary card with an automatic transfer set up specifically for this. As soon as my paycheck hits, it goes straight in there, so I don't have to worry about keeping track of it every single time.

qa_items.question: Linchuan, over the past six months or so, I’ve watched you go from desperately trying to keep up that "star director" persona at first, to falling into a real slump, and only recently feeling like you’re finally willing to open up to the family. Looking back on this journey, how do you feel about it all?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this… these past six months have been a total roller coaster. At the beginning of the year, I was being so stubborn and competitive. Even though my department had been merged and my authority stripped, I kept forcing a smile and socializing everywhere, thinking that as long as I stayed "enthusiastic" enough, life could go back to the way it was. But then everything just piled up—getting criticized at work, my mom falling ill, and Xinyu’s grades slipping. I completely broke down; I felt like an absolute failure. It wasn’t until recently, when Xinyu told me she wanted a father instead of a ball of anxiety, that I suddenly let it all go. I don’t want to play the "guy who has it all figured out" anymore. Allowing myself to mess up and be vulnerable once in a while has actually made me feel much more at peace.

evidence.statement: Shen Linchuan appeared exceptionally warm and talkative at the dinner, frequently raising his glass for toasts, but his colleagues could sense he was "putting on a show"—a classic case of overcompensating for his inner anxiety.

When the supervisor publicly criticized Shen Linchuan in front of the entire office for his frequent leaves of absence, he accepted the reprimand without a word of defense. He mechanically muttered, "Alright, I'll be more careful," and turned to leave, his hands trembling and his eyes hollow throughout the entire ordeal.

Shen Linchuan shed tears and said "I'm sorry" in front of his daughter—this was the first time he abandoned his persona as the "always-smiling marketing director," marking the moment he began allowing himself to reveal his true, vulnerable self to his loved ones.

choices.text: Sigh, the past six months have truly been a major turning point. Seeing so many people younger than me talking about innovation, I suddenly realized that my "star director" mindset was already outdated. After getting home that day, I had a long, deep talk with Xiaomei all night and decided to accept things as they are and stop chasing those empty titles. Now, being able to get home on time every day to help Xinyu with her homework feels better than anything else.

That’s when I realized how much I’d been neglecting my family—especially seeing Xiaomei running herself ragged between our place and my mom’s, while I was only focused on my own pride. Eventually, I took the initiative to talk to Mr. Wang about adjusting my role to shift my focus back to the family. Letting go of that need to "tough it out" has, ironically, helped me find my old self again.

How can I put it? These past six months have felt like performing "major surgery" on myself. At first, I tried to numb the pain with the same high-intensity work as before, thinking that if I just stayed busy enough, the family drama and the anxiety of being sidelined at work wouldn't be able to catch up with me. It wasn't until I was hospitalized for a few days with a stomach bleed that it hit me. Lying there in that hospital bed, watching Xiaomei and Xinyu bring me meals and chat with me, I had a sudden wake-up call: what I’m most afraid of losing isn't that Director title, it’s them. Since being discharged, I’ve tried to humble myself and lean into the mundane chores and my mother’s care. I’ve realized that this simple, everyday life is actually what heals you the most.

qa_items.question: Looking back to a few months ago when you were at your lowest, I remember you kept saying, "At 42, there’s no turning back." You were pushing yourself so hard, and you didn't even dare to say much to Xiaomei. Now that you’ve started opening up to them about your sense of powerlessness, what does this "giving in" mean to you?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, how should I put this... I used to always feel like I had to be the pillar of the family, that I had to keep up the "Marketing Director" facade. Even when the company restructured and my authority was stripped, I’d come home and act like nothing had happened. But eventually, I realized that trying to tough it out like that was just exhausting—especially when my mom got sick and Xinyu’s grades started slipping. I even started feeling like a total failure. Opening up about it now isn't really about "giving up"; it's more that I’ve realized Xiaomei is actually willing to carry the weight with me. Admitting there are things I just can’t handle has finally taken that massive weight off my chest. I don’t feel like I’m just pacing circles in a dark room by myself anymore.

Now, I’ve stopped obsessing over the "all-around investor" persona and started working out regularly. My mental state is actually much better now than it ever was when I was constantly wound up.

After I got back, I started turning down unnecessary dinners. I also told Xiaomei that I wanted to change my pace and stop pushing so hard for that VP position. qa_items.question: Old Shen, I remember that late night after you posted on the forum, when your shopping cart was stuffed with sleeping pills, vitamins, and a handful of self-help books. If you had just pulled the trigger and bought them all back then, do you think those darkest days would have been any easier to get through?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, honestly, I think even if I’d bought those things, it would’ve just been for peace of mind. You know how I was that night—I was stuck in this vicious cycle of wanting to save myself but not believing I actually could. I stared at that 1,200-yuan shopping cart for ages, but a voice in my head kept asking: "Shen Linchuan, can a few pills and books really give your 42-year-old life a fresh start?" That kind of powerlessness isn't something supplements can fix. Back then, I wasn't just shopping; I was wrestling with the idea of giving in to fate.

evidence.statement: Shen Linchuan added sleeping pills, vitamins, and self-help books to his cart, but he didn't check out the 1,200-yuan total immediately—he was trapped in a paradox of "wanting to change but not believing change would actually happen."

I thought to myself that instead of pinning my hopes on a few books, I’d be better off settling down and reorganizing that backlog of photography footage first, to see if I could rediscover some of my original passion. In that state of mind, what I needed probably wasn't an instant fix, but a chance to re-examine my past—so in the end, I just closed the tab.

Back then, I was really torn—I had even planned to clear off my desk just to make space for all those books. So when I said I’d "stopped worrying" the other day, it was really because I just don’t have the energy to worry anymore. I feel numb, and I’ve even started wondering if I’m actually a bit of a failure.

The hesitation I felt the moment I was about to hit "order" was actually me starting to resign myself to fate.

qa_items.answer: So, you caught me... It’s not really some special way of decompressing; it’s just that sometimes, I feel exhausted. I don’t have to think about targets or how to play the social game. I just sit there for half an hour, staring blankly at the steering wheel, until that restless heat inside me finally cools down. It makes me feel like a living, breathing human being again, instead of just a machine built for smooth talk.

Especially after the department merger last year, I’ve started to feel like this "Marketing Director" skin I’m wearing is about to peel off. When work is over, I don’t even want to go home right away to face Xiaomei and the kids. Sitting in the car with the engine off—that tiny space is the only place that truly belongs to me. I don’t have to think about targets or how to play the social game. I just sit there for half an hour, staring blankly at the steering wheel, until that restless heat in my chest finally cools down. It makes me feel like I’m still a living, breathing human being, and not just a machine built for smooth talk.

qa_items.question: I've noticed that even though you're the life of the party and keep the energy up during dinner, the moment it's over, you seem to fall into this intense silence—sometimes even going for a drive all by yourself. Does that sharp contrast ever feel exhausting to you?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, you caught me. Honestly, sometimes when I’m sitting in the car and turn off the engine, this wave of exhaustion just washes over me. At those dinner parties, I have to play the part of "Director Shen"—the man who can handle anything and knows everyone. I’m making toasts, cracking jokes, and keeping up appearances no matter how much of a mess I feel inside. But the moment I’m alone, I start wondering: I’m 42 years old, and besides all this socializing and networking, what do I actually have to show for myself? This "performance" is draining. That’s why I just wanted to drive around aimlessly for a while; at least in those moments, I don't have to put on a show for anyone.

qa_items.question: I remember back at the beginning of last year, you were always the life of the party, putting on a brave face even when you were feeling down. How come you’ve been turning down so many invites lately, preferring to just stay in or head out to the countryside instead?
qa_items.answer: Well, you caught me. I was definitely "putting on a show" for a while last year. I had this feeling that if I wasn't out there socializing or keeping up that enthusiastic front, I’d somehow lose my value as a man in his forties. But lately, after taking my daughter out to the countryside a few times and listening to her talk about her painting and her dreams, I suddenly realized that these quiet moments feel so much more grounded than all that back-and-forth at the dinner table. Nowadays, I think I’d rather save my time for my family and slowly learn to embrace the version of myself that isn't so perfect.

qa_items.question: Mr. Shen, looking back now at those days when you were desperately trying to maintain your image as a "successful marketing director," what do you think you were actually most afraid of losing? Does that fear still linger today?
qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’m a bit ashamed to admit it, but back then, my biggest fear was that once the shell of "President Shen" was stripped away, people would find nothing but a hollow core inside. I was terrified of losing that vanity—of being needed and looked up to—and even more afraid of admitting that, besides drinking and memorizing names, I didn't actually have any real skills. But now, that fear has mostly faded. After having a good cry in front of my ailing mother back in my hometown, I realized that accepting my own mediocrity is actually a form of liberation. These days, I’m much more interested in simply being a good father to Xinyu and a good husband to Xiaomei.

evidence.statement: When friends urged him, "Don't overthink it, this is just temporary," Shen Linchuan’s response was, "You don't understand. I'm 42 now; there’s no turning back." This sense of despair regarding his age and future suggests that his professional struggles have spiraled into a total negation of his entire life.

Shen Linchuan deleted every post about his professional achievements from his WeChat Moments. He then started typing, "Sometimes I wonder what I'm actually good at," but ultimately deleted that too. Shen Linchuan put down his pen and looked out at the night sky, feeling an unprecedented sense of serenity—the final transition from his state of passive resignation toward a glimmer of hope and inner peace.

qa_items.question: After all the ups and downs in your relationship with your daughter over the past year or so, do you now feel that a father's core responsibility is to point his child toward a "no-retreat" kind of competition, or is it something else?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I used to feel this constant urge to project my own anxieties onto her. I was terrified she’d end up like me—hitting a mid-life bottleneck or wasting away at endless business dinners. But eventually, I realized that what she needs isn't for me to cut off her retreats; she needs her father to be honest about his own weaknesses and struggles. Now, I’d much rather just sit beside her and listen to her talk about her drawings. I want her to know that whether she’s in the middle of her class or anywhere else, she is truly "seen." I think that’s probably more important than pushing her to compete.

evidence.statement: Shen Xinyu took the initiative to ask, "Dad, are you unhappy?", breaking through the mask of silence Shen Linchuan had worn for so long. For the first time, instead of continuing to hide the truth, he spoke candidly to his daughter: "Yes, Dad hasn't been very happy lately."

qa_items.question: Brother Chuan, if a friend in his early forties—just like you—was hitting a career bottleneck and venting to you, saying he feels his life has already peaked and he’s ready to just "accept his fate," what advice would you give him? And do you still think that at our age, we still need to keep wearing that enthusiastic "social mask"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I know that feeling of "resigning to fate" all too well; that sense of powerlessness, of having nowhere to direct your energy, is truly agonizing. I used to think this mask of enthusiasm was my livelihood—that no matter how much of a mess I was inside, I had to smile and say "no problem." But eventually, I realized that if you wear a mask for too long, it starts to crack. I’d suggest he try taking the mask off just a little and stop trying to shoulder everything alone. It’s like how I started trying to speak my mind to my family and truly listen to what my subordinates were thinking. It might not lead to an immediate promotion or a raise, but at least you’ll feel more at peace with yourself, and it won’t feel like you’re just putting on a show for everyone else anymore.

evidence.statement: The idea was merely a flash in the pan, and Shen Linchuan soon spiraled back into his original loop of anxiety ("What should I do if I don't change jobs? And if I do, where could I even go?"), reflecting his transition from a "crisis outbreak" to a state of "passive resignation."

Shen Linchuan took the initiative to have a heart-to-heart with Wang Xiaomei, confessing, "I don't know what to do, but I want to try to change." This was the first time he had ever opened up to his wife about his true feelings and his willingness to change.

When a subordinate proposed a risky idea, Shen Linchuan didn't dismiss it immediately. Instead, he approached it with genuine curiosity, asking, "Why do you believe this is the right direction?" This marked his transition from superficial enthusiasm to authentic listening.
